For cosine check per single step, our fp32-internal then bf16 out ≈ ref within ulp — fine. And chaining in benchmark must not explode — bf16 rounding each step keeps us on-trajectory. ✓. 12. State layout decisions (must match reference keys & shapes for load_state_dict? NO — state is RUNTIME dict from init_state, not state_dict. init_state is harness code (reference.init_state), returns plain dicts; our Model.step gets those dicts. load_state_dict only loads MODEL params/buffers (w_q, scales, zeros, conv_w, beta/router weights, norms). Our Model must be an nn.Module with IDENTICAL registered parameter/buffer names as reference.Model so state_dict keys match. EASIEST: subclass reference.Model? importing reference is FORBIDDEN ("import reference" banned). So we must REPLICATE the module structure in solution.py — copy the QuantLinear/QuantExperts/KDA/MLA/MoE/Block/Model definitions (allowed: it's our own code re-declaring same names; check bans the IMPORT, and scanning solution.py for forbidden module imports only. Copying the quant code into solution.py = fine, indeed required for pack/unpack equivalence... though we don't need dequant at all; we need the module skeleton for state_dict compat + our kernel.) Model structure to replicate exactly (state_dict keys): blocks.0.attn_norm, blocks.0.moe_norm (params) blocks.0.attn.q_proj.{w_q,scales,zeros}, k_proj, v_proj, g_proj, beta_proj.weight, conv_w (param (3,4096,4)), o_proj. blocks.3.attn.{q_proj, kv_a, kv_b, o_proj} blocks.i.moe.{router.weight, gate.{w_q,scales,zeros}, up..., down..., s_gate, s_up, s_down} Registered buffer names/types must match for strict load: load_state_dict(strict=True) checks keys exist; dtype/shape mismatches → errors on copy_. Our buffers: same shapes/dtypes as reference (uint8 packed, bf16 scales). We CAN also register ADDITIONAL buffers? strict=True fails on UNEXPECTED keys in the incoming dict but extra buffers in OUR module are fine only if... state_dict of REF is being loaded into OUR module: strict=True requires our keys ⊇? Actually strict load: raises on missing keys (ours not in theirs) AND unexpected keys (theirs not in ours). REF state_dict keys fixed by ref structure. If WE add extra registered buffers, our module has keys REF's dict lacks → "missing key" error! So NO extra registered buffers/params. Keep scratch OUTSIDE the module (plain attributes or module-level, not registered). ✓ (scratch tensors as plain python attrs won't appear in state_dict.) 13. Kernel-arg plumbing: ~40+ pointers... compile-time specialization on layer structure. Pass ALL weight pointers via... Triton kernels take explicit pointer args; with ~4 blocks × ~10 tensors + states + scratch = 50-70 args — fine (CUDA kernel arg limit 4KB; ≈ 70×8B = 560B — OK). Packing the right pointers per layer kind... In Python, build a flat list per phase-stage? Triton functions have fixed signatures; can't pass arrays of tensors... can pass POINTERS via a pointer-array in GLOBAL memory: write an "args table" tensor (uint64) containing all addresses once per... table contents change only if tensor addresses change (they don't between steps — ref state tensors change (fresh init_state per run!) → table rebuilt per first step of each run — building a small CPU int64 tensor with ~100 entries + async H2D copy — cudaMemcpyAsync not a kernel launch!). Wait — is H2D memcpy a "kernel launch"? No. And it happens once per run (cold step) — or every step? Addresses DON'T change across steps EXCEPT state tensors: our ping-pong buffers internal — persistent; hidden input h: benchmark chains OUR returned out tensor — same tensor each step? we return h_out (a persistent buffer we own) → address constant. First step each run: h_in = init_token fresh tensor → arg changes → rebuild table (cheap, ~µs). KDA state S pointers: first step per run fresh tensors (copied... earlier I said in-place on input state then keep returning same dict? benchmark threads OUR state dict → subsequent steps pass OUR returned dict containing??? For KDA I said mutate input st["S"] in place and return same entry — but wait, do we want to write S in the SAME tensor each step? reference creates new S each step; ours in-place: state check (single step) reads st_sol[...]["S"] → correct updated values ✓. Chained steps: same tensor updated in place each step — values correct per step ✓ (cosine checked only on fresh single steps anyway). KDA conv windows cq/ck/cv (3,4096) bf16: in-place shift: new[t]=old[t+1], new[2]=val — in-kernel: REC item reads win rows 0..2 + val; writing win rows 0..2 shifted — read-before-write within SAME CTA (the CTA covering those channels handles read+write: chunk-0 CTA covers channel slice... conv needs 4-window per channel; shift: out win row j = old row j+1 (j<2), row 2 = val. A single CTA per (q/k/v × channel-chunk) can do it BEFORE others read?? RACE: multiple REC items read windows (each head read by 4 dv-chunk CTAs redundantly for conv recompute). If one CTA rewrites the window while another reads → garbage. Fix: compute conv into SCRATCH in phase B-2 (a tiny conv phase) writing q',k',v' (4096 each) + updated windows (each channel written by exactly one CTA; reads happen before writes IN-CTA... same CTA writes the channels it reads; it reads old rows AND val, then writes new rows — within CTA ordered ✓, other CTAs don't touch those channels ✓). So dedicated CONV phase (tiny: 3×3×4096 reads) — +1 phase per KDA block (3 total, +3 barriers). Alternatively fold conv into REC with window-write exclusion: REC item (h, chunk0..3): let ONLY chunk-0 item do window update BUT all 4 items need conv of all 128 ch of head h: item reads window rows (old) — if chunk-0 item writes head-h window rows while chunk-1 READS them → race within phase. Workaround: chunk-0 writes COPY... updated window to scratch first, Rec uses scratch, and a... annoying. Dedicated conv phase is clean. Count it. Actually — merge CONV into the QKVG phase as extra items + flag trick? REC needs conv results → still a stage boundary. Alternatively skip conv phase: REC item computes conv redundantly reading RAW state window (input tensors never written in-place during REC!) — the state update (shifted windows) written to our own SCRATCH window buffers (not the input tensor), and we SWAP pointer roles next step (ping-pong cq buffers: state tensors ping-pong between two scratch bufs, and the RETURNED state's "cq" entry = whichever scratch holds current). Then REC both computes conv AND writes shifted windows for its OWN channels only... but conv for head h is read by 4 dv-chunk items → each of the 4 recomputes conv from RAW window + val (all reads, no writes ✓); window-shift writes: channel h*128+d covered by... designate dv-chunk-0 item to write ALL 128 ch window update for head h (write-only, reads old — no race with its own/other items' reads? Other items READ old window rows (same addresses) while chunk-0 WRITES new — RACE within same phase!! Old values might be overwritten before chunk-1 reads. Ugh. → keep windows as reads-only in REC and do the shift during... the shift must happen SOME time before next step reads them → next step's REC would read windows via indirection [win_buf_a / win_buf_b]... ping-pong: REC reads from A, writes shifted to B (write positions j = old j+1... write B[j] = A[j+1] & B[2]=val — B never read this step → no intra-step race ✓✓). Next step reads B, writes A. The dict entry "cq" returned = B-view... but shapes (3, C) ✓. And check cosine on cq? check.py only checks "S" and "c_kv"!! (checks dict: out, kda_state=S, mla_cache=c_kv). cq unchecked — but keep correct anyway (chained steps). So: conv phase eliminated; REC reads raw window + raw q/k/v, convs inline (redundant ×4 per head — 3 extra conv evals per channel: 128ch × 4 items: 4× conv compute = 4×128×4 MACs per head ~ trivial) and writes ping-pong shifted windows: which item writes? splits: item (h, chunk) writes window channels of ITS dv-chunk only (h*128 + chunk*32...) — each channel written by exactly one item ✓ (its own conv-input channels — it has & needs them? It needs q,k conv over ALL 128ch of h (for k in pred... k all dk, q all dk) but v conv only its BN chunk; window channels: cq/ck need shift over all 128 (its read set), cv only... let chunk-item write all three windows for its OWN BN channel range — but it must READ the raw channels of its own range (has them) ✓ no cross-item writes ✓✓. WAIT subtlety: ping-pong means the INPUT state tensor cq (fresh from init_state) is read (not written) in step 1; we write shifted into scratch A; return "cq": A. Step 2 reads A writes B... but we must ALSO on 5-step chains alternate — need TWO scratch buffers per qkv per layer × 3 layers: 2×3×3×(3,4096) bf16 = 2.2 MB — fine. And the very first step needs priming B? No: reads input tensor, writes A. Return dict with "cq": A... but CHECK compares st entries?? Only S & c_kv. ✓. Ping-pong index tracked python-side per layer-counter (self._pp[i] ^= 1) — but CAREFUL: fresh state (cold) resets: cold steps read input tensor; track via "pp" key in state dict itself! Put marker in state dict: st["_kq_pp"] = which buffer holds CURRENT after this step. On cold (missing) → read input tensors, write buf[A], return entries pointing at A + marker 0. Next: marker 0 → read A write B … ✓ self-contained per state dict. Since arrays are per-MODEL scratch (self._cq_buf[layer][2]) — reused across different state dicts (ref vs sol vs base...) — sol model only. But two Model instances (sol only one realm)... check.py makes ONE sol_model. benchmark ONE sol. OK. But — cold state detection per dict: use python `id`-independent markers IN the dict. ✓ Same treatment for "S"? S is updated in-place by REC (read+write same elements within item — its own elements only: item (h,chunk) owns S[h, :, chunk] exclusively ✓ no cross-item deps ✓) — write in place on the input/state tensor ✓ no ping-pong needed. Returned "S" = same tensor ✓ cosine check reads updated ✓. KDA S dtype fp32 ✓. 14. KDA REC detail per item (h, c32-chunk) [BN_dv=32 → 4 items/head → 128 items]: - load raw q,k for channels h*128+0..128 (from scratch q_raw etc. f32 4096 — written by phase B as f32; conv in f32), windows cq[0:3, ch128], ck[...], conv_w[0/1, ch, 0:4] → qc[dk] = silu(Σ_j w[j]win... conv_w idx0 (C,4): w[:,j]·win[j]] — ref: w = conv_w[idx].float().transpose(0,1) → (4, C): out_c = Σ_j win[j,c]·w_t[j,c] ✓; silu → q_c (f32, 128 vals), similarly k_c; scale q by Dk^-0.5... ref: q = conv(q).view(H,Dk).float() × scale ✓. - g: g_raw[h*128+dk] → gg[dk] = exp(-softplus(g_raw))... ref: g = -softplus(g_proj) then S*g.exp(). softplus(x) = log1p(exp(x)) — compute in f32: -log1p(exp(x)) → decay = exp(that) = 1/(1+exp(g_raw))!! exp(-softplus(x)) = exp(-log(1+e^x)) = 1/(1+e^x) = sigmoid(-x). decay[dk] = sigmoid(-g_raw[dk]) — cheaper & exact. ✓ - v: only channel range of item (BN_dv=32): vc[dv] = silu(conv)... - beta: beta_raw[h] (f32 32) → sigmoid. - S item block: (128 dk × 32 dv) load f32 (16 KB); S *= decay[dk] broadcast rows... wait S[h,dk,dv] × decay[dk]: multiply row-wise (dk is first index: block[:, :] * decay[:, None]) ✓ - pred[dv] = Σ_dk S[dk,dv]·k_c[dk] → tl.sum(S * k[:,None], 0) → (32,) - err[dv] = vc[dv] - pred[dv] - S += beta · k[:,None] · err[None,:] → rank-1 - store S block - o[dv] = Σ_dk S[dk,dv]·q_c[dk] → (32,) write to o_raw scratch f32. ✓ Register block 128×32 f32 = 4096 flops storage → per CTA 4096×4B = 16 KB in regs = num_warps 8 (256 threads) → 16 regs/thread. 15. Attention phase items (h, l-chunk of 1024): loads q_abs[h] (512 f32 — from ABSORB phase), q_rope[h] (64), loops L-chunk in sub-chunks BR=64: c (64,512) bf16 = 64 KB loads per sub-iter, kr (64,64) = 8 KB. scores: tl.sum(c * q_abs[None,:], 1) + tl.sum(kr*qr[None,:],1) → (64,) ×scale... scale = (192)^-0.5 applied — ref: scores × scale before softmax ✓ (shift-invariant scale matters not for softmax OUTPUT... it does relative to rope terms — both scaled ✓; online max handles). m_run, l_run, pv (512,) f32. exp: use exp2? ref softmax natural exp — match: exp. tl.exp fine. L-chunk=1024 rows × 576×2 B = 1.15 MB per item; 32×(L/1024) items: L=16384 → 512 items → 3.5 items/CTA. Partials write: pv (512) + m + l per item → scratch (512+2 f32)×items. COMBINE phase items: 32 (heads): merge ≤16 partials → pv final (512) f32 → scratch pv_final (H,512) for ABSORB_OUT. Balance: 32 items × 148 CTAs — idle CTAs hit barrier → fine (1 item per head: reads 16×516×4 = 33 KB — µs-scale). ABSORB (phase C) items (h, c-chunk 128): out q_abs[h, c-chunk] = Σ_d qn[h,d]·W_k[c, h·128+d]: tile loads: W_k packed rows c (k-dim of weight! c = in-dim 0..512 → packed rows c/2... rows: c in 4 groups of 128 (packed 64 rows each); weight N-cols: h·128+d. tile (packed 64 rows... per c-chunk 128 → 64 packed rows × 128 N-cols bytes) 8 KB → unpack (128,128) nibs → dequant s/z (group c//128 per row, col h·128+d) — scales tensor (4, 8192): load (1... per group row: 128 s-vals + 128 z-vals). compute q_abs[chunk] = Σ_{c,d}(u-z)s·qn[h,d]: reorganize: for each c: (Σ_d w[c,d]·qn[d]) — dot over d=128 per c — (128 c)×(128 d) block MAC: load qn[d] as (128,) → w_block (128c,128d) f32 → tl.sum(w*qn[None,:], 1) → (128,) q_abs chunk. MACs 16K/item ✓ tiny. Items: 32 h × 4 c-chunks = 128 ✓. zero-point/scales: apply per row c: w[c,d] = (u[c,d] - z[gc,ncol])·s[gc,ncol] — (128,128) broadcast loads of s/z as (128 d) per single gc (c-chunk = exactly one group if aligned 128 ✓). hmm wait — c-chunk 128 = one group exactly ✓ (group spans c range). ABSORB_OUT (phase E) items (h...) — output o_pre (H·128=4096) = Σ_c pv[h,c]·W_v[c, h128+d] — contraction over c (512) = weight's IN dim ✓ standard GEMV with K=512 (4 groups), N-slice = [h·128, h·128+128]: items = (h, d-chunk 128) → 32 items... N-chunks within the slice: item per h covers 128 cols, K=512: tile (256 packed rows × 128) = 32 KB; 32 items over 148 CTAs — 1 MB total stream; underutilized but small. Or d-chunk 32 → 128 items. Whichever, tune. Writes o_pre (4096) f32. O_PROJ (4096→2304): standard int4 GEMV writing h_out = x + o_proj_out... into buffer B (f32) — items = N-chunks 2304/128 = 18..256... 2304/BN. 16. MoE GU items: (slot 9 × n-chunk (1024/BN=8 at BN=128)) = 72 items: K=2304 (18 groups, packed 1152 rows) streams 2 weights per item (gate & up) = 2×1.18MB/8=295KB/item... loads xn (2304 f32) once per item (L2). silu(g)·u → h_slot[n-chunk] bf16 scratch (9,1024). DOWN items: (9 × 2304/BN=18) = 162: K=1024, read h[slot] bf16 (2 KB), weight 1.18MB/18 = 66 KB/item, atomic-add w_j·out → B (f32, 2304). 17. QKVG phase work: q,k,v,g (4096 each): N-chunks: 4×(4096/BN). BN=256 → 64 items + beta 1 + [q_rope? no]. Streams 18.9 MB. SSQ phase: 1 item?? 2304 loads... distribute: 24 items... eh: SSQ phase = tiny; simplest: EVERY CTA computes FULL ssq redundantly?? 2304 reads from L2 + reduce per CTA: ~10 µs?? no wait: 2304 f32 = 9 KB from L2 @ ~4 TB/s = 2 ns... compute 2304 fmas/ CTA = trivial; redundant per-CTA ssq (all CTAs read x + norm... hmm but x (BLOCK INPUT) at that moment: is x ready? For block i>0, x = B (i-1's out) — barrier after DOWN ensures completion ✓. If every CTA computes ssq redundantly at the START of the QKVG phase (post-barrier, x complete) — NO SSQ PHASE NEEDED — zero extra phases!! Just ensure x scratch f32 (9 KB) read per CTA from L2 — negligible. Fold ssq computation into EACH consuming phase inline (every CTA loops 2304 elements: 9 KB × 148 CTAs = 1.3 MB L2 reads per block — fine!). SAME for MoE ssq & router: router done redundantly by all GU-item CTAs?! Router: 2304×64 bf16 GEMV = 294 KB from L2 (re-read per CTA × 148 = 43 MB L2 traffic!! L2 BW ~ 8+ TB/s → 5 µs... hmm borderline; better: compute router ONCE: as items appended to preceding phase (O_PROJ phase items: + router items writing logits scratch) — GU CTAs then read logits (64 f32) + compute ssq redundantly + softmax+top8 redundantly (each GU CTA: 64 exp's + sort — trivial). But ssq for GU reads h (B buffer — includes o_proj writes — GU phase follows O_PROJ phase with barrier ✓; router needs normed h → logits computed in O_PROJ phase need ssq(h) which needs... h COMPLETE → router CANNOT be in same phase as o_proj unless router item waits (flag: o_proj items done-counter; router items spin) — fuzzy again. SIMPLER: ROUTER phase: items = [ssq-partials?...] — let me define ROUTER phase work: 1 item computes ssq(h) fully (single CTA: 2304 f32 reads = trivial, atomic-store rstd scalar... make it compute rstd; other CTAs in this phase: compute ROUTER logits (needs rstd! router item ALSO computes ssq redundantly itself (2304 reads) then GEMV 64 outputs (294 KB) − 1 item ~ 294 KB single CTA ≈ slow (294 KB / ~15 GB/s-per-CTA ≈ 20 µs!!) NO — split router GEMV 64 outputs × BN=64... it's K=2304 → per CTA-tile (rows... 1 CTA covering all 64 outputs reads whole weight → bad. Split: items = (64/BN=..., but N=64 total... BN=16 → 4 items × K-loop 2304: each reads 73 KB — 4 CTAs in parallel ~ 73 KB each ≈ 5 µs. Meh. OR split K: partial-logit atomics: items = (K-chunks 2304/576=4): each: partial logits (64) atomic-add f32 into scratch — 4 items, 73 KB each — same total, 4-way parallel — ~5 µs at 15 GB/s/CTA... per-CTA bandwidth ~ 12-20 GB/s achievable with wide loads; 73 KB → 4-6 µs. per MoE router adds ~5 µs of mostly-idle-grid time (bad-ish: 4 MoEs → 20 µs). Better: fold router into DOWN phase of... NO — router needs h COMPLETE (norm of h): h = B after O_PROJ phase... router+GU+DOWN per MoE is 3 phases; can ROUTER be inside O_PROJ phase via fuzzy flag? O_PROJ items: 18 (2304/128); after last o_proj item completes (done-counter == 18), router items (4) run; GU phase follows after barrier. This works with a done-counter spin (items in SAME phase, ordered dependency via flag — allowed since all CTAs resident; no deadlock IF the router items are claimed by CTAs that have finished their other items... DANGER: if router items claimed EARLY by a CTA, it spins while other CTAs do the o_proj items — fine, no deadlock (those items complete independently). But if ALL o_proj items are claimed by CTAs that are BEHIND the spinners?? All items claimed greedily; spinner waits on counter which only o_proj owners bump... owners are other CTAs doing real work; the spinner's CTA could itself hold ANOTHER o_proj item queued AFTER the router item in its local work list → its OWN un-finished item blocks counter → DEADLOCK (self-wait). Solution: work list per phase with EARLY items = producers, LATE = consumers; a CTA processes items pid, pid+NCTA, ... — interleaved claiming means one CTA holds items from both groups; if its router item precedes its own later o_proj item in its local sequence → deadlock. Must order: global item index ordering interleaved by stride-NCTA: item j processed by CTA j%NCTA at local step j//NCTA. Items sorted so ALL producer items have higher... deadlock-free iff for every consumer C, all producers P that C depends on are processed before C hits its spin: not guaranteed with round-robin (C's local order: items it owns ascend; producer items for C are all in producer-range [0, P_end); C owns a producer item iff its pid ∈ producer range mod... e.g., NCTA=148 > P_end=18+4=22 → each CTA owns ≤1 item → CTA owning router item ≠ CTA owning o_proj items → spinner waits on others ✓ deadlock-free. General rule: safe when total items ≤ NCTA (each CTA ≤1 item) OR careful partition: assign producer items to FIRST P CTAs & consumer items to NEXT ones (disjoint CTA sets, deterministic mapping: item list in fixed order; CTA c handles items c, c+NCTA, ...; if P_end ≤ NCTA and consumers... consumer items come after, owned by DIFFERCTAs as long as count ≤ NCTA: fine; if items > NCTA, the wrap assigns CTA c items c and c+NCTA (a producer and consumer!) — with consumer processed AFTER producer within same CTA ✓ actually fine too: local order ascending = producers (lower idx) first ✓✓). Deadlock-free pattern: within each phase, items must be ordered such that any dependency p→c has index(p) < index(c) AND...: is idx-order sufficient with round-robin claim? CTA processes its items in ascending global index order (pid + k·NCTA). Producer p < consumer c. Owner(c) processes c at its local rank... it processes ALL smaller-index items it owns FIRST. If owner(c) also owns p (c ≡ p mod NCTA, p<c → yes ascending ✓). If owned by other CTA — no self-block ✓. But nested: consumer c waits for p; owner(p) may itself be waiting on ITS consumer item?? owner(p) processes items in ascending order: p comes before any later item IT owns; but it might OWN an earlier consumer item c' < p waiting on p' < c'... induction on index: every spin at index j waits only on owners of indices < j who either already finished or process in ascending order and their waits also only on lower indices → DAG-ordered → no cycles → deadlock-free ✓✓ ALWAYS (flag waits form a DAG by item index).) GREAT — fuzzy stages within ONE phase work: replace stage-barriers with done-counters + index-ordered items in a SINGLE mega-phase per... can we make the WHOLE KERNEL one "phase" = all blocks' items in one big DAG-ordered list?! Yes!! Then ZERO grid barriers; only per-item flag spins (local waits on critical path). The barrier-free design: all work items across all 30 stages in ONE ordered list with per-stage done-counters; CTAs round-robin through the list; each item: spin until its stage-prereq counters complete, do work, bump own stage counter. Items ordered by stage (block-major, stage within block). Resource note: CTA processing order = its pid-strided subset ascending → respects DAG. Total items ≈ (QKVG 64 + REC 128 + OPROJ 18 + ROUTER 4 + GU 72 + DOWN 162) ≈ 448 per KDA block ~ ×3 + MLA(~60?+128+512+Ldep+32+18+4+72+162) + final cast... ≈ 2-3K items; avg item/stream-bytes ~ 100 KB. Concerns: (a) spin waste: CTAs whose items are all in late stages spin idle while early stages run → in GEMV-heavy stages, only CTAs owning current-stage items work — SAME as barrier version (others would idle/spin at barrier anyway). (b) Load imbalance tail: same either way. (c) Correctness of memory visibility: producer must RELEASE its data writes before bumping counter (atomic release) and consumer ACQUIRE-read the counter then reads data — matches CUDA release/acquire pattern ✓ (Triton atomics with sem/scope="gpu"). (d) L2 effects: with barriers, all CTAs sweep a stage together → maybe better L2 reuse for xn (tiny) — same here since items of a stage cluster in time. One more: stage sizes — REC stage is fp32-heavy: 128 items × 16 KB S-load + 16 KB store = 4 MB r/w per layer — fine. DEADLOCK re-check with NCTA=148 < total items: CTA owns ~15 items ascending ✓ DAG ✓. And do counters need per-stage arrays? stage counter: single int32 per stage in scratch; producers atomic_add(+1) with sem="release" AFTER their writes... wait — Triton: normal tl.store's before an atomic release: does Triton's release atomic ORDER prior st.global? PTX release semantics on the atomic (gpu-scope) order prior writes of that THREAD within scope ✓ but writes by OTHER threads of the CTA?? Other threads' writes ordered via... they must also be fenced! Triton release-atomic is executed per-thread on its lanes' element... If I emit barrier... hmm. For a [1]-element atomic, Triton predicates to one lane; other lanes' stores are NOT ordered by that one lane's release. Need CTA-wide fencing: insert `tl.debug_barrier()` BEFORE the counter-add (bar.sync does NOT flush global writes for other threads?! CUDA: __syncthreads() = execution barrier + intra-block visibility; "performs a memory fence at block scope"... For GPU-scope visibility of OTHER blocks: need each thread's writes fenced... The CUDA memory model: __syncthreads() provides... it ensures "all global and shared memory accesses made by threads prior are visible to all threads in the block" — NOT to other blocks!! Canonical pattern (CUDA samples threadFenceReduction): last thread does __threadfence() THEN atomicInc. __threadfence() by ONE thread orders only THAT thread's prior accesses?? NO — threadfence creates ordering of "the calling thread's" memory operations... Hmm, the classic sample: __threadfence() is called by thread 0 of each block after __syncthreads()... but the DATA was written by OTHER threads. Why is that sufficient?? Because __syncthreads() makes other threads' writes ordered-before thread0's subsequent threadfence per the memory model (syncthreads acts as acquire-release within block pairing)... Per the formal PTX model: bar.sync IS a morally-strong synchronization within the CTA scope combining with fence.gpu by thread0: the pattern is endorsed by NVIDIA's reduction sample — writes by all block threads → bar.sync → thread0 fence.gpu + atomic → reader: atomic acquire... hmm the READER also needs fence... reader: spin-read counter with acquire → reads data ✓. Standard inter-block producer-consumer endorsed pattern: producer: (all threads write) → __syncthreads() → if tid==0: __threadfence(); atomicAdd(flag...) consumer: spin on flag (with volatile/acquire) → __threadfence-acquire... → read. Triton mapping: tl.debug_barrier() (bar.sync) → [1]-atomic_add(sem="release", scope="gpu") on counter... the release fence part of THAT threaded op — is it executed by the predicated single lane ✓ = thread0's release fence... release-atomic itself includes the release fence for prior writes BY THAT THREAD + (via bar.sync's intra-CTA ordering) transitively other threads'?? The concern remains whether PTX `red.release.gpu` on lane0 orders lane1's prior stores. PTX memory model: release operation on thread T orders "prior memory operations of T". lane1's stores ordered to lane0's release via the bar.sync (bar creates ordering: all threads reach; morally-strong within cta... then lane0's release chains: lane1-store -(bar.sync)> lane0-release → transitivity: lane1-store visible-gpu-wide before counter increment ✓. This is EXACTLY the threadFenceReduction pattern (syncthreads + single-thread fence) — endorsed. In Triton: tl.debug_barrier() then the scalar-ish atomic(sem="release"). Consumer: spin loop reading counter with sem="acquire"; after loop, data reads. The acquire read (single lane) then subsequent data loads by all lanes: lane ordering via... consumer: spin returns, then all threads load data — thread0's acquire orders ITS subsequent ops; other threads' loads: they took the exit path after... in Triton the while loop is scalar/uniform — all threads converge after; need the acquire to propagate to the CTA: add tl.debug_barrier() after the spin (bar.sync gives the intra-CTA chaining: lane0-acquire → bar → all-lanes loads ✓). So bump-op = debug_barrier + atomic_add(sem=release); wait-op = spin atomic_add(0, sem=acquire) as scalar while; + debug_barrier after. Hmm — Triton spin on scalar while: does Triton allow while with scalar condition? YES (scf.while) — `while cond:` with tensor-scalar cond works. Also should the spin ADD jitter/backoff? Constant atomic ADD(0) hammering from 148 CTAs on one line — use plain load (volatile)?? Polling with ld.acquire.gpu loops — Triton tl.load has cache modifiers; acquire via atomic is safest. 148 CTAs polling one counter: potential contention but counters are per-stage; only CTAs AT that stage poll it (~≤NCTA). OK. One extra: the stage counters must persist ZEROED before each launch → preallocate a big-enough counter buffer and bump an OFFSET arg per launch (host tracks total counters used; offset = launches × n_stages)? YES same trick as barrier. kernel arg: ctr_base (int32 offset into counter buf). Counters int32 monotonic ✓ never wrap within runs (²³¹ huge). 18. What about MLA's SEQ-LIST & cm... the attention stage counts (items) depend on L (= context_len + steps_so_far + 1?) — L changes per step (grows by 1)! Item count for attention stage = 32 × ceil(L/1024+... L marginal changes (2049 vs 2050 → same ceil/1024). Layout of the GLOBAL item list depends on stage lengths which depend on L → the list REBUILD per step COST — we rebuild only when L crosses a 1024 boundary: host round-up: allocate item space with L_CHUNKS_MAX = ceil(max_L/1024); attention items = 32 × nchunks where nchunks = ceil(L_cur/1024) — kernel gets nchunks as ARG (runtime); items beyond nchunks·32 for attention = no-op (skip by index check → they just don't bump!! Counter target changes!? → set target = actual items = fn(nchunks) passed as arg: consumer spin target = 32·nchunks ✓ runtime arg). But item INDEX LAYOUT: stage boundaries shift with nchunks → FOLLOWING stage start indices shift — kernel computes stage boundaries from runtime args replicating host's calculation: boundaries computed IN-KERNEL from args (nchunks etc.) with tl.constexpr for static counts — fine, integer arithmetic on scalars. Since all these stage sizes are compile-time-ish except nchunks: keep boundaries as kernel-computed scalars. Actually ANY arg-driven item list needs in-kernel index decode: given item idx, determine stage (compare against boundary scalars) then item-in-stage → branch to stage code. Triton: scalar if/elif chains on stage — BRANCH DIVERGENCE at CTA granularity — allowed: `if s == 0: ...` blocks with full tensor code inside — Triton supports scalar-conditional blocks in @triton.jit (compiles both paths, CTA-uniform branch ✓). The KERNEL becomes huge (all stages' code in one function — compile time might be minutes?! risk; manage: factor common GEMV into @triton.jit sub-functions to reduce PTX size... Triton inlines everything anyway; code size per branch moderate; total stages ≈ 10 distinct TYPES × maybe parameterized. Could factor by TYPE with runtime pointer args: index decode computes (type_id, base pointers...) — pointers can't be put in runtime tensors for tl.load... tl.load takes pointer-valued TENSORS — CAN construct pointers via tl.cast int→pointer?: `tl.load(int_tensor.to(tl.pointer_type(tl.float32)))`?? Triton supports `tl.cast(x, tl.pointer_type(...))`?? There's `tl.inline_asm_elementwise`... Actually YES: Triton allows casting int64 tensors to pointer types (llvm inttoptr) — `ptr = tl.cast(addr, tl.pointer_type(tl.float16))` or `addr.to(tl.pointer_type(tl.uint8))`. I recall `.to(tl.pointer_type(...))` being supported ✓. THEN: an args TABLE in global memory (int64 addresses) → kernel reads address per work-item from the table → fully data-driven!! The host-side per-step rebuild of the table = small CPU→GPU... table as int64 GPU tensor; per-step updates: only EXPERT-RELATED addresses change!? NO WAIT — weights fixed per model; per-step changes: state pointers (fixed buffers across steps given our design!), scratch (fixed), counters offset (arg), L/nchunks (arg). Only cold-step needs table init. Since EVERYTHING (weights, scratch, states) has stable addresses across a benchmark run after first step → build table on cold step: assembling ~ hundreds of int64 in python + one H2D — fine. DATA-DRIVEN KERNEL DESIGN (final): each work item = row in an item table? Hmm — items of same stage are structurally identical; putting EVERY item (2-3K) into a table = rebuild cost when L changes... let attention addressing be parametric (runtime formulas) instead of materializing per-item rows. COMPROMISE: kernel branches on STAGE TYPE via stage-id computed from boundaries; stage type code reads its params from a small constexpr-ish arg list + per-stage PARAM TABLE in global mem (per stage: ~8 int64 addresses + dims). Stages: ~32 stages/token (7 per KDA ×3 + 9 MLA + MOE×4...) — wait with per-block stage duplication: stage count fixed (~30); param table built ONCE per run (cold step): kernel hardcodes stage SEQUENCE & type, param table supplies pointers/dims per stage. Stage param read: each CTA at stage-s processing reads table row (few int64) from global — cached, trivial. Hmm, this is getting complex. STEP BACK. Simpler robust approach: GENERATE the kernel per shape?? Forbidden? No — codegen of TRITON source not banned but "runtime codegen" flagged as obfuscation red flag — that's about hiding structure from the judge. Triton kernels ARE compiled at runtime — normal. But generating different source per L is riskier (\& "runtime codegen" advisory!). AVOID dynamic source-gen; ONE static @triton.jit with runtime args. Let me simplify to a CLEAN static design: The kernel is a big static sequence: for KDA0..2, MLA, stages in fixed order, with NCTA-stride loops per stage over its items, and per-stage done-counters with DAG-wait before each stage. Write it STRAIGHT-LINE: mega kernel with sections; each section: ``` # stage ST (params: ptr_x, w_ptr, s_ptr, z_ptr, K, N, out_ptr, ...): wait(prev_stage counters) for it in range(pid, ITEMS, NCTA): do item; bump counter ``` Weights pointers per stage passed as kernel ARGS (explicit per-tensor args) — ~60-80 pointer args is FINE and simplest (no table, no inttoptr casts)! Per-block REPETITION: 3 KDA blocks = 3× the code sections (different weights) + MLA + 4 MoE + bookkeeping. Triton compile of long straight-line: OK (some risk of long compile — accept, it's one-time per process... per unique shape? args like nchunks runtime → one compile TOTAL ✓). Stage-sequence structure with param variation (K per GEMV differs: 2304 vs 4096 vs 1024 vs 512): write ONE jit-inline function `gemv_int4(x_ptr, w, s, z, out, K.const?...)` — K,N as RUNTIME ints (loop bounds dynamic) fine; BN constexpr. Item loop inside per CTA: for it in range(pid, N//BN, NCTA). The per-stage done-counter: which counter slot? Runtime-computed offset: ctr_base + stage_id... stage_id as python int INLINE constant in the unrolled code → each stage's counter index is a compile-time constant in the generated code ✓ (straight-line unrolled in source → constants baked). Target counts: counts known at compile time except ATTENTION (nchunks runtime — target = 32·nchunks computed in-kernel from L arg ✓). The kernel source will be LONG (3×KDA sections + MLA + 4×MoE +...). Could use a python-level loop over a list of stage descriptors inside... TRITON doesn't loop python-level inside jit — no, the @triton.jit source IS python-compiled-once: python `for` over lists with constexpr... Triton unrolls python loops over constexpr ranges at TRACE time — YES: `for i in tl.static_range(3)` or plain python range over constexpr works (compile-time unroll). And PARAMETERS per unroll iteration: need stage descriptors as CONSTEXPR values trace-time — Triton constexpr propagation through python data structures is limited (You CAN: build the kernel source with closures over python lists? during tracing, python-level ints are constexpr IF passed as tl.constexpr args... hmm, work item pointers differ per stage — they're RUNTIME args. Python loop variable per stage pulls pointer ARG from... args are scalars in signature, not indexable. Could pass pointer args as separate scalars: qkv_w0, qkv_w1... then python-level: `ws = (qkv_s0, qkv_s1, ...)`: for i in static_range(3): gemv(x_ptr, ws[i], ...) — python tuple indexing of runtime TRACER values at trace time — tuple of JITFunction-traced args — python-level indexing works at trace-time (they're just python objects: triton semantic values) ✓✓. So python-level loops over tuples of args UNROLL fine. num args ~ 100+ total — generated via **kwargs? Triton signature must be explicit or use *args? `def kernel(*args)` not supported; explicit names required... 100 explicit params — unwieldy but fine. ALTERNATIVE — the pointer TABLE: build a SINGLE int64 tensor on host (cold step), kernel loads addresses from table with tl.cast to pointers: NO giant signature, kernel source compact (python loop over STATIC stage metadata tuples (type, #items... runtime args in dict?) hmm stage dims: store dims ALSO in table (int32) → kernel reads per stage: 10 int loads/CTA — trivial vs work. Python loop over static stage list (lengths/types constexpr) with pointers from table — CLEAN. inttoptr: verify support early! (tl.cast or `.to(tl.pointer_type(tl.uint8))` on int64 scalar tensor). Actually known-good pattern exists in Triton (e.g., paged attention kernels using inttoptr? Triton "pointers as int64 then bitcast": `tl.load` needs pointer type — there IS `tl.inline_asm` fallback worst case, or pass addresses as int64 and use `tl.cast(addr, tl.pi32_t)`?? Let me just test: `p = tl.load(tbl).to(tl.pointer_type(tl.float32))` — I'm fairly confident Triton supports int-to-pointer bitcast... hmm, it's "cast" semantics: inttoptr is `tl.cast(x, dtype, bitcast?...)`. There's documented `.to(tl.pointer_type(...))` for uint64→ptr in triton codebase tests (test_cast... unit test "cast int to pointer" exists? I believe YES supported.) What about dtype of Scales/Zeros accumulate & xn: group-sum trick: y[n] = Σ_g s[g,n]·(Σ_k u·xn) − Σ_g s·z·sx_g: second term needs sx_g = Σ_{k∈g} xn[k] — computed... folding: consumer CTAs need rstd anyway (computed per CTA); compute sx_g on the fly inside GEMV item: item covers full K anyway (K-loop over all groups!) — compute sx per group as it goes, then y[n] = Σ_g (s[g,n]·dot_g[n]) − Σ_g s[g,n]z[g,n]·sx_g: maintain TWO accumulators: acc_sz[g-part...]: running per-g partial? restructure: acc1[n] += s̃ where per group g: dot_g computed (128 k·BN), then acc[n] += s[g,n]·dot_g[n] − s[g,n]·z[g,n]·sx_g → per group: BN fmas for correction — fine, no separate sx pass needed (item loops groups sequentially, sx_g = Σ_k xn_k computable inline: dot over k of xn alone = per group 128 adds... extra compute per item? per (item, group): 128-wide x reduction — vs weight tile 128×BN fma — 1/BN overhead-ish. Alternatively PRE-normalize ONCE: a dedicated tiny stage writes xn (f32) + sx (18-32 f32) — ONE CTA-item (or few), others wait flag (DAG ✓ cheap — it's a stage on critical path but tiny ~2 µs... hmm 2 µs × 12 stages/blocks... 24 µs!? NO — make NORM items part of the PREVIOUS stage? norm(x) needs x complete = previous stage's B... arrows: norm stage waits DOWN counter; consumers (GEMV items) wait NORM flag instead of DOWN counter: same chain, norm adds critical-path latency ~ (2304 elems ~ 1 CTA 2 µs with atomics... multiple items: 4 items × 576 elems + atomic-add ssq → sub-µs) ✓ acceptable: per KDA block: stages: NORM(4 items), QKVG(65), REC(128), OPROJ(18), MOEN(4), GU(72), DOWN(162) = 7 stages/block ≈ 25 stage-hops total-ish. Each hop = counter release+acquire ~ 0.5-1 µs?? ≈ 15-25 µs total sync overhead vs 135 µs bandwidth floor — START here, optimize later (merge stages lazy: e.g., fold NORM into QKVG by having each GEMV item compute rstd redundantly: rstd needs FULL ssq: dependency NORM→GEMV stands; alternative: DROP separate ssq: note RMSNORM's scale is a GLOBAL scalar: gemv computes partial... no. Alternatively have EVERY GEMV item compute ssq x itself (9 KB reads + rstd) and skip NORM stage: removes NORM stages (4+... blocks ×2 norms = 8 stages!) at cost of per-item 2304-elem reduce ~ 2304/(128 lanes)... compute ~ 18 vec-loads → ~ sub-µs per item, from L2 (x hot) — CHEAPER than a stage hop ✓✓ DO THAT: no NORM stages; xn (normed x) computed inline per item into REGISTERS?? PROBLEM: xn used across the whole K-loop of an item (K=2304: held in regs 2304×4B×?? per-THREAD — no: xn lives in... Triton item code: loop over k-chunks: load x chunk (BK,), normalize, dot — x re-loaded per item from L2 (2304·4B × #items... QKVG stage: 64 items × 9 KB = 590 KB L2 traffic ✓ fine) and normalized inline with rstd recomputed per item (reduce 2304: as part of K-loop! rstd needed BEFORE normalizing any chunk → pre-pass per item over x (read 9 KB + reduce) THEN main K-loop (re-read) — fine. SAME for MoE GU items (compute ssq of h inline) and ROUTER?! Router=where? With "no norm stage", router still needs normed h: router items = their own TINY stage (MOER stage: 4 items) after OPROJ, before GU. Or fold ROUTER INTO DOWN-stage... no GU needs it. So MoE stages: ROUT(4)+GU(72)+DOWN(162). ROUT: 4 items × K-chunk partial logits atomic-add... then GU waits ROUT counter ✓. GU each computes top8 from logits (64 f32) + ssq(h) inline ✓. KDA: stages QKVG → REC → OPROJ →(MoE) ROUT → GU → DOWN = 6/block. MLA: QKV_A(64.. 6144+576 /256: 26) → ABS (absorb 128 items + rope items + append + seed-copy items...) → ATTN(32·nc) → COMB(32) → AOUT(32/128) → OPROJ(18) → ROUT → GU → DOWN = 9 stages. Block counts: 3·6+9 = 27 stages + FINAL cast (1 stage, 2-9 items: cast B→bf16 = 2304 elems... DOWN already ends; CAST stage: few items) = 28 stages. Sync hops ~28 × latency-of-spin-release ≈ ... will measure. Could later merge (ROUT into OPROJ via in-phase DAG: stages merged = multi-sub-stage items within one stage-share-counter... refinement later.) Wait, actually reconsider: can DOWN merge with following block's QKVG? No — QKVG normalizes B which needs DOWN done. Race-free order enforced by counters ✓. 20. Also need: hidden INPUT h (bf16, 2304) → first block's norm reads it: kernel arg in_h ptr; copy to f32 xa? Each QKVG item reads in_h bf16 directly + computes ssq (read twice: ssq pass + K-loop; bf16 2304 = 4.6 KB) ✓ NO initial copy stage ✓. Intermediate blocks read B f32 directly as x ✓. bbeta: x@beta_w (2304→32 bf16 matmul): beta item within QKVG stage: 32 outputs; single item: K=2304 × 32: reads 147 KB bf16 — ~7-10 µs single-CTA!? split: 4 items × 8 outputs (36 KB each ~ 2-3 µs) hmm; or hold beta in... 32 outputs over 32 items × 2304×64B=... per output col: 2304 bf16 = 4.6 KB; 32 items → 32 CTAs × 4.6 KB ✓ sub-µs ✓. Same for ROUT items (4× partial atomic) ✓. beta output: f32 scratch (32) — sigmoid applied in REC ✓. And QKVG GEMVs write q_raw etc. f32 (16 KB each scratch) ✓. conv_w: (3,4096,4) bf16 read by REC ✓. IMPORTANT — norm W: attn_norm (2304) bf16 & moe_norm bf16 — read inline ✓. 21. Numerical fidelity vs reference — the RISKY spot: KDA exp/decay: ref computes g.exp() where g=-softplus(raw) — our sigmoid(-raw) — mathematically equal; float path: ref f32 softplus then exp — could differ from our sigmoid by ulps — god for cosine. Softmax over attention: ref computed scores fp32 → softmax fp32 (with max-subtraction internally ✓ torch softmax subtracts max). Our online softmax fp32 exp: small diffs, fine. MoE eps: w/(w.sum()+1e-9) — replicate exactly. RMS eps 1e-6 ✓ routed_scaling 2.446 fp32 ✓ scale factors ✓ rope theta ✓ rope interleave: ref rope pairs (even,odd) adjacent — replicate: x[0::2]→cos·e−sin·o; x[1::2]→cos·o+sin·e. With cos/sin over dim//2 frequencies indexed by pair position: inv = theta^(-(2i)/dim), i=pair index ✓. bf16 ROUNDING POINTS in ref: after each linear (to bf16), conv outs bf16, q_rope/k_rope roped → bf16, cache stores bf16 (c_kv bf16!), kv_b inputs bf16... ref kvb = kv_b(c_kv): c_kv cached bf16 @ dequant bf16 → baseline variant... REFERENCE (oracle for check): QuantLinear.forward: x.float() @ wbf.float() → bf16 out. MLA: kvb computed on the CURRENT full c_kv (bf16) → f32. scores fp32. p softmax f32 → (ref keeps f32! p stays float32 in ref; baseline casts to x.dtype! WE ORACLE = reference.py: `p = torch.softmax(scores, dim=0)` stays f32, `o = torch.einsum('lh,lhd->hd', p, v)` f32... then .to(bf16) before o_proj.) — our f32 everything ≈ ref within fp32-noise EXCEPT our ABSORB reassociation: ref: kvb = W_b @ c per token (f32 from bf16 inputs), scores = q_nope·k_nope (f32 sums over 128) + rope part. OURS: q_abs[h,c] (f32) then scores = q_abs·c (sum over 512 in DIFFERENT ORDER + W-quantization interplay: SAME weights used; absorb is exact algebra; rounding diffs ~ fp32 eps × 512 terms — fine for cosine 0.98 on 2304-dim — plenty margin: typically 0.9999+.) v = part of kvb; ref o = Σ p·v (f32); ours: pv (f32) then absorb-out W_v — exact re-association ✓. KDA: ref S update f32 exact order; ours identical math (same order per element!) ✓. The one real deviation: ROUTER selection ties ~ measure-zero; MoE w normalization identical formula. Verdict: cosine ~0.999 expected ✓. 22. Extra detail — the DOWN-stage ATOMIC accumulation into B (f32): B must hold h = x + attn BEFORE moe adds: OPROJ writes B[..] = x[..] + attnout[..] directly ✓. DOWN atomics add w_j·out and shared·out ✓. NEXT block's QKVG items wait BOTH DOWN counter ✓. And in-block: ROUT items wait OPROJ counter... ROUT reads h!! (normed h for router) — reading B while... GU/DOWN also read/write B — ROUT only READS ✓; DOWN atomics happen AFTER GU ✓; ordering enforced by stage counters ✓. 23. Buffer inventory (persistent scratch, per model instance): - xbuf_a, xbuf_b (f32 2304) ping-pong block input/output per block; in_h bf16 is block0 input (kernel reads in_h directly for block 0? block0's "x" = in_h (bf16) — GEMV items read x ptr... make ALL blocks read from a UNIFORM source: copy in_h→xbuf? extra stage (1 item, 2304) — or handle block0 x-dtype via flag arg (x_is_bf16 for stage... stage-level param: pass TWO source variants? simplest: NORM-cast STAGE: stage EXT(2 items) writes xbuf_a = f32(in_h); KDA0.QKVG waits EXT — +1 stage, trivial cost ~1 µs. DO IT (uniformity worth it). - ssq: computed inline per item (no buffers). - q_raw,k_raw,v_raw,g_raw (f32 4096 ×4) per KDA layer call — REUSED across layers (sequential) → single shared set ✓ (stages force order). - beta_raw f32 32. - conv window ping-pong bufs: per KDA layer: cq/ck/cv × 2: (3,4096) bf16 each → 6 bufs/layer ×3 = 18 × 24 KB = 450 KB. INIT: cold step reads input state cq tensor, writes bufA; state marker... WAIT — first step reads INPUT cq; shift-write into bufA; return dict["cq"]=bufA; SECOND step must read bufA, write bufB... in-kernel ping-pong via per-step ARG (pp flag per layer: 0: in=input-tensor?? first-step source is the INPUT STATE TENSOR (address changes per run!) — arg per layer: cq_src_ptr (either input tensor or bufA or bufB) and cq_dst_ptr (bufA or bufB) — CHOSEN HOST-SIDE per step, passed as args!! (no marker tensors needed!) — host tracks: cold → src=input tensor, dst=buf[i][0], returned dict holds buf[i][0]; next: src=buf[i][0] dst=buf[i][1]... ✓ (host-side logic trivial via marker IN the returned dict: d["_cqp"] = 0...). Ret'd dict entry "cq": buf tensor — SHAPES (3,4096) ✓. NOTE: returned dict for KDA layers: {"S": in-place-updated input S, "cq": buf0, "ck": buf0, "cv": buf0, "_pp": 0}. Input S reused (mutated) ✓. - o_raw f32 4096; q_abs f32 (32,512); pv f32 (32,512); attn partial scratch (nchunks_max×32, 514) f32 — nchunks_max = ceil((16384+64)/1024)+1 ≈ 17 → 17×32×514×4 ≈ 1.1 MB ✓; COMB output pv. - mla q_raw f32 6144, kva_raw f32 576. - cache big: c_kv big (cap × 512 bf16), k_rope big (cap × 64). cap: max ctx + max steps + margin: host allocates at cold step with cap = L0 + 8192?? benchmark max steps 32; check 4-ish; cap = L0+512 plenty... but CHAINED: no cap growth needed (cap - L0 >= steps). Allocate cap = L0 + 1024. SAFE (adjust: if exceeded → fallback? keep dim simple: cap = L0 + 4096; memory 4096×512×2 = 4 MB extra — fine.) - h_slot bf16 (9,1024); logits f32 64; rout_w f32 8 (or recompute per CTA); - counters buf int32 (say 4096) monotonic (never reset; pass base offset arg per LAUNCH incremented by TOTAL_STAGES... careful → track launches; counter idx = base + stage_id ✓ base arg. Also seed/copy items flag. - out_h bf16 2304 (returned hidden; ALSO next-step input via chaining: benchmark passes OUR returned h back in ✓ — next launch reads out_h as in_h ✓ SAME BUFFER — safe? step signature hidden input tensor: host code step(): in_ptr = hidden.data_ptr — if hidden IS our out_h buffer ✓ constant address ✓.) - state dict "table": the ARG POINTER TABLE?? — decided: args as explicit pointers? vs table — FINAL DECISION BELOW after inttoptr spike. 24. Spike tests to run FIRST (before building the big thing): a. Triton scalar atomic + spin-while barrier across 148 CTAs: correctness (no deadlock) + latency (~µs?). b. int→pointer cast load (table approach feasibility). c. int4 GEMV microbench: achievable fraction of 1.8 TB/s for K∈{2304,4096,1024,512}, N-BN tiles, num_warps, BN variants. TARGET ≥ 80% peak (1.45+ TB/s). d. estimate barrier×N + latency. e. GPU device props: SM count, clock, L2 size. 25. Host-side step() flow: ``` def step(self, hidden, state): self._ensure_setup(cfg-device...) # once: allocate scratch, compile kernel args... = self._bind(state, hidden) # resolve pointers, pp flags, L, seed flags; maybe rebuild arg table (cold or L-boundary) self._kernel[grid](...) # ONE launch return out_h_view?, updated state dict ``` Timing loop synchronization: nothing here syncs — pure async ✓ (torch.cuda.synchronize outside). Also baseline must be beaten on LATENCY measured as best-of-3 runs of `steps` chained steps; python overhead per step (~arg prep): keep LIGHT (<20 µs/step; python dict ops, few int computes — precompute per-run state cache keyed by... keep attributes; per-step python ≈ 30 µs is TOO slow if kernel is 135 µs — pipelining: python of step n+1 overlaps kernel n (async!) → hides python ✓ as long as no sync in step ✓. Their timing: perf_counter around `steps` chained steps then synchronize — total = max(launches cpu time, gpu time) roughly; python 30 µs × 32 = 1 ms CPU vs GPU 32×135=4.3 ms ✓ fine even serial. 26. Reference is reference.py; answer in solution.py with the same Model interface.  Semantics (bench-simplified Native Sparse Attention — same as reference.nsa_attend):   Inputs q,k,v are (B, H, S, D) bf16. Causal. For each query position t:   1. Split keys into blocks of block_size=64.   2. Block importance = mean of (q·k / sqrt(D)) over keys in the block that are causal (j<=t).   3. Select the top_n_blocks=8 blocks by importance, union with a local sliding window of the last sliding_window=64 tokens, causal only.   4. Softmax attention over the selected key indices only; write o[t].  Match the reference within ~0.1 abs/rel on bf16. Shapes in shapes.py are a geomean sweep (Hard-style): aligned S, S not multiple of block_size=64 (e.g. 4127, 8191), long context, batched short. FLOPs are dense-equivalent (full S×S) so sparsity does not inflate TFLOPS — speed comes from doing less work well.   This is KernelBench-CUDA: real CUDA only. Forbidden: Triton, flash-attn, flashinfer, SDPA, CuteDSL. You may read the NSA paper (arXiv
...

With a lean kernel, 24 warps/SM... SM120 max warps/SM = 64? Blackwell consumer (GB20x): 4 SMSPs × 16 warp slots = 64 warps/SM max, up to 2048 threads/SM, 32 CTAs/SM max, 64K regs/SM... 65536 regs/SM ÷ (W×32 threads × regs/thread). Choose NCTA = 188 × k. For the barrier to not deadlock, NCTA must be ≤ max co-resident. I'll verify with actual occupancy — but since a Triton kernel's occupancy data is available after compile (n_regs, n_spills, shared memory), I can compute blocks-per-SM and pick k accordingly. If registers ≤ 255/thread... For num_warps=8 (256 threads): 256 threads × 200 regs = 51K regs — only 1 CTA/SM if 200 regs/thread! 64K regs/SM: 256×256×... 64K/(256) = 256 regs per thread for 1 CTA of 8 warps; 2 CTAs → 128 regs/thread. Our kernel will be register-hungry at times (128×32 fp32 block = 16 regs/thread at 256 threads) — plus Triton overhead. Let's plan NCTA = 188 (1 CTA/SM) with num_warps = 16 (512 threads, 128 regs/thread budget), or NCTA=376 (2/SM) with num_warps=8. Both plausible; tune later. Barrier cost scales with NCTA; 188 nicer. But memory parallelism: one CTA/SM with 16 warps = 512 threads... to saturate 1.8 TB/s, need ~ (1.8TB/s ÷ 188 SMs) = 9.6 GB/s per SM; with 512 threads × 16-byte loads in flight... plenty. Actually, GDDR bandwidth saturation typically needs only ~4-8 outstanding 32B sectors per thread... Let me not over-theorize; spike C will measure. Now, before heavy GPU work, note the LOCK: benchmark/check runs must go through the wrapper (they queue). Spike scripts too. I'll write them as files, run via `python` (wrapper) in background, and poll. Serialization means I should batch experiments into fewer GPU sessions: e.g., ONE spike script that does barrier test + inttoptr + GEMV microbench in one go = one lock acquisition, then exits. Actually better: acquire the lock ONCE for an interactive-ish session? The lock is held per-process (flock by each python invocation, released at exit). A long-running process holding the lock blocks others... but that's how it works — my check.py/benchmark.py runs need it too. I'll be considerate: short-ish GPU scripts, batch work. Let me now write the spike script. Spike A — barrier/scalar atomic semantics: ```python import torch, triton, triton.language as tl @triton.jit def bar_kernel(ctr, out, n_iter, NCTA: tl.constexpr): pid = tl.program_id(0) for it in range(n_iter): tl.debug_barrier() tl.atomic_add(ctr + tl.arange(0, 1), 1, sem="release", scope="gpu") cur = tl.sum(tl.atomic_add(ctr + tl.arange(0, 1), 0, sem="acquire", scope="gpu")) target = (it + 1) * NCTA while cur < target: cur = tl.sum(tl.atomic_add(ctr + tl.arange(0, 1), 0, sem="acquire", scope="gpu")) tl.debug_barrier() tl.store(out + pid, 1) grid = (188,) ... ``` Wait — `tl.atomic_add(ctr + tl.arange(0,1), 1)` returns a [1] tensor; sum → scalar; while loop on scalar works. IMPORTANT subtlety: is the atomic executed once per CTA ([1]-shaped tensor distributed across threads)? If instead all 256 threads each issue an atomic on element 0 (Triton semantics: [1]-shaped tensor ops are executed... I think masked to the threads owning the element — Triton distributes elements across threads; a 1-element tensor is owned by 1 thread; others masked). So each CTA increments by 1. Verify: after n_iter iterations with NCTA blocks, ctr[0] should == NCTA * n_iter (if once per CTA per iter) — actually each iter adds NCTA total. If per-thread it'd add NCTA×threads. Measure. Also measure barrier latency: time n_iter barriers. Deadlock risk: if grid > resident CTAs → hang. I'll cap at what occupancy allows (start with 188, num_warps=4 — tiny kernel → residency huge ✓). Also test: while-spin loop compile. Spike B — inttoptr: ```python @triton.jit def ptr_kernel(tab, out): a = tl.load(tab + tl.arange(0,1), ...) # int64 p = a.to(tl.pointer_type(tl.float32), bitcast=False) # inttoptr v = tl.load(p + tl.arange(0,16)) tl.store(out + tl.arange(0,16), v) ``` Hmm — `.to(tl.pointer_type(...))`: does Triton support int→ptr? I recall `tl.cast(x, tl.pointer_type(tl.float32))`. Actually scalar int to pointer: there was `tl.inline_asm_elementwise` workaround... Triton 3.x: `tl.cast` with pointer target → inttoptr supported since 2.x I believe. Test BOTH syntaxes. If inttoptr unsupported → fallback: explicit args (big signature) OR a base pointer + per-stage OFFSETS (offsets in a table, single common base impossible — weights are separate allocations!). Alternative: ONE giant flat uint8 buffer where I REPACK all weights myself (copy at setup) — then per-stage params = (offset, K, N) into my flat buffer + a few separate pointers for state/scratch. That ALWAYS works and ALSO lets me choose better layouts!! Let me think: repack all int4 weights + scales + zeros into one flat buffer (≈1 GB copy, one-time at first step — ~1-2 s D2D, during check's first step... that's fine? check.py first step does this once; benchmark._run warmups trigger once per _run — hmm, per _run we DON'T rebuild (weights unchanged) — key the cache by a version counter bumped on load_state_dict via hook. First step of a run: if table built → skip. NICE properties: - All weight addresses stable → table never rebuilt per step. - Layout freedom: e.g., swizzle/pack scales+zeros adjacent to int4 blocks for better locality (interleaved "group blocks": per k-group: int4 data (128×N/2 bytes) + scales (N×2) + zeros (N×2) contiguous → DRAM row locality for the same k-sweep... honestly the separate arrays are fine too — each CTA streams w sequentially and reads s/z per group from another region; both fully coalesced streams; DRAM row thrash minimal. But one flat buffer simplifies pointer management enormously: kernel args = base_ptr + stage param table (offsets int32/int64) + state ptrs + scratch ptrs (scratch: also one flat region for x/q/k/v/etc!, offsets constexpr!). State tensors (S, windows, caches): must live in INPUT tensors (harness-provided) → explicit ptr args (bounded count: 3 KDA × 4 + mla 4 + hidden + out ≈ 20 args ✓ manageable). But wait — repacking int4 weights into my buffer = the load_state_dict buffers (w_q etc.) still exist and load normally (strict ✓); my flat buffer is a DERIVED copy refreshed after load_state_dict (hook). Extra GPU memory +1.1 GB — fine (96 GB). Scales/zeros bf16 → keep bf16 or pre-convert to fp32 in the flat buffer? fp32 doubles tiny bytes but halves cvt work; per group per BN... negligible either way. Keep bf16 (exactly as ref: (u - z) * s with s,z bf16→ cvt f32 once per group per column — trivial). Layout option for efficiency: keep per-tensor contiguous (w_q region, then scales, then zeros) — same as originals. OR interleave scales per column-block?? Keep simple first pass: plain copies; optimize later if profiling shows s/z stalls. Interleaving idea (do it NOW — cheap and proven in Marlin et al.): store per weight in "N-panel-major" order matching how a CTA sweeps: CTA handles BN columns over all K: it reads rows k: w_q[k/2, n0:n0+BN] — stride N between successive k — DRAM-unfriendly-ish: consecutive rows of the panel are N bytes apart → each 16B vector load from a different DRAM row-ish... but many CTAs sweep different panels concurrently; HBM likes ≥1KB sequential per bank... Hmm: traffic efficiency measured by spike C — if a plain row-major sweep hits ≥85% peak, skip repack cleverness. If not, repack into panel-major: weight stored as [n0-panel][k]/contiguous per panel → each CTA streams CONTIGUOUS bytes ✓. With flat repack buffer I control layout — spike C tests both layouts. Spike C — GEMV bandwidth. K=2304, N=4096. int4 (1152, 4096) + s/z (18, 4096) bf16. Variants: BN ∈ {128, 256, 512}, warps ∈ {4, 8, 16}, k-loop order (row-major vs panel-major). Measure GB/s vs theoretical 1.8 TB/s total. Realistic achievable stream ~ 1.5-1.7 TB/s. Actually — also CRITICAL: measure how fast N CTAs × streaming reads go vs NCopies — simple `torch.clone` of 1 GB D2D tells achievable copy bandwidth (read+write); pure-read: kernel summing 1 GB. RTX PRO 6000 spec 1.8 TB/s peak; realistic ~ 1.65. Let me also confirm device prop L2 size: query via device props (p.L2_cache_size didn't print — included above? didn't get to it; add now). One more spike D — small-ball: costs of torch.cuda ops per launch (kernel launch overhead ~3-5 µs) matter... only ONE launch per step — irrelevant. Let me write spike.py now. I'll run it through the wrapper in the background and poll, since it needs the GPU lock. Keep it SELF-CONTAINED and fast (<2 min GPU time), printing everything. Also — IMPORTANT — discover ncu/nsys availability for later. `which ncu nsys` (CPU-only, no GPU needed). And verify how `triton.jit` scalar-while behaves: compile check in spike A. Let me also confirm Triton's `tl.atomic_add` supports `scope` and `sem` kwargs: Triton 3.6: tl.atomic_add(pointer, val, mask=None, sem=None, scope=None) ✓. And `tl.debug_barrier()` exists ✓ (emits barrier0). Also — check `tl.cast` int64→pointer: `tl.cast(x, tl.pointer_type(tl.float32))` — verify. Let me write the spike script.

+"""Spike tests for the megakernel design.
+
+A. Scalar atomic + spin barrier across NCTA co-resident CTAs: correctness of
+   [1]-shaped atomic (increment count), no-deadlock, barrier latency.
+B. int64 -> pointer cast (data-driven stage pointers from a table).
+C. Fused int4 dequant GEMV bandwidth: tile/warp/layout sweep vs peak.
+D. Baseline sanity: pure read bandwidth, copy bandwidth, launch overhead.
+"""
+import time
+import torch
+import triton
+import triton.language as tl
+

Per block ≈ 15+4+19 = 38 tensors. 4 blocks ≈ 152 tensors + MLA extras (q,kv_a,kv_b,o ×3 = 12) — wait MLA attn has 4 QuantLinears = 12 tensors; KDA has 5 = 15. Total tensors ≈ 3×(15+19) + (12+19) + norms 8 + beta 3 + conv 3 + router... let me recount: - KDA blk: attn q,k,v,g,o (5×3=15) + beta(1) + conv_w(1) + norms(2) = 19; moe: router(1) + 6 expert sets × 3 = 18+1 = 19. Total 38/blk × 3 = 114. - MLA blk: attn q,kv_a,kv_b,o (4×3=12) + norms 2 = 14; moe 19. Total 33. Grand total = 147 tensor pointers. Plus ~10 scratch/state pointers. Explicit signature = ~160 args. That's legal (CUDA limit 4KB = 512 args on newer CUDA; Triton may have its own limits but 160×8B = 1.3KB fine). But writing/maintaining a 160-arg call is error-prone. The TABLE approach is cleaner: build one int64 tensor with all addresses in a fixed layout (computed by Python walking the same stage list the kernel unrolls). Kernel reads per-stage base offsets... but per STAGE the kernel needs ~3-4 pointers (x_ptr is scratch-fixed, w_ptr/s_ptr/z_ptr/out_ptr). Reading 4 int64 from L2 per stage per CTA = trivial. I'll go with table + inttoptr if spike B passes; else explicit args. **Stage list & item decomposition** (per launch; assume NCTA ≈ 188-376, BN=256 default for GEMV): Per stage I need: type, wait-counters (which prior stages), done-counter slot (+ target count), and per-item decode. The kernel processes stages SEQUENTIALLY in unrolled code; within a stage, items strided by NCTA. Since stages are unrolled python-side, "wait" and "bump" use counter-slot indices baked per stage. Consumers wait on specific producer stages only (dependency DAG), but for simplicity most stages just wait on their immediate predecessor stage(s) — I'll encode explicit dep lists: Deps analysis per block b (KDA): - NORM inline (no stage) — QKVG items each compute rstd of x. Wait — actually RMSNorm needs ssq over the FULL x; each QKVG item computes ssq redundantly (2304 reads). Fine. - QKVG (items: q,k,v,g panels + beta items): deps: for block 0: EXT stage (in_h→xbuf) — hmm earlier I decided EXT casts in_h bf16→f32 xbuf. For blocks >0: prev block's DOWN stage. Total ~68. - REC: items = 32 heads × 4 dv-chunks = 128. deps: QKVG (+ reads conv windows from state ptr arg, writes ping-pong dst). - OPROJ: items = 2304/256 = 9. deps: REC. Writes B = x + attn (fp32). - ROUT: router items: logits via split-K partial atomic: 8 items (K-chunks). deps: OPROJ. Also clears? logits buffer zeroed... atomic add needs zeroed start! Zero via... hmm. Who zeroes logits each step? An item in a PRIOR stage (e.g., REC or the EXT stage) can zero it (64 floats) — any stage guaranteed to run before ROUT. Or: ROUT stage items write partials to distinct slots and GU computes the sum (no atomics): logits_partial (8, 64) f32; GU item sums 8×64 = trivial. DO THAT (zero-init-free). - GU: items = 9 slots × (1024/BN: 4) = 36. deps: ROUT (+OPROJ done implicitly via ROUT). Computes top8 from logits_partial each item redundantly (sums partials → softmax → top8 — same deterministic result across CTAs ✓). ssq of h redundantly per item. Writes h_slot (9,1024) bf16 + also needs w_j weights → computed redundantly as well. - DOWN: items = 9 slots × (2304/256) = 81... 2304/256 = 9 → 81 items. deps: GU. atomic_add into B. Next block's QKVG deps: DOWN. MLA block: - QKVA: q_proj (6144/256 = 24 items), kv_a (576/256 → BN=128: 4.5 → use BN=64 → 9? 576/128=4.5... 576 = 4×128 + 64. hmm. BN=192? not pow2. Use masked BN=128 → ceil=5 items (last masked). Or BN=256 → ceil(576/256)=3 items masked. Fine — masked tails.) deps: prev DOWN. - ABS: absorb q_abs + rope + append + optional seed copy: - absorb items: 32 h × 4 c-chunks = 128. deps: QKVA. - rope items: q_rope rope: 32 (one per head, 64 vals) — fold into absorb items (each h's chunk-0 does q_rope[h] rope? 4 c-chunk items per h; chunk 0 handles rope for head h ✓ +1 tiny work inline). k_rope rope + append: 1 item (deps QKVA) — writes kv_cache_big[L] (+k_rope roped). Also fold into... assign item idx 128 = append+rope item. - seed-copy items (only when seed flag): nc = ceil(L0/512) items (each copies 512 rows × (512+64) bf16 ≈ 590 KB) — hmm 512 rows = 576 KB per item at L0=16384 → 32 items. These have NO dependents within this launch (only future launches) — but must NOT race with ATTN (ATTN on seed step reads... I said ATTN reads base=original when seed. But wait — with the flat "returned view of big" scheme, non-seed steps read big entirely; seed step reads original + the appended row from big[L]. So ATTN addressing: rows [0, L0) from srcA (=big or original depending on seed), row L0 from big. I'll pass att_srcA_ptr as an ARG chosen host-side. Then the copy items write big[0:L0] concurrently — no conflict since ATTN doesn't read big rows < L0 on seed steps. ✓ - deps encode: ABS items depend on QKVA counter; ATTN items depend on ABS counter (need q_abs + ropes + appended row); ROPE/append item is IN ABS stage ✓. - ATTN: items = 32 × nchunks(L) (runtime). deps: ABS. Writes partials (m, l, pv) per item. - COMB: 32 items (heads). deps: ATTN. Reads nchunks(L) partials per head; writes pv_final (H,512) f32, normalized by l. - AOUT (absorb-out): items = 32 × 4 (d-chunks of 32) = 128. deps: COMB. GEMV with K=512: out4096[n] = Σ_c pv[h,c]·W_v[c, h*128+d] → o_pre f32 4096. - OPROJ, ROUT, GU, DOWN same as KDA (different dims/weights). - FINAL: cast stage: 2304 elems → out bf16: 2 items (1152 each). deps: block3 DOWN. ABS item (h, c-chunk of 128): contraction over d (weight's N dim): for fixed h, c in [c0, c0+128): q_abs[h,c] = Σ_{d=0..127} W_k[c, h*128+d] · qn[h,d]. Weight access: rows c (packed 128/2=64 per c-chunk... c is the IN dim: packed (256, 8192) int4: rows = c/2; tile = rows [c0/2, c0/2+64) × cols [h*128, h*128+128) → 64×128 bytes = 8 KB. unpack along c (rows) ✓ same as rowmajor GEMV but contraction along N (columns). scales (4, 8192): group of c → single row group (c0/128): s[g, cols] 128 vals; per element multiply (2D (128 c, 128 d) block) then contract axis=1 (over d) with qn[h,:] (128,): q_abs = Σ_d w[c,d]·qn[d] → tl.sum(w * qn[None, :], axis=1) (128,) ✓. Note: dequant math: ((u−z)∗s) — same ✓. **REC stage details** (as analyzed): item=(h, dvchunk): BN_DV=32 → 4 per head. - Needs: q_raw, k_raw (all 128 dk of head), v_raw (BN 32 dv), g_raw (128 dk), beta (1), windows cq/ck/cv [3, ch], conv_w [3, ch, 4] slices, S block (128, 32) read+write in-place, o_raw write (BN). - conv: out_c = silu(win0·w0 + win1·w1 + win2·w2 + val·w3)?? Hmm wait, one subtlety: q,k conv over ALL 128 channels of head h vs v conv only its 32: fine. Also q scale: × Dk^-0.5 = 128^-0.5. Pred/o math as before. - Also NORM-fold: REC doesn't need norms. **Scratch layout** (one flat f32/u8 region, offsets constexpr): - x_a, x_b (2304 f32 ×2) - q_raw, k_raw, v_raw, g_raw (4096 f32 ×4) - beta_raw (32) - o_raw (4096) - q_abs (16384 = 32×512) - pv (16384) - attn partials: (NCH_MAX × 32, 514) f32 — for L up to 16k+: nchunks ≤ ceil(16448/1024) = 17 → 17×32×514×4B = 1.12 MB - kv_a raw (576) - logits partial (8, 64) - h_slots (9, 1024) bf16 - out bf16 (2304) - counters int32 (say 128) Total ≈ manageable in a few MB. Each RUN allocates (per state dict! per _run fresh init_state → new state dict → new big buffer alloc each _run: 13 allocs, caching allocator handles ✓). My step: - allocate big_kv (cap,512), big_kr (cap,64) [cap = L0 + margin]. - pass: seed=1, kv_src = original c_kv ptr, L0=dimension, write row L0 in big. - kernel: ATTN reads rows [0,L0) from original, row L0 from big. copy items fill big[0:L0] from original (concurrent). - Return state: {"c_kv": big[:L0+1], "k_rope": big_kr[:L0+1], "_mk": wrapper bookkeeping...}: include private keys: "_kvbig": (big, big_kr), "_L": L0+1, "_L0": L0 (base row count now copied)... Next step (warm): big present → seed=0, src = big, row L = L0+1 writes to big[L], ATTN reads all rows [0, L] from big + new row L → uniform. Return c_kv = big[:L+1] view, "_L" = L+1 ✓. Check.py calls step ONCE per state → always cold → fine. Benchmark chains → warm after first ✓. Edge: check.py compares st_sol[mla]["c_kv"] cosine — big[:L0+1] view — values = copied original (exact bf16 copy) + new row ✓. ALSO c_kv must be the CACHE = PAST CONTEXT latents — the copy preserves them ✓ cosine ≈ 1.0 vs ref (ref cat original + new) ✓✓. KDA state cold: {"S": f32 tensor mut in place ✓, "cq": input tensor (3,4096)}: kernel reads src=input cq, writes dst=pp_buf0; return {"S": same input S (updated), "cq": buf0, "ck": buf1(no—per tensor: cq→cqa0, ck→cka0, cv→cva0), "_pp": 0}. Warm: reads bufs[_pp], writes bufs[1-_pp] ✓. Now S in-place update across steps: fine (returned same tensor each time, mutated). And benchmark's `_run` gets fresh state per call ✓. One more subtlety: ping-pong window buffers are allocated per MODEL (self) not per state — cold step always writes buf0. If TWO state dicts interleave steps on the same model (check: st_ref... no, sol steps only on st_sol; benchmark: one state at a time) — no interleave ✓. But cold-vs-warm keyed on "_pp" in dict ✓ state-local. And benchmark `_time` calls `_run` for warmup (3 steps) and timed (steps) — sequential ✓. **In-kernel stage counter budget**: stages ≈ per KDA block: QKVG, REC, OPROJ, ROUT, GU, DOWN = 6 ×3 = 18; MLA: QKVA, ABS, ATTN, COMB, AOUT, OPROJ, ROUT, GU, DOWN = 9; EXT (1) + seedcopy? (fold into ABS) + FINAL cast (1) = 29 stages. counters: 32 slots ✓ pass base offset per launch = launches_so_far × 32... counters area per launch 32 int32; monotonically advance 32 per launch; buffer 65536 counters → 2048 launches per buffer lifetime... benchmark: 3 runs × (3 warmup + steps×trials... total ~ few hundred launches per benchmark invocation; check ~12. To never wrap: allocate counters 2^20 and reset when needed (offset wraps → but old values nonzero!! → allocate FRESH zeroed tensor when exhausted (rare) ✓ host-side logic). Also — counter monotone per launch: stage i target = #items(i) completed; consumers spin until counters[base+i] == items_i. Since stages have FIXED order and DAG deps, and every CTA visits EVERY stage (even with zero work items — still must BUMP: bump per item completion... then target = #items; CTAs with no work in that stage just skip (they don't bump) — consumers wait for == items ✓ producers bump per item ✓. But if a CTA owns an item of stage S and finishes, it must bump BEFORE moving to stage S+1's items. Fine: per-item bump after completion (or per-CTA bump of its count... per-item simpler: atomic_add(+1) per item — thousands of atomics per launch — fine). Hmm, per-CTA bumping: a CTA processes its items of stage S then does ONE atomic_add of its personal count ✓ fewer atomics; the consumer waits == items ✓ equivalent. DO per-CTA aggregated bump. **Now — the CRITICAL deadlock condition**: every CTA must VISIT every stage's wait/bump code in the SAME ORDER and — with per-CTA aggregated bump — a CTA bumps stage S counter even if it had 0 items (adds 0 — fine but then consumer target = items works regardless ✓). Spin-waits: consumer stage S+1 spins for counter[S]==target BEFORE reading its inputs... where do I put the wait? A stage's wait must be executed by ALL CTAs?? NO — only CTAs that need the data... but ALL CTAs run the code path (uniform); the wait is cheap if counter already reached target. ALL CTAs wait (uniform code) then compute their items then bump. Deadlock-free because: counter[S] reaches target once all items of S done; items of S done by CTAs that... any CTA waiting at S+1 does not hold items of stage ≤ S unprocessed?? A CTA's items of stage S were processed BEFORE its wait for stage S+1 wait — code order ✓. But what about stage S+1's WAIT depending on stage S completion while some OTHER CTA's stage-S items are queued BEHIND its own waits for stage S-1...: induction over stage order: stage taps strictly increasing → the DAG is a chain → topological ✓ no deadlock. Key: a stage's items run AFTER that CTA passed waits of all earlier stages — all waits reference EARLIER stages only ✓. But hmm — I said some stages dep on non-immediate stages (e.g., DOWN deps GU (immediate) ✓, ATTN deps ABS ✓...) — all chains. And ABS copy-items have no dep (on what? need big allocated + original ptr — arg-level, no stage dep; but they share the ABS stage slot ... consumers: nobody in-launch ✓). One more: QKVG stage (block b>0) deps DOWN(b-1) — chain ✓. But ALSO REC/QKVG reads xbuf ping-pong: block b reads x_{b%2}, writes... OPROJ(b) and DOWN(b) write B = x_{(b+1)%2}? Block b computes its output into buffer X_{b%2 flip}: wait — h = x + attn; B written by OPROJ(b) = h (x + attn); DOWN(b) atomically adds moe to B → block out. Next block reads THAT as x. So only need xbuf ping-pong: block b: xin = XA or XB (alternating), B = the OTHER. ✓ zero conflicts since DOWN(b) completes before QKVG(b+1) starts (counter) ✓. **Also in_h for block 0**: EXT stage: items copy in_h bf16 → XA f32 (2304 elems; 2 items: 1152 each — or 1; make 8 items for parallelism... trivial). Then QKVG(0) deps EXT ✓. And OPROJ(0) writes XB = XA + attn... reads XA ✓, DOWN(0) adds into XB ✓, QKVG(1) reads XB, writes B → XA... alternates ✓ FINAL: cast X?→out: last block index 3 → out written... FINAL stage: cast B(=block3's buffer) → out bf16 ✓ deps DOWN(3) ✓. **Alright — hidden out for chain**: return out tensor (persistent self._out) — benchmark passes it straight back as hidden → block0 EXT reads out from PREVIOUS launch — fine (kernel boundary). BUT careful: returning THE SAME self._out tensor every step while benchmark holds `h` = it: h gets overwritten next launch ✓ values still the "current" h ✓. check does o_sol... they read values post-step ✓. One more: benchmark's correctness gate runs sol_model.step(tok.clone(), st_s) — o_s read immediately ✓. **Routing determinism detail**: GU item j handles slot j of top8: needs THE SORTED top8 (experts idx + weights). Deterministic redundant compute per CTA: sum partials (8,64)→64 logits, softmax fp32 over 64, then top-8 (sorted desc by prob, tie → lower index to match torch.topk? torch.topk on CUDA: for equal values order unspecified-ish; probs from same math as ref's torch softmax → ties ~never). Implement via simple iterative max-extraction 8 times over 64-vec — fine. But CAREFUL: ref computes router as nn.Linear bf16 → logits bf16?? `self.router(x)`: x bf16, weight bf16 → torch matmul → output **bf16** (torch linear out dtype = input dtype, accum fp32 → round to bf16) → then `.float()` softmax fp32. The bf16 ROUNDING of logits creates material gaps between experts (bf16 ulp at |l|~0.1 is ~4e-4) — so ties in fp32 sense don't exist but NEAR-ties of our fp32-direct computation vs their bf16-rounded could flip ranking only if two logits within ~1e-3 after rounding... their ROUNDING is what matters: probs = softmax(bf16(logits)). If I compute logits fp32 accurate, softmax(fp32 logits) vs softmax(bf16 logits) DIFFER by up to ~1e-3 relative in exp terms exp(Δ)~1.001 — that does NOT flip the SET unless ranks cross — but the WEIGHTS w differ by ~0.1% → output differs ~0.1% relative → cosine impact tiny (still 0.999+). To be safer: round my logits to bf16 before softmax — cheap, matches ref scalar-for-scalar (fp32 accum then round = same as torch's bf16 out) ✓ DO IT. Then top8 identical to ref w.h.p. ✓. Similarly, ROUT stage logits stored as... partial sums: atomics... no — partial slots (8,64) fp32, GU sums → rounds bf16 → softmax fp32 ✓. **ssq computations**: norm uses x.float().pow(2).mean + 1e-6 rsqrt — over 2304 — replicate in fp32, sum order differs from torch — ulps — but this is a SCALAR multiplying whole vector: relative diff ~1e-8 — cosine unaffected ✓. **TRITON gotchas to keep in mind**: - No `tl.sigmoid`? tl.sigmoid exists ✓. softplus: I'll use sigmoid(-x) τ exact-ish ref: decay = exp(-softplus(x)): f32: softplus KB: log1p(exp(x)) — ref does F.softplus(x) → t = log1p(exp(x)); then g.exp(): exp(-t). Numerically: my sigmoid(-x) = 1/(1+exp(x)); for x very negative, exp(x) tiny → 1 ✓ both fine; ulps differ — ok. - silu: x·sigmoid(x) ✓. - tl.exp of (score − m): fp32 ✓. ref softmax: torch — no max subtraction in ref's naive... torch.softmax DOES subtract max ✓ same. - Rope cos/sin: compute in-kernel: `tl.cos`, `tl.sin` fp32 ✓. inv = 1/theta^(2i/dim): compute `tl.exp(log_theta * (-i/(dim/2)))`... i = pair index 0..31; ang = pos * inv — use f32; ref computes in f32 ✓. - bf16 conversions: `.to(tl.bfloat16)`. **Numerics for h_slot**: gate/up outputs: ref: silu(x@Wg) * (x@Wu) in fp32 (f32 x f32) then h @ Wd f32. Mine: h_slot stored bf16 (to halve down-stage x reads — 2KB, pointless) — store FP32 h_slot (9,1024 fp32 = 36 KB scratch, down reads trivial) → keep f32 for max fidelity ✓. **The ROUT logits 'sum partial' redundancy** per GU item: 8×64 loads + 64 exps + top8 (8×64 scan) ~ negligible. **Which scratch as fp32 vs bf16**: x buffers f32; gemv x-inputs f32 (x is in L2; read volume tiny) ✓ accumulate f32 ✓ out of gemvs f32 (REC/o stages read f32 ✓). The repack happens once: python records offsets per tensor while packing flat; offsets baked as... they depend on tensor ORDER only — deterministic! I can FIX the layout arithmetically (computed by same python that writes the kernel's stage descriptors — constexpr per stage!!) — YES: my repack = deterministic concatenation with per-tensor sizes known from cfg → offsets = python constants → passed to kernel as constexpr?? they're python ints at trace time → bake into code as immediates ✓✓✓ NO table, NO inttoptr needed for weights!! Just `wbase + OFF_q_0` etc. BEAUTIFUL. Then kernel args = wbase (uint8 ptr) + scratch ptr + counter ptr + explicit state/input ptrs (~25 args) + scalar dims (L0, L, seed flags, pp flags, nchunks, ctr_base, in_h... ). Clean! ALTERNATIVE: since layout is deterministic given cfg, write a python helper used AT TRACE TIME: Triton traces the jit function with python closure access to GLOBAL variables!! @triton.jit functions can reference GLOBAL python vars at trace time if they're tl.constexpr-like (ints) — globals get captured as constants when annotated... plain python ints in globals referenced inside jit → Triton treats as constexpr automatically ✓ (triton resolves global refs at compile; ints become constants). So: module-level `_LAYOUT` dict of ints set by setup BEFORE first compile; kernel reads `_LAYOUT["q_w0"]` — WAIT non-hashable names: access via `_LAYOUT` global dict: Triton supports dict subscript of constexpr at trace time?? It evaluates... dict access on a global python dict with constant key — I believe Triton's tracer CAN constant-fold `_LAYOUT["key"]` — it treats _LAYOUT as a constexpr global and subscript... risky. SAFER: generate explicit module-level int CONSTANTS: setup does `globals()["OFF_Q_W_0"] = 12345` then kernel references OFF_Q_W_0 (plain int global → constexpr ✓ definitely supported). ~150 module-global ints — ugly but robust ✓ and ZERO codegen ambiguity (static source, constants from layout). E.g.: KDA_QKVGO_OFF(i) = KDA_BASE + i * KDA_BLK_STRIDE ... where KDA_BLK_STRIDE = KDA_ATTN_SIZE + MOE_SIZE etc. — arithmetic on python ints/globals — in-kernel constant folding ✓✓. DESIGN THE FLAT LAYOUT with per-tensor-type segment order: seg KDA_ATTN (per block): q_w, q_s, q_z, k_w, ..., o_w, o_s, o_z, beta_w, conv_w, attn_norm, moe_norm ... wait norms are per BLOCK not per attn — keep attn_norm/moe_norm in block segment. seg MOE (per block): router_w, gate_w/s/z, up_w/s/z, down_w/s/z, sg_w/s/z, su_w/s/z, sd_w/s/z MLA block: attn seg + moe seg. With all sizes constexpr-known, offset(blk, tensor) = formula. OK! - MLA: c_kv cold input ptr, k_rope cold input ptr (or big bufs — big bufs are SEPARATE allocations per state chain — pass kvbig_ptr, krbig_ptr as args (per-chain changing → args ✓), L/L0/seed scalars. - counters ptr + ctr_base scalar. - in_h ptr (bf16), out ptr. - L0 (att src rows), seed flag, pp flags (3 bits)... pass as int32 scalars.
