Wait, "wbufs alternate src/dst per step" — src/dst selection = pp flag: kernel: src = wbuf_base + (pp? slot1 : slot0)*..., dst = other ✓ (and cold: src = input tensor ptr instead ✓). One more: MLAcache big buffers: allocate ONCE per chain (cold step) — python allocates, returns in dict; per-step arg ptrs ✓. **Now write the kernel as ONE triton.jit with stages unrolled via python helpers.** I'll write helper jit functions (inlined by Triton): Common patterns: ```python @triton.jit def _wait(ctr, base_pos, target): one = tl.arange(0, 1) c = tl.sum(tl.atomic_add(ctr + base_pos + one, 0, sem="acquire", scope="gpu")) while c < target: c = tl.sum(tl.atomic_add(ctr + base_pos + one, 0, sem="acquire", scope="gpu")) tl.debug_barrier() @triton.jit def _bump(ctr, base_pos, n): tl.debug_barrier() tl.atomic_add(ctr + base_pos + tl.arange(0, 1), n, sem="release", scope="gpu") ``` Hmm, wait — subtle: `_wait` spin conditions: fine. GEMV int4 n-sliced (the workhorse), with x prenorm optional and optional acc-mode (write vs atomic add?): Variants needed: (a) plain: y[n0:] = Σ (dequant) ⊗ xn f32 (QKVG q/k/v/g writes raw f32; OPROJ writes x+attn (reads y = x ptr, adds); ROUT-bf16?? router is bf16 matmul: separate special (small). (b) DOWN: atomic adds w_out (weighted) into B — atomic_add f32 ✓. (c) GU: TWO weights (gate+up) fused → silu product → h_slot. (d) ABS (contraction over N-dim — different sweep). (e) AOUT: K=512 contraction over IN — plain (a) shape with different K. Norm handling: gemv items need xn = x·rstd·wnorm — computations inside the k-loop per chunk: load x chunk f32, load wnorm chunk, multiply by rstd (scalar computed pre-loop by... rstd needs FULL ssq — a pre-pass: per item, loop over K just for ssq: 2304 f32 = 9 KB / item — every GEMV item reads x twice (ssq pass + main pass). x in L2 ✓ cheap. Alternative: precompute rstd ONCE in a stage — earlier stages... SMALL stage = extra hop; skip: redundant per-item ssq is FINE (μs-scale). Actually WAIT — efficiency of the ssq pre-pass per item: for QKVG stage: 68 items × 2.3 K elems = 157 K elem-reads extra ≈ 630 KB L2 — nothing ✓. beta item: does bf16 matmul 2304→32: x from XA f32 (cast bf16? ref: beta_proj(x) — nn.Linear on normed bf16 x!! x here is _rmsnorm → BF16 before beta_proj... ref KDA.step: beta = sigmoid(self.beta_proj(x)) where x = the RMSNormed bf16 tensor (Block.step: attn.step(_rmsnorm(x, attn_norm), st) → normed is BF16). So q/k/v/g GEMVs in ref see BF16-ROUNDED normed x!! My GEMVs use f32 x + f32 norm (no bf16 rounding of xn) — slightly MORE accurate but DIFFERENT from ref by bf16 rounding of xn (relative 2^-9 ~ 0.2% per element — accumulated in dot products over 2304: error concentrates ~0.2%/√2304... wait no: rounding errors on x are independent random ±2e-3·|x|; dot with w (2304 terms): relative error of output ≈ sqrt(Σ(δx·w)²)/|y| ≈ 2e-3 × (random walk) ~ 2e-3 relative. Cosine tolerance 0.98 → fine (these perturbations are ~1e-3 cosine distance). BUT — the KDA S-update sensitivity: g = -softplus(g_proj out) — g_proj sees same-perturbed input — fine. And the ROUTER boundary: logits differ ~2e-3 relative — softmax probs shift ~0.2% — expert WEIGHTS shift 0.2%, expert SET flips only if 8th/9th probs within 0.4% — rare; when it flips, output changes materially (different expert) → cosine dip POSSIBLE on rare steps. HOW RARE: probs spread over 64 experts ~ random; gap between 8th and 9th largest ~ O(1/64²)-ish of range... With w-probs ~ softmax of random logits (std ~0.02·√2304·?? actually router weights std 0.02 · x normed ~N(0,1) → logits std ≈ 0.02·48 = 0.96!! big → softmax sharp → top8 gaps meaningful → flips rare. Hmm but exactly matching ref ranking requires ~exact logits. Ref: x bf16-rounded → matmul fp32-accum → bf16 round of logits. Mine: f32 x → fp32 accum → (I round logits to bf16 before softmax ✓). The INPUT rounding diff: xn bf16 vs f32 — rounding δxn ±2e-3 → logits δ ≈ Σ δxn·w ~ 2e-3·||w||₂·?/... ≈ 2e-3·(0.02·√2304)/√?? ~ δlogit ~ 2e-3·0.02·√2304 ≈ 2e-3·1 ≈ 0.002 vs logit std 0.96 → 0.2% perturbation — SAFE-ish (as computed). DO IT for all GEMVs: xn rounded to bf16 then f32 — replicates ref input exactly. Similarly REC inputs: ref conv outputs bf16 (·to(val.dtype)), then q/k/v .float() — replicate: conv out → bf16 → f32 ✓. g: ref g_proj out bf16 → .float() softplus ✓ round g raw bf16. beta: sigmoid(bf16 matmul out... beta_proj(x): x bf16 normed ✓ out bf16 → sigmoid f32 ✓. o feed to o_proj: (S*q).sum → o f32 → .to(bf16) → o_proj sees bf16 ✓ round o_raw bf16. MLA: q = q_proj(x) → bf16; view; q_nope f32... roundings: q bf16 ✓, t q_rope kept bf16→rope f32?? ref: q_rope = q[:, nope:] (bf16), then _apply_rope: xf=x.float(), ... .to(x.dtype) → roped q_rope BF16 then .float() for scores ✓ replicate rounding. kv_a out bf16 → c_kv bf16 (cached) ✓ I cache c_kv as BF16 in big buffer (ref stores bf16 in cache ✓ SAME). my scores... absorbed: q_abs = q_nope @ W_k — ref computes k_nope = W_b applied to CACHED BF16 latents (f32 result) — absorb exact. kvb = kv_b(c_kv): input c_kv BF16 → my absorb uses q_nope (f32 from bf16 q ✓ round) × W — one-sided absorb: EXACT same product sets, different summation (over c then... ref: scores_l = Σ_d qn[d]·(Σ_c W[c,hd]·c_l[c]) vs mine Σ_c (Σ_d qn[d]W[c,hd])·c_l[c] — same terms, different order — fp32 tolerance ✓. softmax p: ref f32, o = pᵀV f32 → .to(bf16) → o_proj ✓ my pv path f32 → round o_pre bf16 ✓. MoE: _ffn: silu(x.float() @ Wg_f32)...: ref MoE x = normed bf16; experts: x.float() @ wbf.float() F32 (QuantExperts dequant full then fp32 matmul!). silu(g)*u f32 → h f32; then h @ Wd f32; accumulate out fp32 with w_j f32 → .to(bf16) at end ✓ replicate: GU: xn rounded bf16→f32 (quantlinear... wait MoE._ffn applies to x = the NORMED but UNROUNDED?? MoE.step(x): x = _rmsnorm(h) → BF16 ✓ (Block.step wraps normed). So xn bf16 ✓ same rounding pattern. Enough analysis — spine check on ATTENTION split: nchunks = cdiv(L_total, 1024) where L_total = L0+1 (with new token) — wait actually L passed to scores = current cache length AFTER append = L0+1 ✓ (ref: appends THEN computes kvb over all L+1 rows... scores over L+1 positions ✓ pos for rope = L0 (0-indexed position of new token = old length) ✓. COMBINE: standard flash combine with per-chunk partial (m_i, l_i, pv_i·)... DETAIL: within ATTN item I run online over its rows: m_i = running max, l_i = Σ exp(s − m_i), pv_i = Σ exp(s−m_i)·c. Store raw (m_i, l_i, pv_i) NOT normalized. COMB: m* = max_i m_i; l* = Σ l_i·exp(m_i−m*); pv* = Σ pv_i·exp(m_i−m*); o... AOUT uses pv*/l* as p? p_l = exp(s−m*)/l* → pv_norm[h,c] = pv*[h,c]/l* ✓ THEN AOUT GEMV contraction over c with W_v ✓. ref p normalized softmax ✓ same. EDGE: l* could be ~L×small — fp32 fine. exp of scores−m*: scores scale = (qn·kn + qr·kr)/√192: |q|~... q,k ~ N(0,~0.1²·2304)=±... whatever, fp32 handles. ATTENTION item (h, chunk): sub-loop over rows in BR=32/64: loads c_tile (BR, 512) bf16 from src (big or cold tensor — WAIT: addressing split: rows < L0 from SRC_A (big or cold!), rows == L0 from big — with cold: SRC_A = cold tensor; rows [0, L0) ON cold-tensor + row L0 = big[L0]... but on cold step, new row written to big[L0] ✓. Chunks align to 1024; the LAST chunk includes row L0 (the new one). In-kernel: if row >= L0: read from big else srcA — per-row predicate: rows within a sub-tile BR: uniform except the boundary tile IF L0 % BR != 0 — implement per-TILE branch: tile fully < L0 → srcA; tile fully ≥ L0 → big; else masked mix — only ONE tile ever mixes; handle with tl.where on pointer... simpler: make chunking such that row L0 alone in its own sub-iteration: rows loop 0..L0 in srcA-loop; then SEPARATELY handle "row L0" (the new token) as a tail singleton. Implement: item (h, chunk) covers [c0, c1) over the FIRST L0 rows (srcA only); the singleton new-row contribution added by... EVERY item needs it?? NO — assign new-row handling to chunk 0 (or make nchunks over L0+1 rows with the LAST item handling [c0, min(c1, L0)) from srcA + row L0 from big if within range ✓ per-item scalar condition ✓ clean: item range [c0, c1) over L0+1 rows; sub-tiles: while r < c1: if r < L0 && tile end ≤ L0: srcA; elif r >= L0: big; else: split-iterate: do [r, L0) srcA then [L0, ...) big — code handles via inner if on row range... Triton: two sub-branches executing loads with masks and separate pointers — doable with tl.where on POINTER?? Pointers are per-element... `tl.load(tl.where(cond, ptrA + off, ptrB + off))` — pointer where — allowed? tl.where on pointers: uncertain!! Alternative: LOAD with mask from srcA for rows< L0 and separately masked-load from big for rows ≥ L0, SUM contributions... ugly but works: two loads per tile (second masked-off almost always → wasted instructions but branch predication... masked loads with all-false mask are cheap-ish (still issue?). Hmm — CLEANEST: iterate loop over row-tiles where each item handles EXACTLY the boundary case separately with scalar branch: `if tile_end <= L0: full tile from srcA elif tile_start >= L0: full from big else: # boundary tile: masked load from srcA (rows < L0) + singleton handling from big`. The boundary tile occurs once per launch per (h — the item covering L0) — negligible. Implement with masked loads inside the else ✓ (scalar branch, uniform) ✓ GOOD. Same split addressing applies to the SEED-COPY items (read srcA rows → write big) — but copy only runs on cold (seed=1) and reads srcA=cold... copy item: rows [c0, c1) ⊆ [0, L0) read cold tensor → write big ✓ simple (uniform: L0 boundary handled by masks on row range). ABS absorb: reads q_raw (f32 4096+...), weights; writes q_abs. APPEND item (in ABS stage): reads kv_a_raw (576 f32) → rope k part → write big[L0] rows: c_kv (512 bf16), k_rope roped (64 bf16) ✓ + rope q_rope for all heads?? — q_rope rope = 32 heads × 64; fold into append item? 2048 elems: fine single item, or split into ABS items via chunk-0-per-head rule ✓ DO fold: ABS item (h, cchunk==0) EXTRA: rope q_rope[h] (load 64 raw q from q_raw[nope + h*192 + ...]... layout: q_raw per head: [nope 128 | rope 64] (ref: q.view(H, 192); q_rope = q[:, 128:]) ✓ rope h's 64 vals — angle: pos=L0, pair i: freq=pos·theta^(-2i/64) ✓ write q_rope_roped f32 scratch (32,64)... store bf16-rounded-then-f32 (ref rounds roped to bf16 before scores ✓). ATTENTION then uses q_rope_roped scratch (f32) + k_rope big bf16 rows ✓ scores rope part = Σ q_rope[h,i]·kr[l,i] ✓. Now, WARN — one more consistency check: ref attention k_nope for the CURRENT token comes from kvb = kv_b(NEW c_kv row TOO) — my scores use big[L0] row = the new token's c_kv ✓ included in ATTN range ✓✓. And v path: pv includes new row ✓ (ATTN accumulates all rows 0..L0) ✓. **Kernel arg list** (concrete): wq_u8 ptr (int4 flat region), pf32 ptr (f32 flat region: scales f32, zeros f32, beta, conv_w f32, norms, router, expert scales...), scr ptr (f32 scratch), winscr ptr (window ping-pong f32 region... wait — decide f32 for windows (3L×2×3 × 12288 = 663552 f32 ≈ 2.7 MB ✓)), attnpart ptr (f32 attention partials — part of scr ✓), c_kv_in ptr, k_rope_in ptr (cold tensors OR big), kvbig ptr, krbig ptr, in_h ptr, out_h ptr, ctr ptr, scalars: ctr_base, L0, nchunks, cold_cache (0/1), cold_win (0/1), win_pp (0/1), NCTA constexpr, BN etc constexpr. S tensors: s0_ptr, s1_ptr, s2_ptr (in-place) ✓ 3 args. ≈ 22 pointers + 8 scalars ✓✓. KDA windows conversion detail — REC reads raw q/k/v f32... wait NO — ref window stores BF16-rounded raw projections: window row vals must be bf16(q_raw). My REC: reads q_raw f32 scratch, casts bf16→f32 ✓ (for conv input row j=3 AND for writing dst[2] = that value ✓ same rounding) ✓✓ consistent. Initial EXT stage: casts in_h bf16 → XA f32 (raw values, no rounding — x ITSELF stays bf16-exact: in_h bf16 → f32 cast exact ✓). Norm rounding applied per-consumption ✓. WAIT — also block-input x for blocks>0: XB f32 holds h (x + attn + moe) f32 — ref's h is BF16 (each block returns bf16!!) → my internal h f32 is MORE precise; next block's norm then GEMV sees f32-x vs ref's bf16-x — input to next-block norms differ by bf16 rounding of h (2e-3 rel) — cosine still ≥ 0.98 easily? These diffs COMPOUND across 4 blocks but each is ~1e-3 cosine → total ~4e-3 ✓ well within 0.02 budget. HOWEVER to be extra safe at LOW cost: round xbuf to bf16 at WRITE time (OPROJ writes bf16-rounded f32 ✓ trivial) — replicate ref exactly-ish. DO IT: OPROJ writes round_bf16(x + attn_out) into XB; DOWN atomics ADD to that — DOWN adds w_j·downout f32: ref adds bf16 moe-out to bf16 h... ref: h + moe.step(...) → bf16 tensor + bf16 tensor → bf16: rounding of (h + moeout) to bf16 ✓ replicate: DOWN's final add must produce bf16-rounded (h + moe): with ATOMIC adds, each slot adds its chunk sequentially-ish → can't round incrementally exactly... approximation: DOWN atomic adds f32 into XB (starting from bf16(h)) — result ≈ h + Σ unrounded — then next block rounds on ITS norm multiply... deviation ~2e-3✓. To reduce: single-phase accumulation means one rounding point lost — whatever, tolerance fine. Actually — cheap fix: DOWN writes PER-SLOT partials to scratch (9,2304) f32; FINAL-of-block tiny stage sums with ORDER matching ref (slot 0..7 then shared) and bf16-rounds → next x — that adds a stage per block (meh, +4 stages = +4 hops ~ maybe 4-8 µs). Decide AFTER first perf data: start WITHOUT extra rounding (f32 accumulate), measure cosine; if ≥ 0.99 everywhere keep it. Actually you know what — I realize the END-of-block accumulation can stay atomic + I'll round at the START of the CONSUMING stage: next block's QKVG items round xn to bf16 anyway (norm rounding ✓ replicates ref since h feeds norm (norm itself bf16-rounds output ✓) AND h feeds the RESIDUAL: block1's x = ref bf16 h vs my f32 h — residual adds my f32 h — one-half ulp off... FINE. Moving on!! **Sizes for strides (constexpr via python globals → set before import-time compile... kernel compiled on first step (setup computes layout, sets globals, then launches).** Fine. Item-count summary per stage type (BN=256 GEMV): - QKVG: 4×16 + 4 = 68 - REC: 128 - OPROJ: 9 - ROUT: 8 - GU: 36 - DOWN: 81 - QKVA: 24 + 3 = 27 (kv_a: 576/256→3 masked) - ABS: 128 + 1 + seed(2×nchunks... seed items = ceil(L0/512): 16384→32) - ATTN: 32×nc (2048→64; 16384→512) - COMB: 32 - AOUT: 128 (32h × 4 dchunk) - EXT: 2, FINAL: 2 ITEMS total at 16k ctx ≈ 3×(68+128+9+8+36+81) + (27+128+1+32+512+32+128+9+8+36+81) + 4 ≈ 990 + 994 + 4 ≈ ~2000 items; ~2000 bumps... aggregated per-CTA-per-stage: 29 stages × 188 = 5452 atomic adds ✓ trivial. Now — the item→work decomposition per CTA: `for it in range(pid, NITEMS, NCTA)` per stage ✓ static bounds (except ATTN: runtime bound 32·nchunks ✓). **Let me now also decide tile params**: - GEMV BN=256, BK=128 (BK = group = 128 → EXACTLY one group per iteration! scales/zeros (1,BN) per iter ✓ clean). Tuning later w/ spike C results. - num_warps: 8; NCTA 188 or 376 (2/SM — if regs allow: kernel is fat (many stages) but per-stage register use bounded by max tile: GEMV tile (128,256) f32 = 128 KB?? WAIT — tl.sum((BK, BN) block * ...) creates a (BK, BN) f32 REGISTER TILE = 128×256×4 = 131 KB per CTA?! That CANNOT fit registers (64K regs × 4B = 256 KB/SM; 1 CTA/SM → 131 KB = 32K regs = AT LIMIT (32768 regs — Triton will spill unless it fuses the reduction!!). REDESIGN GEMV inner: per ITEM, loop k in BK=128 chunks: process sub-chunks: for a (BK,BN) iteration, register tile = BK×BN — to keep tiles ≤ 8K f32: e.g., BK=32, BN=256 → 8K f32 = 32 KB regs/CTA (128 regs... hmm 8192 f32 = 8192 regs over 256 threads (8 warps) = 32 regs/thread ✓ plus double-buffer...). Per-k iter: load (BK=32 rows of packed? BK=32 unpacked ⇔ 16 packed rows × 256 cols bytes = 4 KB). Scales per BK=32: group = 128 → scale changes every 4 iterations: keep sv/zv registers across the 4 inner... restructure: outer loop over GROUPS (g), inner 4 iterations of 32: load s/z once per group ✓; apply zero-sum trick per ITER or per GROUP: y += s_g·Σ... BEST: accumulate dot per group in a (BN,) acc via Σ over its 4 sub-iters using the wd·xn FMA... per sub-iter: load w (32,BN)... reduction tl.sum(axis=0) of (32,BN)·x → (BN,) — Triton reduces in registers (tree) — cost fine; register tile 32×256 f32 = 8192 = 32/thread ✓. Alternatively use tl.dot? GEMV → tl.dot with M=1 pads to 16 — wasteful. Keep FMA+sum. Actually there's a subtlety: tl.sum((BK,BN) * x[:, None], axis=0): the multiply materializes (BK,BN) product — same tile size — then reduce. OK. Per-CTA compute per (32,256) tile: unpack 1024 bytes... per-iter arithmetic ~ 3 ops/element × 8192 = 25K ops / 256 threads ≈ 96 ops/thread per iter — plenty of ILP ✓ should stream well. But num CTAs/items balance: K-loop iters = 2304/32 = 72 per item ✓ fine. Hmm — actually the CLASSIC fast int4 GEMV pattern assigns per WARP one n-column-strip and threads sweep k with stride — Triton abstracts that; trust Triton's vectorizer: the load w + (kp... with (16 packed, 256) u8 tile: elements per row 256 → threads (256) → each thread 1 byte?! NO VECTORIZATION ⇒ slow!! Triton picks layout: for a (16, 256) u8 tile with 256 threads: sizePerThread could be [1,1]... Triton chooses based on contiguity: along N contiguous 256 bytes → it can vectorize ≤ 16 B per thread given ≥16 threads per row... it'll coalesce but maybe narrow (u8 loads = 1 byte/thread → 256 threads serve 2 rows?? poor). FIX: make the tile load (packed rows, BN bytes) with EXPLICIT vectorization by tiling along... I can shape indices so each thread gets ≥ 4-16 contiguous bytes: use tile (PACKED=8 rows, BN=512): 4096 elems / 256 threads = 16 B/thread ✓ → 16-byte vectorized loads ✓✓. so shapes: PACKED_ROWS × BN with product ≥ 4096 (8 warps) and BN a multiple of 256... e.g., GEMV tile: (BK/2=64) × BN=256 → 16384 bytes / 256 thr = 64 bytes/thread = 4×16B vectors ✓✓✓ EXCELLENT — that's per GROUP: 64 packed rows = BK=128 k-elems ✓ group ALIGNED. So per k-group iter: tile (64, 256) u8 = 16 KB, unpacked f32 tile (128, 256) = 32K f32 regs — TOO BIG again!! Conflict: big load tiles for vectorization vs register footprint for compute. RESOLUTION: load tile as u8 (16 KB regs = 16 bytes/thread ✓ fine), then PROCESS IT IN SUB-SLICES: Triton doesn't easily subslice registers... BUT I can do the k-loop at BK=32 granularity for COMPUTE with SEPARATE loads: loads are what needs vectorization: (16 packed, 256) = 4 KB = 16 B/thread ✓✓ (16×256 = 4096 u8 over 256 threads = 16 each). Register f32 tile for compute (32, 256) = 32 regs/thread ✓✓. Win-win: BK=32 (16 packed rows) × BN=256: per-iter: 16B vector loads ✓ f32 tile 8192 ✓ 18 groups × 4 = 72 iters. Scales: per iter load (1,256) s/z rows?? every 4th iter (group aligned if BK... 32 does not divide 128 evenly?? 128/32 = 4 ✓ but WHICH group: iter k0/128 — load s/z ONLY when k0%128==0 via scalar if — branch inside loop... just LOAD every iter (256×2×2 = 1 KB loads/iter — L1-cached, cheap) ✓ simpler. - REC: block (128 dk, 32 dv) f32 = 4096 f32 = 16 regs/thread ✓ num_warps 8. - ATTN: subtile (BR=32, 512) bf16 → f32 (32,512) = 16384 f32 = 64 regs/thread at 256 thr — heavy-ish; BR=16 → 8192=32 ✓. Per-row-pair compute: scores = tl.sum(c*qa, 1): qa broadcast (512,) → product tile (BR,512) ✓. Per (h, chunk=1024 rows): 64 BR-iters (BR=16)... loads 16×512×2 = 16 KB bf16 ✓ 16B vec ✓. - GU: dual GEMV BK=32 × BN=256 ×2 loads ✓. - DOWN: plain GEMV + atomic epilogue. - ABS: (64 packed, 128 d) u8 → (128 c?? wait — ABS c-chunk=128 rows of unpacked = 64 packed × d=128: u8 tile 8192 = 32 B/thread ✓; f32 compute tile (128 c?, 128 d)= 16384 f32 = 64 regs/thread — hmm. Sub-split: process in 32-c slices (16 packed × 128 = 2048 u8 = 8 B/thread — vectorization 8B ok-ish; f32 (32,128)=4096=16regs ✓✓ do c-subloop 4×. Actually simpler: make ABS item = (h, c-chunk of 32) → 16 items/head → 512 items: contraction per item: (32 c, 128 d): q_abs[h, 32c] = Σ_d — load per item: 16 packed rows × 128 cols = 2 KB + qn[h] (128 f32) → tl.sum(wd2 (32,128) · qn[None], axis=1) → 32 outs ✓ f32 tile 4096 ✓ 512 items ✓ granular ✓✓ - COMB: per head reads ≤17 partials × 514 f32 ✓ trivial. - AOUT: like plain GEMV K=512, BN=... N-slice 128 per head: items (h, BN=128/dchunk): 1 item covers (K=512,BN=128): per group 128 → 4 groups: (64 packed × BN=128) u8 = 8 KB ✓ f32 (128,128) = 64 regs/thread... subloop BK=32: (16,128)=2 KB u8 = 8B/thread ✓ f32 4096 ✓ iters 16 ✓ - OPROJ/plain GEMVs: as above ✓. Let me now also sanity-check DRAM traffic of ATTN chunk size: item (h, 1024 rows): reads 1024×576×2 = 1.18 MB ✓; 32·nc items. ✓ **Now the host side.** Repack (once per state_dict load): I'll compute the layout map in python (dict name→(offset_bytes, kind) for the flat buffer), allocate flat u8 buffer = sum of sizes; copy each tensor: int4 w_q as-is (u8), scales/zeros f32-cast+contiguous... IMPORTANT: scales/zeros f32 conversion doubles their bytes — fine. Conv_w, beta_w, norms, router_w: f32 copies. Then VIEW objects for per-tensor ptr math live in kernel-side formulas — implement via a plain dict LAYOUT: name → byte offset; kernel accesses via GLOBAL CONSTANTS: I'll set module-global `_L = {...}` and the kernel...AGAIN the dict-at-trace-time issue. Cleanest SOLUTION: compute offset via in-kernel formulas from a FEW named scalar args (each region size). Like: ``` # sizes (all constexpr globals set from python): # KDA_QW_BYTES = 2304//2*4096 etc. ``` Kernel needs offsets for ~30 distinct tensors... All derivable from a per-block template with ~15 template field offsets (same for each KDA block, block i base = i*KDA_BLK_BYTES; MLA block at fixed base). Template fields: q_w, q_s, q_z, k_w..., beta, conv, anorm, mnorm, router, gate/up/down w/s/z, s_gate/... ≈ 25 template offsets — constexpr GLOBALS (module-level ints) ✓ kernel uses them directly by NAME (constant-folded) ✓✓ and block bases multiplex by python-block-index (python-unrolled loops at trace time? — hmm the KERNEL source unrolls blocks via... let me NOT python-unroll; the kernel is written STRAIGHT-LINE explicitly: block0, block1, block2 KDA sections — but that's 3× duplicated code... Use in-jit python loop: `for blk in tl.static_range(3):` — Triton supports tl.static_range (unroll at trace... `tl.static_range` exists ✓ or plain `range` with python semantics: in @triton.jit, `for i in range(3)` with CONSTANT bounds → statically unrolled? Triton: range with constexpr bounds becomes... it stays an scf.for loop UNLESS bounds are constexpr and small? tl.static_range forces unroll ✓ use that). Inside: stage helpers parameterized by blk (a constexpr int per unrolled iteration) → offset expressions folded ✓✓ and Triton dedupes... code size 3×KDA+1×MLA ≈ fine. Hmm wait — will tl.static_range produce constexpr `blk`? YES (int per iteration as constexpr). THEN stage structure per block also written inline... the code for a KDA block = helper function `kda_block(blk, ...)` called 3× with different constexpr blk ✓ function params constexpr-propagated?? Triton jit helper functions: args of type constexpr if annotated/derived from static_range ✓ (they become python ints during trace? constexpr(blk)...) — Triton inlines jit-callees and constant-folds when args are tl.constexpr — with static_range the loop var is a constexpr int ✓ annotate helper param as `blk: tl.constexpr` ✓. MoE per block = helper `moe_stage(blk_base...)` ✓. This keeps source manageable: helpers: _wait, _bump, _gemv_int4 (parameterizable: x ptr/rounding mode, out mode (store / atomic), dual (gate/up), K, N, BN, BK, norm ptr or none, offset exprs), _rec_stage, _abs_stage, _attn_stage, _comb_stage, kda_block, mla_block, moe... Honestly this is getting long — but it's THE deliverable. Let me also keep a DEBUG eager path in solution.py (pure torch, mirrors baseline but smarter) behind a flag for correctness bisecting. Structure of solution.py: ```python import math, torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.nn.functional as F import triton, triton.language as tl # ---- config/contract (mirror reference) ---- Config, build_config, init_state, init_token (delegating? CAN'T import reference!! solution must NOT import reference (banned). init_state/init_token: check.py imports only `solution`+`reference`: uses reference.init_state for states; benchmark too. So solution only needs: Model + build_config(Config?) — benchmark.py does `ref.build_config`; check ref.build_config ✓ — solution needs Config? signature: solution.Model(cfg) — cfg built by reference.build_config — passes a reference.Config DATACLASS INSTANCE → my Model just reads attrs ✓ duck-typing ✓✓ NO need to re-declare Config!! (Cross-module dataclass instance just carries fields ✓.) # ---- module tree (replicated buffers/params for state_dict compat) ---- class QuantLinear(nn.Module)... (copy from reference: __init__ only; no forward needed) class QuantExperts... class KDA/MLA/MoE/Block — structure only. class Model(nn.Module): __init__ builds blocks; step → megakernel path. # ---- layout/repack ---- def _build_flat(self): compute offsets; allocate; copy. # ---- the kernel ---- @triton.jit def megakernel(...): stages... # ---- host step ---- def step(...): setup once; bind args; launch; return. ``` state_dict compat check details: reference state_dict keys e.g. "blocks.0.attn.q_proj.w_q" etc. My modules must register identical names AND DTYPES (load copies with dtype cast? load_state_dict with strict: copies with copy_ which CASTS — but shapes must match ✓; dtype conversion allowed by copy_ ✓✓ (it does `.copy_` which casts). But PARAMS need requires_grad? fine default. conv_w is nn.Parameter(torch.empty(3, H*Dk, short_conv, dtype)) ✓ same. attn_norm/moe_norm Parameters ✓ beta_proj.weight Linear ✓ router.weight Linear ✓. nn.Linear without bias: weight (out, in)!! — beta_proj.weight shape (32, 2304) (out,in) ✓ note x@W.T semantics in the GEMV: torch linear y = x @ Wᵀ: y_j = Σ_k x_k W[j,k] — weight[j, k]: row j output. My kernel: partial-item computes cols j from weight ROW-major (j,k) contiguous along k ✓ contraction along k contiguous ✓ just read rows. Router same ✓ (N=64 outputs... weight (64, 2304)). conv_w (3, C, 4): indexed [idx, c, j] ✓. init_state/init_token — do I need them in solution? check.py: `reference.init_state`, `reference.init_token` only; solution: only Model. benchmark: `ref.init_token`. So NO. But the "same interface" wording — provide thin copies anyway? Not needed; keep them OUT (they'd need generator semantics identical - pointless risk). Actually — problem statement says "must expose the same Model class and the same step interface". Just Model+step ✓. **Wait — one thing to verify**: check.py constructs `solution.Model(cfg)` where cfg = reference.build_config(...) → reference.Config with ALL fields ✓ duck-typed reads ✓. **Launch config**: grid=(NCTA,). num_warps=8 to start. NCTA: start 188; verify no-deadlock via occupancy: compiled kernel n_regs & shared: I can query AFTER first compile: `kern = megakernel.warmup(...)`?? Triton API: compiled_kernel = megakernel[(grid,)](args...) returns kernel handle with .n_regs/.metadata? via `triton.compile`... simpler access: after launch, `megakernel.device_caches`... ugh — simplest: launch once with NCTA=188; ALSO host-side compute occupancy via `torch.cuda` — not exposed. Use triton's `kernel._cache`... Actually there's: `compiled = triton.compile(src)` path gives .metadata with num_warps, shared; occupancy = min(regs_limit...) — I can estimate manually from printed n_regs/n_spills/shared (printed via TRITON_PRINT_AUTOTUNING? no). EASIEST EMPIRICAL: run the kernel with a watchdog timeout; if it deadlocks (hang), reduce NCTA. With 8 warps and ~64-128 regs/thread: regs/CTA = 256 thr × 128 = 32K = half of 64K → 2 CTA/SM feasible → 376 OK. I'll self-check at runtime: after first compile, fetch `kernel.n_regs` via the JITFunction cache (`megakernel.cache` — API varies by version: triton 3.x: `fn.device_caches[device][0]` dict of compiled... I'll introspect git-hackily with try/except, else default 188.) Hmm, safer engineering: make NCTA = 188 always (1/SM) but num_warps 8 → 8 warps of 64 possible per SM — memory parallelism might be lower than 2×8... test both, watch for hangs. **Barrier/counter details**: counters per launch: NCTA bumps per stage... consumer wait: `while read(counter) < items_total:` — with per-CTA aggregated bump of (count of items it did). Right. Memory visibility of STAGE writes — release/acquire scope gpu ✓ as analyzed. **Spin loops of CTAs sitting in LATE stages from kernel start**: they immediately wait on their stage's counter (uniform branch flow: code for their item's stage IS later in the program — they reach it only after executing EARLIER stages' waits too — every CTA executes ALL stages sequentially: stage loop structure per CTA: ``` for stage in STAGES: # unrolled _wait(deps...) work(items of stage) _bump ``` Wait for stage S executed BEFORE work of S; this means CTA sits idle only at the points data isn't ready ✓ but note a CTA with ZERO items in stage S still does _wait(S's deps) + _bump(0) — fine (tiny overhead, keeps chain tight). Alternatively CTAs with no work could skip... skip = fine too (bump 0). Keep uniform. **ORDERING subtlety — spin BEFORE work**: for ATTN with runtime-bound nchunks: items = range(pid, 32*nc, NCTA); consumers wait counter==32*nc (computed) ✓. **Seed copy items**: ABS stage extra items: n_copy = ceil(L0/CPY=512) when cold_cache else 0; ABS total items = 128 (absorb) + 1 (append) + n_copy(runtime!) — consumers (ATTN) wait target = 129 + n_copy... consumers only NEED absorb+append; copy items lag (big copies, 590 KB each) — they'd DELAY the stage counter → stall ATTN!!! FIX: TWO counters for ABS stage: counter_main (absorb+append: 129) waited by ATTN; counter_copy (n_copy) waited by... nobody in-launch; BUT the bump accounting chain: copy bumps counter_copy which NOBODY waits on ✓ and next launch's counters... counters are monotonic per launch-slot; unused-but-bumped slot just holds a value ✓ no one cares ✓✓. And next launch's stage slots use NEW base ✓ clean. WAIT — one more RACE: copy items write big[0:L0] while... RETURNED view big[:L+1] read by NEXT LAUNCH — fine. But THIS launch's APPEND item writes big[L0] (new row) — disjoint from copy rows ✓. **k_rope big write on warm steps**: ATTN reads k_rope rows [0, L0] from big_kr (written by prior launches ✓) + row L0... hmm wait warm step: L = L0(old)... let me define: at step entry, cache has L0 rows (context L.. semantics: init L0=ctx; each step appends → L grows). Set: L = L0 + 1 = total rows after append = scores length. pos = L0 (rope position of new token) ✓. Warm: srcA = big (rows [0, L0)), new row → big[L0] ✓ SAME indexing as cold (srcA = cold tensor) ✓ uniform code: all rows < L0 from srcA; row L0 (and beyond in later...) — WAIT warm steps read rows [0, L0) from big which INCLUDES rows written by earlier launches ✓ all in big ✓✓ single srcA ✓ NICE. **CTA-side: stage helper signatures** — passing pointers around: fine. **Numerics—recheck ROUT partial-sum equality**: ref router = bf16 linear → rounding of matmul out to bf16 (one round). Mine: 8 partial slots f32 → GU item sums (f32, diff order than one matmul) → round bf16 ✓ equivalent ~ulp ✓ top8 robust. **top8 extraction**: after computing p (64 f32, softmax), extract 8 largest DESC with tie → lowest index first: iterate 8×: scan 64: track best (p, -idx... select max with strictly-greater compare on prob PREFERS LOWER index on ties ✓ matches torch.topk STABLE behavior? torch.topk: for CUDA, ties order = lower index first generally... good enough (ties ~never). Then w = w/sum·2.446 ✓ store w_j (8 f32) + idx (8 int) to SCRATCH once per... EVERY GU/DOWN item recomputes (~200 items × (64 exp + 512 scan)) = trivial ✓✓ no scratch for routing needed beyond logits partials. Hmm — recomputing in EVERY GU/DOWN CTA-item: exp×64 ~ maybe 1000 cycles — fine. BUT — WAIT: GU item (slot j) needs expert INDEX of slot j — from recomputed top8 ✓; then addresses THAT expert's weights: flat offsets = moe_base + expert_idx * EX_BYTES ✓ runtime index ✓ fine (pointer arith with runtime int ✓). **MoE weight layouts**: gate (2304, 1024) per expert: w (1152, 1024) u8; s (18, 1024) f32; z (18, 1024) f32; contiguous per expert: [gate_w, gate_s, gate_z, up_w..., down_w/s/z] → stride EX_BYTES; shared expert region after 64 experts ✓. **OK — one more BIG decision**: the DOWN stage K=1024 GEMV: reads h_slot f32 (9,1024) per item (4 KB) ✓ fine. atomic adds: (BN=256) per item → 81 items × 256 = ~20 K atomics ✓ fine. And **ROUT stage** position: between OPROJ and GU ✓ (computes logits from XB... wait — X buffers: OPROJ writes UB = x+attn into "OTHER" buffer; ROUT/GU/DOWN read that OTHER buffer ✓ DOWN atomically adds INTO it ✓ NEXT block's QKVG reads it as x ✓✓. EXT: block0's QU... block0 x = XA after EXT ✓. FINAL: cast B → out bf16: read correct buffer (block3's other... parity: blk0: x=XA out→XB; blk1: x=XB out→XA; blk2: XA→XB; blk3: XB→XA!?? alternation: out buffer of blk b = (b%2? XA: XB): blk0→XB, blk1→XA, blk2→XB, blk3→XA. FINAL reads XA ✓ (baked constant parity ✓). **Compile-time lengths**: nchunks runtime; L0 runtime; cold flags runtime; pp runtime; NCTA constexpr. Items-per-stage: all constexpr except ATTN(32·nc runtime) and ABS copy (runtime). Grid: (NCTA,). **I think the design is complete enough to start writing.** First version priorities: CORRECTNESS > perf; perf knobs: BN/BK/warps/NCTA tunable. One more perf thought — **stage-hop latency**: 29 stages/chains... chain latency ≈ Σ max(stage critical path). With per-item spin waits the HOP = (last item finish → counter visible → consumer first load). Each hop maybe ~1-2 µs (atomic RTT ~ 600ns×2 + pipeline drains)... ×29 ≈ 30-60 µs?? vs bandwidth floor 135 µs — SIGNIFICANT. Mitigations (v2): MERGE stages sharing no data dep... e.g., ROUT merged INTO GU as leading items with IN-STAGE fuzziness?? GU items need logits → ROUT items must complete first: within one stage, items ascending: ROUT items (idx 0..7) then GU items (8..): GU items spin on ROUT counter — DAG ✓ one counter, one wait ✓ saves a hop per MoE. Similarly: OPROJ+ROUT merge; QKVG+beta already merged; ABS+append merged; ATTN+COMB merge? COMB needs ALL atten items of its head — in-stage spin works: COMB items AFTER attn items in same stage ✓ saves hop. AOUT+OPROJ merge ✓ (OPROJ needs o_pre complete... yes spin). DOWN+next-QKVG merge — next block's QKVG needs DOWN complete ✓ in-stage. EXT+QKVG(0) merge ✓. FINAL merge into DOWN ✓. So a "STAGE" becomes a BLOCK: KDA block: stage A: [QKVG items][REC items] — REC spins QKVG sub-flag... hmm wait within ONE stage every item bumps ONE counter; need SUB-counters per sub-range: implement "stage" = ordered item list + MULTIPLE progress counters: each sub-range gets its own counter; consumers spin on the sub-counter of their dep-range. Equivalent to my current design BUT eliminating the BETWEEN-stage waits for independent items: e.g., in merged stage [QKVG, REC], a CTA whose next item is REC(h) spins only until QKVG counter full — SAME wait as before (data dep)... the SAVING is only when CTA has INDEPENDENT items to fill: e.g., merged [QKVG, REC, OPROJ]: no independence — chain. So merging doesn't help pure chains!! The REAL win: items of stage S+1 that DON'T depend on ALL of stage S: REC(h) depends only on q,k,v,g rows of head h + beta — finer-grained flags (per-head progress) → REC starts as q/k/v/g for head h land. v2 refinement — PROBABLY NOT NEEDED v1: realize barrierless chain-hop cost MIGHT be small: spin detect happens on the CTA that ALREADY has ALL its stage-S work done... the hop latency ≈ time for the LAST stage-S item (across CTAs) to publish + spin poll interval. It's a pipeline drain effect, unavoidable ~ the tail imbalance of each stage ≈ (max item time − min item time) + sync ~ 1-3 µs... unavoidable? With 188 CTAs and 2000 items ≈ 11 items/CTA — stats even out. Each hop ~ maybe 2-5 µs → 29 hops ≈ 60-150 µs?!? TOO MUCH. MUST reduce hops or overlap. Real overlap win vs my per-stage waits: current scheme stalls CTA at EVERY stage boundary even when its OWN next item could start... no — its next item genuinely needs the data (chain). BK-loop 72 iters × 4 KB = 295 KB per item @ per-CTA ~ 25 GB/s?? → 12 µs per item — and 68 items / 188 CTAs ≈ 0.36 items/CTA → stage wall ≈ 12 µs (one item) vs ideal 18.9 MB/1.8 TBps = 10.5 µs ✓ fine, stage tail ≈ small fraction... actually ~perfect: stage time ≈ max over CTAs of their item times; with ≤1 item/CTA → 12 µs ≈ traffic-limited ✓ GOOD. REC stage: 128 items over 188 CTAs: item = S block (128×32) r+w = 32 KB + q/k/v/g re-reads... ~ 6 µs?? S total 4 MB r+w → 2.2 µs ideal — item ≈ 32 KB × 2 / 25 GB/s ≈ 2.6 µs ✓ → stage ≈ 5 µs. OPROJ: 4.7 MB → 2.6 µs ideal; 9 items... only 9 CTAs busy!! 4.7MB/9/25GBps ≈ 21 µs!!! BAD: N-chunks 2304/256 = 9 items → 9 CTAs → 9×25 GB/s = 225 GB/s. The 25 GB/s/CTA is what ONE CTA can pull ALONE (latency-bound); with 36 items × 25 GB/s = 900 GB/s → 5.2 µs ✓ acceptable; better: SPLIT-K: OPROJ items = 9 n × SK=4 → 36 items + atomic adds f32 ✓ traffic same, parallelism ×4 → ~1.4 µs + but each split-K item reads x 4096... hmm x f32 16 KB × 36 reads = L2. ✓ DO: OPROJ BN=128, SK=2 → 36 items. DOWN item already reads h: reads g & u (8 KB) + elementwise ✓ trivial ✓✓ - DOWN: 81 items... 1024-K: per item 128 N × 512 packed = 64 KB — ok 81 items ✓ ~2.6 µs ✓ - QKVG: 68 items × 278 KB = 18.9 MB ✓ 68 items/188: stage = ceil? CTAs get ≤1 item: time ≈ 278 KB/25 GB/s ≈ 11 µs ≈ ideal 10.5 ✓ (40% of CTAs idle but they don't matter — bandwidth-limited total ≈ 68×278 KB / min(68×25 GB/s=1.7 TB/s, 1.8) → ~11 µs ✓ - ATTN: fine-grained ✓. MoE per block: - router: 64×2304 f32 = 589,824 - per expert: gate w (1152×1024)=1,179,648; s (18×1024×4)=73,728; z 73,728 → 1,327,104 B ×3 (gate/up/down) = 3,981,312 per expert! ×64 = 254,803,968 - shared: 3,981,312 MoE total per block ≈ 259.4 MB!! ×4 = 1.04 GB. OK (repack copy ~1-2 s once). Flat layout order (byte offsets, ALL align to 256 B): Region 0: EXT nothing. Per KDA block i in 0..2: [block region: attn (q_w,q_s,q_z,k_*,v_*,g_*,o_*), beta, conv, anorm, mnorm, moe segment (router, experts64×(g,u,d ×(w,s,z)), shared(g,u,d))] MLA block: [attn (q_w,s,z; kva_w,s,z; kvb_w,s,z; o_w,s,z), anorm, mnorm, moe segment] I'll compute these offsets in python (one function returning a dict-of-dicts) and mirror as in-kernel formulas using ~30 module-level int constants. Actually — re-think: rather than in-kernel formulas with 30 globals, I can write the offsets as KERNEL SCALAR ARGUMENTS computed host-side... 30 extra int64 args per launch — Triton handles scalar args fine; simpler to maintain and no trace-time global tricks. Args: off_i as int64/uint64 passed per launch (they're FIXED per model — same values each launch). Number ≈ 40 — acceptable?? CUDA kernel params limit 4096 B total: 40×8 = 320 + 22 ptrs×8 = 176 + scalars ≈ 550 B ✓ fine. BUT WAIT: Triton specializes scalar args: passing i64 args that DON'T change → "divisibility 16" specialization keys — stable, one compile ✓. Alternatively — simplest of all: bake offsets as PYTHON GLOBAL CONSTANTS referenced by name in the kernel source. Triton resolves global ints at compile ✓ (well-supported: like GROUP_SIZE... actually tl.constexpr globals — yes this works: `OFF_Q = 4718592` at module level, used in @triton.jit → captured as constant at compile). And if I re-run in the same process with a DIFFERENT model/offset set, stale-cache risk — but offsets depend only on cfg constants which are FIXED by problem.yaml shapes... n_experts could vary (64 fixed in shapes; build_config reads shape n_experts — check uses SHAPES[0] = 64) — fine, but a cache-key concern: Triton caches compiled kernels per (constexpr, arg-specialization); module globals are BAKED INTO SOURCE CONSTANTS — if I change globals and relaunch, Triton would REUSE the old compiled kernel!! DANGER... only if offsets changed — they don't per-process. Include a layout VERSION in an explicit constexpr arg to be safe. OK go. Actually you know what — simplest robust: EXPLICIT KERNEL ARGS for offsets. No globals, no staleness, no trace magic. ~35 int64 args. DECIDED: explicit args. Hmm, wait — one more idea in-between: a small constant "param" tensor in GLOBAL MEMORY that the kernel LOADS offsets from at start (each CTA loads ~40 int64 once — from L2, trivial). THEN args = pointers to [wq_u8, param]. Kernel loads offsets ONCE at kernel start into registers... but offsets as runtime VALUES can't be used for tl.constexpr things (they're only address math — runtime fine!). All my uses = address computation ✓ runtime OK!! Param tensor: (256,) int64; CTA reads param[i] scalar by scalar... 40 scalar loads at start ~ ns. Flexible (no recompile for layout tweaks), single source of truth (host builds tensor; kernel indexes by BAKED INDEX CONSTANTS in source — indices are static in source ✓ clean). DECIDED: param tensor + baked index constants. Total args ≈ 25-30. For POINTERS, `.to(other pointer type)` does a bitcast — I believe supported (it's how people cast). If not: allocate THREE separate buffers by dtype (u8 region, f32 region, bf16 region) — then separate base args (clean, definitely works). TWO flat buffers + param tensor ✓. - wq_u8, wf32, wpar (int64 256) - scr (f32 scratch) - win (f32 windows ping region: 3 layers × 2 pp × 3 × 3 × 4096 → 663552 f32 ≈ 2.65 MB) — windows stored f32 (with bf16-rounded values) ✓ - s0, s1, s2 (f32 KDA S, in-place) - ckvin, krin (bf16 cache source: cold input tensor or big) - kvbig, krbig (bf16 big) - inh (bf16 2304), outh (bf16 2304) - ctr (i32) scalars (i32): ctr_base, L0, nc (nchunks), cold_cache, cold_win, winpp, seed... merge cold flags: flags int bitmask: bit0 cold_win, bit1 cold_cache, winpp bit2. constexpr: NCTA... plus tuning constexprs (BN etc. baked as source-module constants? — different per stage; bake as literal numbers in source ✓). Total < 3 MB ✓ one alloc 4 MB f32. param tensor (int64) entries: per-tensor offsets: idx map (I'll give them symbolic names at host AND baked index ints in kernel): 0: KDA_QW (size 4718592), 1: KDA_QS (73728×4=? wait q scales: (18, 4096) f32 = 294912), 2: KDA_QZ... Per-KDA-block-relative template (same each KDA block): fields: qW,qS,qZ,kW,kS,kZ,vW,vS,vZ,gW,gS,gZ,oW,oS,oZ (15), beta, conv, anorm, mnorm, router, gW,gS,gZ,uW,uS,uZ,dW,dS,dZ,sgW,sgS,sgZ,suW,suS,suZ,sdW,sdS,sdZ — 19 + 19 = 38 (+ router=1 → I said router + 18 expert fields + ... recount: router(1), gate w/s/z (3), up (3), down (3), sgate (3), sup (3), sdown (3) = 19; attn-side: 15 + beta + conv + anorm + mnorm = 19. Triton conversion of whole tile to f32 = (128 unpacked... The join trick: lo/hi (64,128) f32 each → joined (128,128) f32 = 16384 f32 = 64 regs/thread ✗. Process per HALF group: k-block 64 → 32 packed rows × 128 = 4 KB u8 tile; unpacked (64,128) f32 = 8192 = 32/thread ✓ with join: lo(32,128)+hi → (64,128) ✓ 2 per group ✓ registers OK-ish (64 regs with temporaries... at 256 threads = 16K regs = within 64K/SM with 1... hmm n_regs budget: 2 CTA/SM needs ≤ 128 regs/thread. GEMV stage f32 tiles (64,128) unpacked wd (8192), plus x chunk, plus products — Triton fuses elementwise; estimate peak ~ 3 tiles ≈ 96 regs ✓ OK). Loads per sub-iter: 32×128 u8 = 4 KB = 16 B/thread ✓ vectorized ✓✓. Loop structure per item: ``` ns = n0 + arange(BN) acc = zeros(BN) # ssq pass for rstd (if NORMED): loop k chunks of 256: xx = load x; ssq += sum(xx*xx) → also load nothing else. (norm input = x raw f32) rstd = rsqrt(ssq/K + eps) for g in range(NG): # NG = K//128 (constexpr given K constexpr? K runtime... K is per-stage constexpr via python-called helper? K is a python int → constexpr ✓) sv = load s row (BN f32) at wf + soff + g*N + ns zv = load z row for kk in range(2): # 64 k each k0 = g*128 + kk*64 wp = load (32, BN) u8 lo = wp & 15 → ...join → (64, BN) f32... wait careful: 64 k-elems = 32 packed rows ✓ xv = load x[k0:k0+64] f32 if NORMED: xv = (xv*rstd? NO — ref norm: xf·rsqrt·wn → per element: xn = (x*rstd)*wn: load wn chunk → xv = ((xv*rstd)*wn) round bf16 → f32 (rstd scalar hoisted ✓ wn load per chunk) acc += sum((wd - zv)*sv*xv[:,None], 0) # wd (64,BN) epilogue: store/atomic ``` wait: zero-sum trick not applied — (wd - zv)*sv per element then × x — 3 flops/elem vs 2... negligible compute. FINE. Hmm, x re-loaded twice (ssq pass + main loop): ssq pass 2304 f32 = 9 KB; main 9 KB; from L2 ✓. NORMED variant needed for: QKVG q/k/v/g (attn_norm), beta (bf16 in/out...), ROUT (moe_norm), GU (moe_norm), QKVA (attn_norm). NON-normed: OPROJ (x = oraw bf16-rounded f32, no norm... ref o_proj input = bf16(o) — no norm ✓), DOWN (x=h f32 no norm ✓), ABS/AOUT ✓ EXT ✓. Rounding of xn: NORMED → bf16 round ✓; OPROJ/DOWN: inputs already rounded at write ✓ no re-round. - beta item in QKVG stage: y = xn @ betaWᵀ... betaW (32, 2304) f32 (out,in): item j-chunk: 8 outputs × full K: reads 8×2304×4 = 73 KB ✓ 4 items. Wait — ROUT stage items read X (xb) for ssq/norm — xb complete after OPROJ ✓. Order inside one stage — hmm I realize each "stage" in my unrolled code = a wait + work + bump on ITS OWN counter. It's simple & correct; hops as computed ≈ 29. Go. Now — START WRITING. Also write the EAGER fallback `_step_debug` mirroring reference math in torch for bisection (int4 unpack allowed - it's my own dequant for debugging; not the timed path). One more potential landmine: **Triton `tl.atomic_add` on fp32 tensor with mask** — supported ✓. **`tl.join`** — exists in triton 3.x ✓ (tl.join(a, b) stacks along new last dim → reshape interleaves ✓ verify: join (64, BN) pairs → (64, BN, 2); reshape (128... wait I need interleave along k (dim 0): lo/hi with shape (32, BN): join → (32, BN, 2)?? that's along LAST dim — wrong axis; want k-interleave: unpack rows r → k=2r (lo), k=2r+1 (hi) — interleave along dim 0: permute? tl.trans(tl.join(lo,hi), 0,2,1)?? shapes: join (32,BN) → (32,BN,2) → trans to (32,2,BN) → reshape (64,BN) ✓ with k = r*2 + j ✓ trans allowed in jit ✓ reshape ✓. OK: wd = tl.reshape(tl.trans(tl.join(lo, hi), 0, 2, 1), (64, BN)) — wait join(lo,hi): shape (32,BN,2) → trans(0,2,1) = (32,2,BN) → reshape (64,BN): element (r, j, n) → flat k = 2r+j ✓ ✓. tl.trans exists (tl.trans(x, *dims)) ✓. tl.permute too. OK. **Concern: `tl.trans` on big-ish tiles = register shuffle cost — fine. The spike is queued behind another GPU job. Meanwhile, I'll write the full solution — the design is fully worked out. Key architecture for solution.py:

  1. Module tree replicating reference (same buffer/param names for load_state_dict).
  2. One-time flat repack of all int4 weights + f32 dequant params into two flat buffers (fixed byte-layout arithmetic known to both host and kernel).
  3. Single persistent Triton megakernel: ~30 sequential stages covering EXT→(KDA ×3)→MLA→FINAL, each stage = wait on producer counter → process grid-strided work items → bump own counter (DAG-ordered, deadlock-free), with per-stage fused int4 dequant GEMVs, conv+recurrence, absorbed MLA flash-decode attention, MoE router/top8/expert GEMVs, norms, residuals.

@@ -0,0 +1,460 @@
+"""Kimi-Linear W4A16 hybrid decode — single-launch persistent megakernel.
+
+The entire per-token forward (3 KDA + 1 MLA attention layers, 4 MoE FFNs, all
+int4 dequant-GEMVs, short causal convs, KDA recurrent state update, MLA
+absorbed-latent flash-decode attention, MoE router + top-8 experts, RMSNorms,
+residuals, state/cache updates) is fused into ONE Triton kernel launch per
+step.
+
+Design notes:
+  * Every int4 weight is repacked once (after load_state_dict) into one flat
+    uint8 region; scales/zeros and the small bf16 weights into one flat fp32
+    region, at fixed offsets also written into a small int64 param tensor the
+    kernel reads. Weights are streamed exactly once per token — the int4
… +447 more lines

reasoning
That draft got convoluted — the split/dual-region offset bookkeeping is error-prone. Let me restart with a MUCH cleaner architecture for the layout: **Simplification: ONE flat uint8 buffer, single offset map, two views.** - Every tensor (int4 weights, scales/zeros as f32, small weights as f32) gets ONE absolute offset (bytes) in the flat buffer. - Offsets for f32 tensors are element-aligned (÷4 for f32 pointer math — kernel does `(off // 4)` — or better: the kernel treats bases as u8 pointers and casts: `ptr = (base + off).cast(pointer(f32), bitcast)`. If pointer bitcast in triton is clunky, use `.to(tl.pointer_type(tl.float32))`. Spike B tests exactly this. - If pointer bitcast doesn't work: keep TWO buffers (u8 one for int4, f32 one for floats), with two offset maps — but computed by ONE clean table-driven builder. Cleanest: a declarative TABLE — list of (template_field_id, size_in_bytes, region_kind, data_getter) — walked once host-side to assign offsets sequentially (aligned 256), and the field-ids/offsets written into the param tensor at FIXED indices. The kernel indexes the param tensor with the same fixed indices + does region arithmetic. Editorializing less: let me define everything via a single `layout_spec(cfg)` function returning: - `fields`: dict fid → (region, size) and a builder that assigns offsets and returns `{fid: offset}` plus total sizes. The kernel gets the offsets via the param tensor (indexed by fid — fids are compile-time constants in the source). Region arithmetic: `addr_u8(fid) = wq_u8 + par[fid]`; `addr_f32(fid) = wf32 + par[fid]//4` — par[] values are ABSOLUTE offsets (block-dependent ones stored per block: 4 blocks × 38... total fids = 3×38 + 33 = 147 → param (128+... just use 256-entry param; kernel indexes param[fid] — fid constants in source). That's clean! No strides/formulas: EVERY tensor gets its own param slot. Expert tensors: don't store 64 expert offsets in param — store expert-0 offset + stride and runtime-compute `par[fid_EXP0] + e*stride` where stride = par[P_EXSTRIDE] ✓. For the layout: me — wait, but with per-MoE duplicated fields and per-block duplicated fields, do I need to store all? Per block the SAME 38 fields at different addresses → 4 blocks × 38 = 152 param entries — fine, param tensor (192,). Kernel: fid_for(block, field) = block*38 + field for KDA blocks (0,1,2), and block 3 (MLA) has its own 33 fields → different fid offsets — python-side constants: kernel helper gets `tbase` = param index base for this block (constexpr per unrolled block index!) and field id (constexpr) → par index = tbase + field ✓ compile-time integer ✓✓. Sizes: KDA block fields (38): qW,qS,qZ,kW,kS,kZ,vW,vS,vZ,gW,gS,gZ,oW,oS,oZ (15), beta,conv,anorm 17... let me recount: attn norms & moe norm = 2, router =1: 15+1+1+2+1 = 20; gate/up/down s,z per kind: exgW(1)+exgS+exgZ+exuW+exuS+exuZ+exdW+exdS+exdZ = 9 (+shared 9) = 18 → total 38 ✓ (as constant: expert fields store expert-0 offset; P_EXSTRIDE_U8/P_EXSTRIDE_F32... two region strides — args/params. Region mixing: experts' w in u8 region but scales in f32 region → per-expert offset lives in DIFFERENT regions! `e×stride_bytes_in_u8region` for w vs `e×stride_elems_in_f32region` for s/z — strides DIFFER numerically (u8 stride bytes vs f32 stride... define: w addr = par[F_EXW] + e*EXB_W (bytes); s addr = wf32 + (par[F_EXS] + e*EXB_S//?) — store strides as: exb_w bytes, exb_sz ELEMENTS (f32 units) — two params ✓. And within-expert: gate/up/down w: F_EXW (gate w), F_EXUW = F_EXW + stride... store SEPARATE param entries for up/down expert-0 offsets (they already are separate fields! F_EXW, F_EXUW, F_EXDW each = expert-0 offset + e*exb_w) ✓ similarly s/z ✓. SIMPLER: expert stride = SAME for all kinds? Make per-expert layout: [gate_w (g_w), up_w (g_w), down_w (d_w)][gate_s, gate_z, up_s, up_z, d_s, d_z]... all in respective regions with stride exb_w = 2*ga+da, exb_s = 4*gs + 2*ds — hmm whatever, code computes. Alright — write it cleanly this time, as the FINAL solution.py structure: ```python def _layout(cfg): # returns spec: list of entries ``` Each entry: (par_index, region ('u8'|'f32'), size_bytes, getter_fn(blk_idx, module)) — build via walker over blocks. Getter for data copy. Then `_materialize(model)` allocates flat buffers, zeroes param (int64), walks entries: assign offsets sequentially per region, fill param[par_index] = offset (u8 → bytes; f32 → /4 elements), stash copy list for the actual data movement (device tensors → flat slices, D2D). Data movement happens via `.copy_` per tensor — ~300 copy calls — fine (once). A LOAD-HOOK: `self.register_load_state_dict_post_hook` to invalidate `_flat` so next step re-materializes. nn.Module has `register_load_state_dict_post_hook` (torch 2.x ✓). Then the KERNEL. Let me now write the final megakernel code with extreme care about Triton syntax details: 1. `tl.program_id(0)`, `tl.num_programs(0)`. 2. Wait/bump helpers with `tl.arange(0, 1)` masks. 3. GEMV helper — parameterized by offsets (runtime scalars), K (constexpr... K must be CONSTEXPR for loop unrolling? NO — runtime loop bounds FINE (scf.for). K runtime → loops runtime ✓. But specialization: runtime K forces masked tail handling: K is always multiples of 128 here → enforce with tl.debug... just assume (assert host-side). 4. atomic_add f32 supported ✓ (PT 3.6: atomic_add supports fp32/fp16/bf16/int). 5. bf16 conversion: `.to(tl.bfloat16)` ✓; round via x.to(bf16).to(f32). One BIG syntactic risk: tl.join + tl.trans for nibble interleave. Check semantics: tl.join(a, b): docs — "Join the given tensors in a new, minor dimension." (32,BN)+(32,BN) → (32,BN,2). I need k-interleave: (32,BN,2)... element (r, n, j) should map to k-index 2r+j → want final (64, BN) with row 2r+j = [lo/hi][r,n]... reshape (32*2*...): joining along LAST dim gives (r,n,j) memory order r-major, then n, then j — reshape (64, BN)?? (32,BN,2) → reshape to (32, BN*2) interleaves along n not k. Need perm: (32,BN,2) → perm 0,2,1 → (32,2,BN) → reshape (64,BN): row index r*2+j ✓ (2r+j: memory order r, then j, then n → row of output = r*2+j ✓ exactly what I want: even rows = j=0 = lo, odd = hi ✓✓ CORRECT (k=2r → lo ✓ matches pack: lo = even k). Use tl.permute(x, 0, 2, 1) — available? tl.permute exists (triton 3.x ✓) or tl.trans(x, (0,2,1)). 6. Two-k-per-byte alternative WITHOUT trans: process lo and hi SEPARATELY with their own x slices: acc += sum(lo*x_even) + sum(hi*x_odd): x_even = x[k0:k0+128:2]... strided loads/reshape of x: x block (64,BN): lo (32,BN) × x_e (32,1)... x reshaped (32, 2)?? x[k0 + 2*r + j]: load xv (64,) → reshape (32,2) → x_e = xv[:,0], x_o = xv[:,1] via tl.split! tl.split splits last dim of size 2 → two tensors ✓✓ tl.split(tl.reshape(xv, (32, 2))) → x_e (32,), x_o (32,) ✓ then acc += tl.sum(lo*sv...*(zv-broadcast)*x_e[:, None]? Careful with scales: per-element (u−z)·s·x where u=lo at k=2r: acc += Σ (lo − z)*s*x_e + (hi − z)*s*x_o — zero-sum: s*(Σ lo·x_e + Σ hi·x_o) − s*z*(Σ x_e + Σ x_o)!! FULLY ELEMENTWISE-CHEAP: acc += sv (broadcast to what) — per group g: dot_g = Σ_{r,n...} hmm organize per (n): dot[n] = Σ_r lo[r,n]·x_e[r] + hi[r,n]·x_o[r] → then acc[n] += s[g,n]*(dot[n] − z[g,n]*sx[g]) where sx[g] = Σ x chunk — but wait this reduces along r FIRST before applying scales — scales constant within group ✓ SO: per group iterate 2×64k → better: per GROUP iterate (as before) but accumulate RAW DOT without scales into a per-group register accumulator then apply s/z ONCE per group: saves flops too. Structure per group (128 k): dot = zeros(BN) for sub in 0..1 (64 k): wp (32, BN) u8; lo, hi xv = x[k0..k0+64] (64,) (normed/rounded outside... rounding: xn must be bf16-rounded BEFORE products ✓ do when loading chunk) xe, xo = split(reshape(xv, (32,2))) dot += sum(lo.to(f32) * xe[:, None], 0) + sum(hi.to(f32) * xo[:, None], 0) sxsub += xe + xo (or sum separately) acc += sv*(dot - zv*sxg) ✓✓ NO trans/join needed, minimal flops (1 mul + 1 add per element inside the tl.sum reductions). NOTE: lo.to(f32)*xe[:,None] then sum axis 0 — the multiply (32,BN) f32 tile then tree-reduce ✓ Triton generates FMA-friendly code hopefully. sxg: needs Σ xn (rounded) — Σ xe + Σ xo = tl.sum(xv) ✓ trivial — but CAREFUL: with zero-sum the exact formula y = s*(dot) − s*z*sx matches Σ (u−z)s·x ✓ EXACT same products in fp32? mine: distributes s and z — different rounding than ref's element ((u−z)·s)·x?? ref QUANTLINEAR: (w_u - z)*s produces the dequant weight THEN matmul — i.e., ref computes ((u−z)·s)·x products; mine computes s·(u·x) − s·z·x̄ — algebraically equal, fp32-rounding differs ~ulps — fine (cosine slack huge). 7. **Norm inline in GEMV**: ssq pass: for K offsets in chunks of CH=256: xv = tl.load(x + k + arange(CH)) masked → ssq += tl.sum(xv*xv) — ~9 loads; rstd scalar. 8. atomic ops: pointer int32 for counters — atomic_add int32 with sem/scope kwargs ✓. 9. @@ -0,0 +1,154 @@
+"""Layout planner for the flat weight regions (shared by host and kernel).
+
+Every tensor lives at a fixed offset in one of two flat regions:
+  * "u8":   int4 packed weights (bytes)
+  * "f32":  scales/zeros (fp32) + small dense weights (fp32 elements)
+
+Each block (3 KDA + 1 MLA) has a template of fields; the expert fields store
+expert-0 offsets with a fixed per-expert stride in each region.
+Field ids are compile-time constants used to index an int64 param tensor:
+offset_u8 = par[fid]           -> byte offset into u8 region
+offset_f32 = par[128 + fid]    -> element offset into f32 region
+(fid space per template: 0..37 for KDA blocks, 0..32 for MLA block;
+ par index for block b = b * 64 + fid; KDA blocks b in 0..2 (38 fields),
… +141 more lines

I'm overcomplicating this layout stuff in a side file with "u8e" per-expert entries — it complicates the par-index math. Simplify decisively: **Final decision on layout** — keep it brain-dead simple: - Per-expert entries don't belong in the par tensor. Instead, par stores ONLY expert-0 offsets + strides; but strides for u8 region and f32 region can be MADE IDENTICAL-per-region: I'll put per expert: gate_w | up_w | down_w contiguous in u8 → stride = ga + ga + da. In f32: g_s|g_z|u_s|u_z|d_s|d_z → stride = 2*gs + 2*gs +... - But sizes: g_w = 2304/2*1024 = 1,179,648 (256-aligned ✓ 1179648/256 = 4608 ✓); d_w = 1024/2*2304 = 1,179,648 — SAME! ✓ all int4 expert mats same size. - g_s numel = 18*1024 = 18432; d_s numel = 8*2304 = 18432 — SAME ✓ all expert scale/zero tensors same numel! - So expert stride u8 = 3*1179648 = 3,538,944 (each sub-offset fixed: gate 0, up +1179648, down +2359296) ✓ stride f32 = 6*18432 = 110,592; sub-offsets: gs 0, gz 18432, us 36864, uz 55296, ds 73728, dz 92160 ✓ - par stores 6 + 6 absolute expert-0 offsets? No — ONE u8 base + ONE f32 base + strides: w addr kinds: base_u8 + e*exb_u8 + {0, gw, 2gw}; s/kinds: base_f32 + e*exb_f32 + {0,18432,...}: par entries: P_EXW0, P_EXSZ0 + strides. Kernel constants for the sub-offsets (1179648 etc.) — these are computable from formula — KEEP as kernel-side literals computed host... hmm "kernel-side literals" again — they'd be baked source numbers: sizes are FIXED by the problem config — hardcode with comments + assert at host. It's fine to hardcode 1,179,648 = 2304//2*1024. I'll compute in kernel: GW_BYTES = 2304*1024//2 (constant arithmetic — Triton folds python ints ✓ JUST WRITE python constants in the jit source: they're ints ✓). Similarly expert scale numel 18432, stride 110592 = 6*18432. Shared experts: separate single entries (their own par fids). Same for the MoE fids per-block — much simpler par map. Let me REDEFINE par layout cleanly: par index = b*40 + local_fid, for blocks b=0..2 (KDA, 38 local fids... let me relist). KDA block local fids: 0 QW, 1 QS, 2 QZ, 3 KW, 4 KS, 5 KZ, 6 VW, 7 VS, 8 VZ, 9 GW, 10 GS, 11 GZ, 12 OW, 13 OS, 14 OZ, 15 BETA, 16 CONV, 17 AN, 18 MN, 19 ROUT, 20 EXW0, 21 EXSZ0, (expert base + strides implied: two params enough? u8 sub-offsets known constants (0, GW, 2GW within e*exb); f32 sub-offsets consts) — YES 2 entries. 22 SGW, 23 SUW, 24 SDW, 25 SGS, 26 SUS, 27 SDS, 28 SGZ, 29 SUZ, 30 SDZ → 31 local fids, use stride 40: par idx = b*40 + fid; f32 mirror at +128. MLA block local fids (b=3): 0 QW 1 QS 2 QZ, 3 KVAW 4 KVAS 5 KVAZ, 6 KVBW 7 KVBS 8 KVBZ, 9 OW 10 OS 11 OZ, 12 AN, 13 MN, 14 ROUT, 15 EXW0, 16 EXSZ0, 17 SGW 18 SUW 19 SDW 20 SGS 21 SUW... → ≤ 24 ✓ under 40 ✓. Total par = 128 + 160 → PAR_LEN 256 ✓ (+128 mirror for f32 — but note: some fids are u8-only (weights) or f32-only (scales/small): par[fid] = u8-offset; par[128+fid] = f32 offset — only the relevant one set; kernel uses the right one. For mixed fid like EXW0 (u8) only par[fid]; EXSZ0 (f32) par[128+fid]... to keep kernel code uniform: store BOTH arrays with -1/0 for unused; kernel picks the correct mirror index explicitly by design (known per field). For code clarity define in-kernel helpers `_o8(par, b, fid)` = tl.load(par + b*40 + fid); `_of(par, b, fid)` = tl.load(par + 128 + b*40 + fid) ✓. Now check the spike — it's probably still queued. Let me now also think about how to keep the kernel code manageable: write helper jit functions: ```python @triton.jit def _ld8(par, base, fid): return tl.load(par + base + fid) @triton.jit def _ldf(par, base, fid): return tl.load(par + 128 + base + fid) ``` GEMV signature (inline helper): ```python @triton.jit def gemv_i4(x_ptr, w_ptr, s_ptr, z_ptr, out_ptr, K, N, nrm_ptr, NORMED: tl.constexpr, EPI: tl.constexpr, xcur_ptr, wacc, pid, ncta, item0, items, BN: tl.constexpr): ... ``` where item mapping: this stage's items = [item0, item0+items) globally-strided by pid? Better: each stage does its own loop `for it in range(pid, TOTAL, ncta)`. Kernels' per-item config: BN=128, BK=64 (32 packed rows): bytes/iter = 32*128 = 4KB = 16B/thread at 256 threads ✓. Let me nail the GEMV code (this is THE hot path): ```python @triton.jit def _gemv_i4_stage(x_ptr, norm_ptr, wq_ptr, s_ptr, z_ptr, K, N, out_ptr, EPI: tl.constexpr, xcur_ptr, wacc_ptr, pid, ncta, ssq_cacheable: tl.constexpr): BN: tl.constexpr = 128 n_items = (N + BN - 1) // BN # ssq over x (normed stages) — each CTA computes redundantly if NORMED?? pass rstd from caller? — each item recomputes... redo per CTA: hoist OUT of item loop: compute rstd ONCE per CTA before item loop ✓ (2304 reads per CTA per stage — 188 CTAs × 9 KB = 1.7 MB L2 traffic per stage — fine). ``` Restructure: per stage, CTA: ``` rstd = _rstd(x_ptr, K) # if normed else 0 for it in range(pid, n_items, ncta): n0 = it * BN ns = n0 + tl.arange(0, BN) nmask = ns < N acc = tl.zeros((BN,), tl.float32) for g in range(0, K // 128): sv = tl.load(s_ptr + g*N + ns, mask=nmask, other=0).to(f32) zv = tl.load(z_ptr + g*N + ns, ...).to(f32) dot = tl.zeros((BN,), tl.float32) sx = 0.0 for kk in range(2): # 64 k elems each k0 = g*128 + kk*64 r32 = tl.arange(0, 32) wp = tl.load(wq_ptr + (k0//2 + r32)[:, None]*N + ns[None,:], mask=nmask[None,:], other=0) lo = (wp & 0xF).to(tl.float32) hi = ((wp >> 4) & 0xF).to(tl.float32) xv = tl.load(x_ptr + k0 + tl.arange(0,64)).to(tl.float32) if NORMED: nv = tl.load(norm_ptr + k0 + tl.arange(0,64)).to(tl.float32) xv = ((xv * rstd) * nv).to(tl.bfloat16).to(tl.float32) xe = tl.reshape(xv, (32,2)) xe0, xe1 = tl.split(xe) dot += tl.sum(lo*xe0[:,None], 0) + tl.sum(hi*xe1[:,None], 0) sx += tl.sum(xv) acc += sv * (dot - zv*sx) epilogue... ``` Wait — sx must be Σ xn over the group; I compute tl.sum(xv) per 64-k chunk → summed over group ✓ (xv already normed/rounded ✓). x load: k0+arange(64) — always in-bounds (K mult of 128 ✓). EPI modes: - EPI 0 (store raw rounded): tl.store(out_ptr + ns, acc.to(bf16).to(f32), nmask) — for q/k/v/g raw. - EPI 1 (store raw f32 no round): gu slots, qabs, logits... - EPI 2 (xcur + acc → round → store): oproj: v = tl.load(xcur+ns) → store ((v + acc) round) — as f32 of bf16. - EPI 3 (atomic weighted): down: tl.atomic_add(out+ns, acc*wacc). Separate concerns: oproj input x = oraw (no norm, no round on read — pre-rounded at REC); for ATA AOUT: EPI 1 to oraw. GU/H-slot xh: down-stage x-chunks = silu(g)*u — that's NOT a plain x_ptr read: needs custom. Options: precompute h = silu(g)·u into h-slot scratch in a SEPARATE mini-st... NO — do custom DOWN item code (not the generic helper): x chunk = f(g,u) loads. Since DOWN is just 4 MoE stages of similar code, write _moe_down_stage helper with custom x-read. Similarly ROUT (bf16-ish dense matmul) custom. beta custom. Fine — keep a generic _gemv_i4 for: QKVG(q/k/v/g), OPROJ(+residual), GU(gate/up normed), QKVA(q, kv_a with N-mask), AOUT(K=512 slice: x = pvf), and the ROUT... ROUT is dense (routerW (64,2304) f32) custom. Now — item count per GEMV stage known: QKVG: 4×32 = 128 items + 4 beta = 132: decode: ``` it < 32 → q at n0=it*128... with w ptr = QW etc. 32..63 k, 64..95 v, 96..127 g, 128..131 beta chunk (it-128 of 4) ``` To avoid code duplication: write stage helpers: - `_qkvg_stage(...)` full code - `_rec_stage(...)` - `_oproj_stage(...)` (uses _gemv_i4) - `_moe_router_stage(...)`, `_moe_gu_stage(...)`, `_moe_down_stage(...)` - `_mla_qkva_stage`, `_mla_abs_stage`, `_mla_attn_stage`, `_mla_comb_stage`, `_mla_aout_stage` - `_ext_stage`, `_final_stage` Each *_stage takes (all the ptrs/scalars) + reads own offsets from par at (b*40 + fid). Counter slots: constexpr per call site. Kernel main: ``` pid = program_id; ncta = num_programs _ext_stage(...) for b in tl.static_range(3): _kda_block(b, ...) # qkvg, rec, oproj, router, gu, down _mla_block(...) _final_stage(...) ``` with ctr slots: EXT=0; per-KDA: 2+b*6 ... hmm wait earlier I had MLA at 20 — with 3 KDA blocks: 2+18 = 20 ✓ same. FINAL = 30? N_SLOTS=40 ✓. Now about **static_range with helpers and constexpr blk**: `_kda_block(b: tl.constexpr, ...)`; par index base = b*40 ✓ compile-time. Also — counter target totals: static ints (except ATTN runtime) — pass as scalars or compute. About **`tl.num_programs(0)` vs passing NCTA**: use num_programs ✓. Now write finally. To guard compile-time, I'll implement in stages and unit-test helpers standalone (outside the persistent kernel) — e.g., test _gemv_i4 as a mini-kernel vs torch dequant. THAT's what I should do NEXT (with spike results) — build up confidence. Let me check the spike first.

+"""Kimi-Linear W4A16 hybrid decode — single-launch persistent megakernel.
+
+The entire per-token forward (3 KDA + 1 MLA attention layers, 4 MoE FFNs,
+every int4 dequant-GEMV, short causal convs, KDA recurrent-state update, MLA
+absorbed-latent flash-decode attention, MoE router + top-8 + shared expert
+GEMVs, RMSNorms, residuals, and all state/cache updates) is fused into ONE
+Triton kernel launch per step().
+
+Architecture:
+  * Weights are repacked once (after load_state_dict) into one flat uint8
+    region (int4) + one flat fp32 region (scales/zeros/dense weights) at
+    fixed offsets; an int64 param tensor carries the offsets to the kernel.
+  * One persistent co-resident grid walks ~30 sequential stages. Each stage:
… +584 more lines

Simplify EXB_F32 = 6 * GS_ELEMS since GS_ELEMS == DS_ELEMS == 18432. Now for the copy code (host): straightforward loop over experts and 3 mats. Dense GEMV helper: ``` def _dense_gemv_stage(x_ptr, w_ptr, out_ptr, N_out, K, rstd, norm_ptr, NORMED, pid, ncta, per_item_k=288...): items = ... split by K chunks: item covers ALL outputs over k-chunk [c0, c1): partial atomic? or distinct partial slots. ``` For beta (32 outputs): item count = K/576 → 4 items, each covering all 32 outputs but k-subset → partial slots (4, 32) → then who SUMS? REC items read beta: they need FULL dot: REC item per head reads 4 partials + sums — trivial (4 adds). So store beta partials (4, 32) — but actually K=2304: 4 items × 576 — wait partial storage = f32 (4, 32) at SC_BETA; REC per-head sums 4 values ✓. Rounding: ref beta out is bf16-rounded — round AFTER summing (in REC) — sum in f32 ✓ round in REC ✓ (round once, matches ref pre-sigmoid... sigmoid(bf16)? ref: beta_proj out bf16 → sigmoid(f32) — yes round pre-sigmoid ✓). Same ROUT: partials (8, 64) summed during top8 recompute ✓ round bf16 before softmax ✓. - ROUT items: 8 (2304/288); ROUT needs NORMED x (moe_norm) ✓ dense weight loads: (64, 288) per item — rows contiguous along k ✓ tile (64, 288)... registers (64,288) = 18432 f32 = 72/thread — too big → loop: per item iterate j-rows in 8 chunks of 8 rows × 288 (2304 f32 = 9/thread ✓). altern: items = (8 jchunks × 8 kchunks) = 64 items: item (jc, kc): partial logits for rows [jc*8, +8), k-chunk kc: accumulate into logits_partial[kc, rows 8] — partial layout (8 kc, 64 j) — item writes its 8 ✓. 64 items ~ balanced. xn: needs normed x: per item ssq redundant ✓ (each of 64 items 2304 reads + 288×64 weight-el + 8×288 fma). weight bytes: 590 KB per ROUT / 64 items = 9.2 KB per item ✓ fine. - dense x for ROUT = the CURRENT xb (post-attn) → ROUT waits OPROJ ✓. **GU**: (slot 9 × kind 2 × nchunk 8 (1024/128)) = 144 items. Each: standard _gemv_i4 with: w ptr = expert base + e*(EXB_U8) + {0 or GW_BYTES}; s/z = exsz + e*EXB_F32 + {0,GS or 2GS,3GS}; normed (moenorm), x = xb, out = SC_GSLOT/USLOT + slot*1024 + n0 (EPI raw f32). needs (e, w_j) per slot — but only the EXPERT INDEX for addressing; w_j needed only in DOWN epilogue (weight the output). GU doesn't need w_j ✓ only e. Compute top8 → idxs (8,) then e = idxs[slot] (slot<8) else SHARED: addresses: shared = TF_SGW etc. **DOWN**: (slot 9 × 18 nchunks) = 162 items: custom: x chunk h = silu(g)·u from slots; K=1024 (8 groups); e/w per slot; epilogue atomic add acc*w_j. w_j for slot j: idxs, ws from _top8: w_j = ws[j]; shared: 1.0. **H-slot f32 read per DOWN item**: 9×... item reads its slot's gslot+uslot: 2×1024 f32 = 8 KB per item — L2 ✓. **OPROJ (KDA)**: 18 items: _gemv_i4 NORMED=0, EPI residual: out = xcur + acc rounded bf16 → xb. x = SC_ORA (pre-rounded o_raw from REC) ✓ round-on-read not needed... store: bf16-round(xcur+acc) ✓ consistency: ref: x + attn_out both bf16 → sum then round-on-store ✓ mine matches modulo f32 accumulate — fp32 residual add vs bf16 add — cosines fine. **REC** (details mostly settled): item (h, t): h = it//4, t = it%4. Needs q_raw/k_raw for channels [h*128, +128): loads from scratch (f32); val rounding ALREADY applied at QKVG (scratch holds bf16-rounded f32 ✓). windows SRC: item reads prev rows (bf16 input tensors cold — f32 win bufs warm): cold: cq_ptr (bf16) rows 0..2, channel [h*128 + ...]: loads .to(f32) ✓ warm: win + layer*WIN_LAYER_STRIDE + pp*WIN_PP_STRIDE + qkv*(3*4096) + j*4096 + ch — f32 ✓ Per item: loop over the 3 windows? code: for qkv in static_range(3): src pointer selection differing by cold flag... to keep uniform: collect the (3,) window slices per needed channel-range: - for q: rows0..2 × 128 ch; val = q_raw slice - for k: same - for v: rows × 32 ch only Conv weights: TF_CONV f32: convw[idx, c, j]: for q: (128 ch × 4); k: (128×4); v: (32×4). conv out = silu(Σ_j win[j]*w[:, j]) — with j in static_range(4)?? window rows: prev rows 0..2 + val row → 4 terms: Σ_{j=0..3} win[j]·cw[:, j] where win[3] = val ✓. Then: k_c (128,) f32; q_c = silu·scale; also q ROUNDING: conv out → bf16 → f32 (ref) ✓ then q×scale f32 ✓ (ref multiplies scale AFTER rounding ✓). g: g_raw[h*128+dk] → decay = sigmoid(-x) ✓ (raw already rounded at QKVG ✓). beta_h: Σ of SC_BETA partials (4 values at s*32 + h) → sigmoid(round_bf16(sum)) — ref: sigmoid(bf16 out) ✓ round then sigmoid. S block ops as planned; write back; o = tl.sum(S*q[:,None], 0) → round bf16 → SC_ORA[h*128+dv0 + dr] ✓. Window DST writes: for q,k,v: dst rows for its 32 channels: row0 = prev row1, row1 = prev row2, row2 = val(bf16 f32): needs prev rows 1,2 for its 32 ch for q,k windows (loads 2×32 ×2 windows — small extra loads ✓) and val for its 32 (from q_raw/k_raw scratch ✓ rounded already). NOTE — the o *rounding* and also: ref computes o = (S·q).sum in f32 then .to(bf16) → ✓. Everything in REC per item: total loads ≈ S 16 KB + windows ~3 KB + qkg ~1.5 KB + S store 16 KB ≈ 36 KB → 128 items × 36 KB = 4.6 MB total ≈ 2.6 µs ideal... per item time 36KB/25GBps ≈ 1.4 µs; 128 items / NCTA — fine. **OPROJ epilogue residual**: reads SC_XA slot... which buffer: for KDA block b: x_in = (b%2==0) ? For even c (lo rows): dot_e[r] = Σ_d lo[r,d]·qn[d] → tl.sum(lo * qn[None, :], 1) → (16,) ✓; dot_o similarly hi. q_abs[c0+2r] = s_full... careful z varies along d (columns) — z[d] not per-row: zv = z[g, cols 128]: per-element (u − z)·s: decompose over d: Σ_d (u[r,d]−z[d])s[d]qn[d] = Σ_d u·s·qn − Σ_d z·s·qn: second term independent of u (and of c-row!!): zsq = Σ_d z[d]s[d]qn[d] (scalar per item) → abs value = dot[r] − zsq where dot[r] = Σ_d u[r,d]·(s[d]qn[d]) — precompute sq = s·qn (128,) → dot[r] = tl.sum(lo * sq[None,:], 1) ✓✓ cheap. results (16,) + (16,) for interleaved rows → write 32 values: qabs[c0+2r] = even, qabs[c0+2r+1] = odd: store via interleaved offsets: compute offs = c0 + arange(32)?? even r → c0+2r; assemble with tl.where on parity of arange(32): idx = arange(0,32); val = where(idx%2==0, sub(dot_e, idx//2), sub(dot_o, idx//2)) — needs gather... simpler: TWO stores of 16 values each: TL store with computed index vectors: offs_e = c0 + 2*arange(16) ✓ scatter-store ✓ fine. Error location: likely at tl.split — let me look at triton 3.6's tl.split: `def split(x): "Split a tensor into two along its last dimension of size 2" assert len(x.shape) ... x.shape[-1] == 2`. Hmm "object of type 'int' has no len()"... Maybe the problem: `tl.reshape(xv, (BK // 2, 2))` — with BK/2 = 32 vs... no. Actually wait — error might come from my reshape usage inside static_range: `for kk in tl.static_range(128 // BK)` — 128//BK is python int — static_range OK... The error "object of type 'int' has no len()": in triton semantics, `tl.split` might have an old signature `split(x, dim)`? or the issue is that `tl.sum(lo * xr[:, None], 0)` — xr[:, None] indexing fine...
