Critical learning: 1. **Per-CTA bandwidth is essentially FIXED ~3.3 GB/s regardless of pipelining** (P2/P3 same as flat). The per-CTA time scales with BYTES-per-group-tile: K512/N8192 hits 229-283 GB/s at grid 64-128 (approx 4 GB/s/CTA). So the design conclusion: **total stage bandwidth ≈ min(items, co-resident CTAs) × 3.3-4 GB/s** — I need to simply MAXIMIZE (items ∩ NCTA) i.e., make every GEMV stage have ~188+ items minimum, ideally ~376+. 2. Split-k (SK>1) helps grid numbers a lot: SK=4 grid=256 → 430 GB/s at (2304,4096)... 3. err=1.4e+00 for the split-k SK versions — my gP2s numerics are wrong somewhere (K2304 SK... err 1.4 — I didn't roll `sv0/zv0/wp0` inside the split loop (deliberate one-shot group count bug: for gi loop reuses wp0/sv0/zv0 WITHOUT updating → wrong). I know — the split variant wasn't a real candidate anyway (its main point was demonstrating grid scaling — which it did at 430 GB/s (with garbage math but same traffic). Also GS from spike_gemv2: similar. Fine. 4. Notably: with enough items, the pattern caps around 430 GB/s at grid 128-256 (still not 1.8 TB/s!). WHY can't we reach higher even with lots of CTAs? At 128 CTAs and 3.4 GB/s each = 435 GB/s — consistent. So per-CTA is STILL the limiter. Per-CTA doesn't improve with more CTAs (DRAM queue not the constraint). So per-CTA streaming is THE knob. Why ~3.4 GB/s/CTA? Per iteration (one group: (64,128)=8KB u8 + 1KB s/z + 0.5KB x = ~9.5 KB): measured per-iter ~2.8 µs?! From 41 µs / 18 iters ≈ 2.3 µs per iter. That's way beyond any latency (DRAM ~0.9 µs). What limits iteration rate? Possibly: the tl.sum REDUCTION resulting in cross-thread shuffles: (64,BN)*x[:,None] — reducing 64×128 = 8192 elems → (128,): tree: in-thread partials then log shuffles... with 4 warps: reduce cost... each thread handles 64 elements: 64 fma + shuffles ~ maybe 200 cycles → ~0.1 µs per iter — not 2.3. Actually something else: **the x strided loads:** `x + k0 + 2*arange(0,64)` — two 64-vec STRIDED gathers per iter (stride 2): Triton generates per-element 4-B loads?? (no vectorization possible on stride-2) → 128 separate 4-B LDG per iter → 128 issue slots... ~ maybe slow but L2-hot. Honestly — per-CTA latency chain profile required. ncu on one of these would tell exactly (L2 latency, issue stalls). LET ME ncu ONE kernel (P2 BN=64 W=4 at scale). ncu section on occupancy and scheduler stats. Alternatively — QUICK WIN paying off regardless: **give the CTAs MORE WARPS (bigger CTAs) and/or give each CTA 2+ parallel item streams (two tiles interleaved from DIFFERENT n-panels)**: a CTA processing TWO items SIMULTANEOUSLY (2 independent accumulators) doubles its in-flight demand → per-CTA ~7 GB/s?? AND items halve. Worth testing: DUAL-panel CTA (two independent dot chains interleaved in one loop) — the loop iterations would interleave two independent dep chains → ILP×2. Or simply: MOAR ITEMS AND MORE CTAs per stage. NCTA=376 vs 188: 2 CTAs/SM: reb sweep said ncta_used=188 always due to my conservative formula — force 376 with num_warps=4 (each CTA 128 thr; regs ≤ 128/thread → 2/SM possible if regs ~ ≤ 64... kernel regs=128 at nw4? earlier sweep: nw16_188 got regs=128/sp8, nw4 got 255. Independent of warps?? — the compile variants show n_regs varies per specialziation. Might just be unlucky.) **FUNDAMENTAL RECONSIDERATION — CUDA load_inline.** Honestly at per-CTA 3.4 GB/s with Triton, the whole-kernel estimate: heavy stages ≈ traffic/ (min(items,376)×3.4): at 376 items: QKVG 18.9 MB → 14.8 µs?? wait — 18.9e6/(376×3.4e9) = 14.8 µs ✓ not bad!! But stages with fewer items (OPR=18) → must raise item counts everywhere to ≥ ~350 via split-K... Doable in Triton: split-K EVERY GEMV stage to reach ~370+ items; GU: 144 → split-k 2x → 288; DOWN: 162 → 324; OPROJ 18 → splitK 16?? K=4096: split into 16 group-halves... x atomics per output col 16× — more atomic traffic but small... QKVA 53 → KV q_proj splitk 4: 48×4=192+... each split-k item has same K/4 work: per-item ~ 74 KB → 590 measured... hmm wait, split-k shares x? Each split-K item reads ALL of x for its k-range... So the optimization plan crystallizes: 1. **Raise all GEMV-stage item counts to ~2× NCTA** (NCTA=188 → ~370 items): split-K with fp32 atomic accumulation (already atomic-friendly for DOWN; for others, atomics to scratch are fine — but then scratch needs zeroing before... atomic-add targets must be ZEROED before each stage — zero via an item in a PRIOR stage? Annoying but small: QKVG area scratch q/k/v/g (3×4096+... wait those need zero-init ─ OR use split-DIFFERENTLY: keep BN-split but with PARTIAL slots (like beta/logits): items write (SK, N) partials; consumers sum SK partials when reading (downstream reads are cheap scratch reads). That avoids zero-init AND atomics. Consumer-side cost: ×SK reads of that scratch — but scratch reads are tiny fractions of traffic. Actually — do BOTH: split-K into partials with the CONSUMER summing (no atomics, no zeroing). Downstream stages in the chain (REC/OPROJ/ATT/etc.) sum a few partials per element. E.g., OPROJ for KDA: out2304 = Σ_k... partial (SK=2 or 4, 2304) — REC would need... no wait OPROJ feeds NOTHING (writes residual). (16,512) products + (512,)-scale-reduce per row-block: ~8K fma + exp — small compute but the RESCALE multiplication of pv (512,) each iter = 512 fma — fine. The CRITICAL PATH per ATT iter: load c (16,512)=16 KB → dot qa → m_new → wj → pv update — the pv update DEPENDS on the loaded tile's scores → serialization: per-iter = load-lat(0.7µs) + compute (~0.3µs) ≈ 1 µs → 33 iters = 33 µs + crossing... to hide: preprocess 2 tiles ahead? The online-softmax rescale loss: prefetch tiles INDEPENDENT of rescale: scores of NEXT tile don't depend on m_run; only the RESCALING of pv/l does (multiplication by alpha). TMA loads go to SMEM via load_tensor_descriptor(desc, [k0, n0]) → returns tile — pipelining via tl.range(num_stages=K). No — they can be placed in a LIST?? Not directly... Some kernels accept tuples of descriptors? In Triton 3.6, descriptor args are explicit kernel params. 800 args — PAIN. +"""Decisive battery: CTA scaling, dual-ILP, TMA descriptor GEMV."""
+import time
+import torch
+import triton
+import triton.language as tl
+
+
+# A: flat GEMV, item count sweep (split-K with separate partial stores)
+@triton.jit
+def gA(x, w, s, z, yp, K, N, SK, BN: tl.constexpr):
+    pid = tl.program_id(0)
+    npan = N // BN
+    pan = pid % npan

[Write] /ephemeral/mega-outputs/runs/20260716_233348_kinetic-claude_kinetic-0715_1m__02_kimi_linear_decode/repo/problems/02_kimi_linear_decode/spike_bw.py

+"""Honest bandwidth ceiling + ncu hooks on the GEMV ceiling."""
+import time
+import torch
+import triton
+import triton.language as tl
+
+
+@triton.jit
+def read_f32(x, out, n, BLOCK: tl.constexpr):
+    pid = tl.program_id(0)
+    acc = tl.zeros((BLOCK,), dtype=tl.float32)
+    for i in range(pid * BLOCK, n, tl.num_programs(0) * BLOCK):
+        acc += tl.load(x + i + tl.arange(0, BLOCK))

WAIT WHAT!! These numbers are ABOVE 1.8 TB/s — 3-5 TB/s on the tile reads?! Those can't be physically right for DRAM... UNLESS: the tile-read loop reads a SMALL footprint (4.7 MB × scale) repeatedly 200 times — IT'S COMING FROM L2 CACHE!!! The L2 is ~128 MB (or 96). Reading 4.7-45 MB repeatedly = L2-resident → multi-TB/s numbers are L2 bandwidth, not DRAM. And the f32 read of 1 GiB: 1527-1533 GB/s — that's the true-ish DRAM read number (1 GiB doesn't fit L2... unless 120+ MB L2 gives partial hits: 1 GiB / (1.5 TB/s) → it's ~85% of spec 1.8). So: pure contiguous fp32 reads achieve ~1.53 TB/s (85% peak). The GEMV tile pattern at 4.7 MB footprint was L2-cached → meaningless there (I need bigger footprints). Key question remains why GEMV caps at 517 GB/s: the GEMV kernel reads the same weight REPEATEDLY in the bench loop (300 iterations!) — 5.3 MB per call at 517 GB/s = L2-HIT-adjusted?! If it were L2-resident it'd be even FASTER (multi-TB/s as shown!!). So 517 GB/s with L2-resident weights means the GEMV inner loop is COMPUTE/LATENCY bound, not DRAM! gA grid=576: 1152-item runs — the loads per item were tiny (ngsk=1: one group per item: 8 KB w + 1 KB s/z): per item: ~9 KB + launch/item overhead + atomic-free store... 10.3 µs per kernel × 576 items/... hmm 576 CTAs each handling 1 item with ~9 KB loads + loads of x (0.5 KB): total 5.4 MB at 517 GB/s. The execution time is bounded by the ROUND-TRIP: launch → loads → compute → store ≈ 10 µs regardless of bytes… i.e., at 576 items it's ALREADY LATENCY-bound (10 µs is the floor of dependent-load iteration + store + kernel teardown?). Hmm — that reframes everything: the ~10 µs kernel-iteration floor = the per-CTA roundtrip cost of ONE dependent group-load iteration (~few µs) plus... So per-CTA-CTA-iteration cost ~10 µs-ish for a small number of dependent iterations, dominated by address-compute-load dependency chains and NOT hidden by triton. The only ways down: (a) fewer dependent iterations per CTA (split-K to the floor: each CTA does ONE 9-KB tile load serially per ~µs...); (b) tile loads with better issue: use 256-bit loads?? (c) TMA (hardware async engine) — the REAL fix: offload the load chain to the TMA units; (d) CUDA manual cp.async. Let me test the TMA path — my gC compile failed. Get the full traceback. Then measure gC performance — if TMA gets the per-CTA roundtrip down to ~2-3 µs for the same tile sizes, per-CTA effective ~3-4x better, and with ~300-600 items we approach 1.3+ TB/s. And still only ~500 GB/s — the L2-resident loop should be FASTER... — meaning the ~11 µs/kernel floor is something else: fixed round trip ~11 µs at ANY size ≈ bound by (kernel launch + full latency chain of ~2 dependent iterations + drain). Roughly: 2304/128/SK=18: ngsk=1: ONE group iteration per item per CTA: dependent chain = desc.load (round trip ~1µs?) → compute → store → done. The whole kernel = launch ~3.5µs + chain ~2-4 µs + drain... ≈ 11 µs ✓. So: per-CTA single-load-latency chain ~2-4 µs EVEN with TMA/NS pipelining?! That's the raw, unhidden cost of ONE dependent load in this environment. That seems HIGH — typical DRAM ~ 600-900 ns. WAIT — is something ELSE inflating these? The SK=18 items each also load x (0.5 KB strided twice) — small. And the s/z loads (1 KB) via desc rest [1,BN] — TMA for 512B row. Store partials (BN f32 = 512 B). Atomic-free. Grid 576 → 3 CTAs/SM... Per-item total = 9.5 KB at ~500 GB/s aggregate — across 576 CTAs — per CTA-issue-window: 9.5 KB / (11.5 µs) ≈ 0.83 GB/s per CTA... VERY low — pointing to **dependent round-trip ~ 8-11 µs per small kernel** — which includes, I bet, kernel LAUNCH (~3.5 µs measured earlier for empty kernel?!) + teardown. And the empty ~9-12 µs per-stage gap INCLUDES this per-stage first-load latency chain ~µs each. Test now: launch empty kernel 1000 times, time; and a "load 1 tile + store" minimal kernel 1000 times. reasoning
Definitive numbers: - **empty launch: 6.5-6.8 µs** (Triton launch overhead — Python dispatch + driver launch). That's per kernel in the microbenchmarks with torch.cuda not synchronized in between — back-to-back the GPU pipelines them... the MEASURED per-kernel cost is ~6.7 µs wall-to-wall. That's the Triton PYTHON dispatch overhead (arg hashing etc.) as seen by the CPU? No wait — bench does 2000 iters and syncs only at end: if CPU dispatch is the bottleneck at 6.7 µs/dispatch, we get 6.7 µs/kernel even though GPU work is ~2 µs. It's CPU-side overhead. This matches the earlier baseline.py being launch-bound too. - **1tile: 6.7 µs** — same as empty: sub-µs GPU work hidden in CPU dispatch. - **chain4 at 576 CTA: 12.3** — GPU work shows (4 dependent tile loads ≈ 5.6 µs + overhead). - **chain18 grid 1-188: 20.5 µs** — 18 dependent iterations ≈ 14 µs GPU = ~0.8 µs per dependent iteration (equal to DRAM latency). At grid 576: 48 µs ≈ 2.3 µs/iter — DRAM queue contention at high CTA count. So the REAL constraints on my megakernel stages: 1. **~0.8-2.3 µs per dependent load-iteration** — this is FUNDAMENTAL DRAM latency not bandwidth. The megakernel hides CPU launch overhead (1 launch/token ✓ good), but every CTA's iteration chain costs ~1 µs. 2. **QKVG item with 18 groups = 18 dependent iters ≈ 14-18 µs per item chain** ✓ matches the 61-66 µs measured stage time (~3-4 item rounds + ramp). The design lever is now crystal clear: **items must have very few dependent load-iterations (K-loops must be split to ~4-8 group-loads max)**, with enough items to fill the machine (~370-570 small items per stage). Per-token traffic ≈ 230 MB: distributed over ~28 stages × ~370 items × ~8 tiles each ≈ 1000+ items of ~230 MB/1000 ≈ 230 KB each... hmm wait: total items = 28 × 370 ≈ 10000 items × ~75 KB avg = needs ~ 230 MB/10000 items /item... anyway per-token total ≈ traffic / DRAM-bandwidth-floor with the latency-hiding stacked against "items per CTA ≈ chains ≈ 10000/188 ≈ 53 chains × ~1.5-4 µs ≈ 80-200 µs" — the practical floor given these latencies is ~150-300 µs. Sigh — it's why decode megakernels are HARD. But that's approximately a 5-8x win from the current 1.5 ms. Also CPU-side: 6.7 µs per Triton launch dispatch matters NOT for the megakernel (1/step) — but torch build-up ops (kvbig alloc, data_ptr marshalling) in step() are sub-µs... fine. So the concrete redesign of stage tiling: - **Split-K every GEMV to ~4-8 groups per item.** Items per stage: e.g., QKVG q/k/v/g (18 groups): split-k 3-way (6 groups each) → 4×32×3 = 384 items + 9 beta ✓ ~2 per CTA. Partials: split-partials → consumer adds K partials... OR atomic-add to the (pre-zeroed) target. Zeroing q/k/v/g scratch (16K floats) = forget it: use atomic into scratch — atomic fp32: the ROUNDING though! QKVG rounds outputs to bf16 on write — with split-K partial sums, rounding must happen after summing ALL partials → atomic ADD can't round per partial!! So split-partials + consumer-rounds: option A: store partials (SK, N) in scratch, a CONSUMER stage sums+rounds. The REC stage reads q/k/v: it would sum 3 partials per element: extra scratch traffic 3× read + write... tiny (16 KB×3×3 = 150 KB) fine. But REC items would do the summing (they read q anyway) ✓ minimal change to REC's loads: 3 loads + adds per slice instead of 1. Same for g_raw. option B: atomic + rounding-at-consume: atomics produce garbage rounding (can't round atomic partials) ✗. → A: split-partials + sum-at-consume. - **MoE GU/DOWN**: GU items don't round (f32 outputs to gslot/uslot) → BUT gslot/USLOT need zero... h — GU writes RAW f32 acc: split-K items → still via partials: gslot_partial (SK, 9, 1024) → DOWN items sum SK partials when loading g/u (they read them anyway, ×SK cost tiny) ✓. - **OPROJ**: rounds... feeds residual → partials (SK, 2304) then... OPROJ writes residual directly; consumer = ROUT/GU+QKVG(next): QKVG's x-buffer IS the residual buffer (xb_out). With split-K partials from OPROJ, SOMETHING must finalize xb_out = xb_in + Σ partials (+round): that's a separate tiny "reduce" stage (adds hop ~...). ALTERNATIVE: accept OPROJ as-is (18 items×2304 outputs / K=4096=32 groups) — item chain = 32 iters ≈ 25 µs!! — split-k by 8 (4 groups): 18×8 = 144 items, partial (8, 2304), tiny REDUCE stage: 9 items sum 8 partials + residual + round. Reduce stage traffic: 9×2304×8×4 B = 660 KB — fine. OR — better: OPROJ outputs are bf16-rounded; make the partial summing happen in... consumer stages (ROUT/GU of same block read xb_out for norm/x) and QKVG of NEXT block... TWO consumers; both would need sums. Cleaner: REDUCE stage. Small hop cost ~2 µs. Accept. - **QKVA/ABS/AOUT/ATTENTION** similar splits. - **ATTENTION**: items (h, chunk): chain iterations = chunk_rows/BR ≈ 1024/16 = 64 iters!! ≈ 50-100 µs chains. Split chunks to 256 rows → 16 iters ≈ 15-25 µs chains, and prefetch next tile each iter (restructure loop so loads overlap softmax update) → effective ~8-15 µs chains. Items at 16k: 32×(16449/256)=2048 → ~11/CTA ✓ good; at 2048: 32×9=288 ✓. - **_rstd**: parallel single-shot (one (18,128) tile read + reduce ≈ 1 µs) ✓ do it. - **Stage count**: 29 transitions × (~2-4 µs) ≈ 60-120 µs!! With per-stage time now ~5-15 µs, transitions are HALF the budget. MUST merge stages: total ~ 8-12 transitions target. Merged-stage design options: (a) Merge without dependencies where possible: [QKVG] → [REC] needs all QKVG; [OPR], [ROUT], [GU], [DOWN] chain. TRUE critical path per block: NORM→GEMV→REC/ATT→OPROJ→MOEGU→DOWN ≈ 5-6 essential sync points regardless. The only merge wins: ROUT into GU (spin-on-router-flag INSIDE GU stage), ABS+ATT, COMB+AOUT, COPY anywhere, OPR+ROUT merge, EXT merge into QKVG block0, FINAL into MDOWN. Saves ~4-6 transitions. (b) REDUCE per-transition latency: the measured barrier ~1.5-2 µs standalone; with skew ~3-4. Keep ~8 → 24-32 µs. Hmm. So merged target ≈ 20 stages: EXT+QKVG0(0), REC(1), OPR(2), ROUTGU→ hmm ROUT and GU merge (3), DOWN(4) ×3 KDA = 15, MLA: QKVA(18), ABS(19), ATT(20), CMBAOUT(21), MOPR(22), MROUTMGU(23), MDOWN(24), FINAL(25) — ~23 stages, ~20 transitions ≈ 40-60 µs sync synced. Wait — actually, there's a MUCH better transition-hiding: **the wait can overlap with INDEPENDENT work in the same stage.** E.g., GU stage items: gate and up items DO need logits... nothing independent. QKVG items all need x normed... hmm. Alternatively — **fine-grained dependency flags to overlap stages**: REC(h) needs only head-h's q/k/v/g slices + beta. With per-head progress counters, REC(h) starts when QKVG items for head h finish. This pipelines the tail: REC of early heads overlaps QKVG of late heads! Similarly DOWN-block's moe could start... This is the "fuzzy" idea from earlier. Implementation: fine counters per group (e.g., 8 groups of 4 heads): bump per GEMV item group; REC items wait on their head's group counter... Average overlap gain ≈ half a stage per boundary ≈ ~10 boundaries × 5-10 µs ≈ 50-100 µs. WORTH IT after basics. Priority order (by expected µs saved / effort): 1. **Split-K all GEMVs to ~4-8 group limit** (latency chains 15-25 µs → 4-6 µs per critical chain). Saves maybe 300-500 µs of the ~1.5 ms!! 2. **_rstd parallel** (~5-8 µs × ~10 stages saved ~ 60-80 µs). 3. **ATT restructure: 256-row chunks + prefetch pipeline** (ATT 365 → ~30-60 µs at 2k; similar at 16k with more items). 4. **NCTA 188→?** marginal. 5. **Stage merging + fine-grained flags** (~50-100 µs). 6. Later: TMA/cp.async if #1-#5 leave us DRAM-latency-bound on the streaming too much. Also don't forget DOWN apply wsel and DOWN rounding correctness with split partials (done at consumer). Guh — one more consideration before going further: I keep seeing DRAM-latency ~0.8 µs. Mentioned earlier ~0.6-0.9 µs. At 230 MB/token floor: items must supply ~188 CTAs × ~N-deep pipelines ~ 5 = ~ = a LOT of concurrent requests — theoretically fine... let me sanity-project the final target: total per-token ~ 230 MB (2304-ctx) → per-CTA share = 1.2 MB streamed as ~130 tiles of 9 KB → with ~5-deep effective pipelines ≈ ~35-50 µs per CTA + sync ≈ 60-80 µs/token?!? That would be ~3-5× better than my traffic-floor estimate pessimism earlier. But realistically the cumulative transitions/tails → ~150-250 µs achievable. Currently 1.5 ms. So 6-10x win possible! Let me now do a substantial kernel restructure — version 2 with: - Split-K partial schemes everywhere (consumers sum partials). - Parallel _rstd. - ATT chunked 256 with prefetch-2. - Merged ROUT into GU (router flags inside GU stage: GU stage items = [64 router items][144 gu items] with intra-stage flag wait on router counter). - EXT merged into first QKVG. - FINAL merged into MDOWN (cast items at end of the last stage). - A few stage merges: ABS+ATT stays separate (copy items + counter targets)... ABS(513) → ATT needs q_abs — merge ABS into QKVA stage as trailing items (QKVA 53 + 513 → abs items wait intra-stage counter on qkva)... — careful with in-stage DAG rule (index order ⇒ abs items AFTER qkva items ✓ fine). Hmm wait — for in-stage producer/consumer (intra-stage wait): earlier deadlock analysis: items processed in ascending index per CTA; consumers wait on a counter fed only by lower-index items → DAG ✓. But NOTE: with NCTA=188 and items ≥ 10000... intra-waits only at boundaries anyway. About in-stage counter: it's just another slot ✓. Plan v2 STAGES (each with its own slot; in-stage sub-slot pairs where merged): KDA block b: S1 = QKVG (EXT when b==0 prepended as 9 items) [+ QKVG wait-slot] items: [EXT 9 (b0 only)] [gemv split-k: 4 mats × 32 panels × 3 splits = 384] [beta 9] consumers wait: prev DOWN counter (or EXT internal). the gemv items write partials (3, 4, 4096): partial layout per mat: (3 splits, 4096) → 4 mats. With split-K gate GEMVs, the partials are pre-silu values of (incomplete dot product) — the SUM is needed BEFORE silu → the REDUCE/sum happens in DOWN items' x-read (they read partial slices then apply silu AFTER summing ✓ — silu(Σ partials) ✓ mathematically right ✓✓ phew. Root cause found: ~0.8-2.3 µs per dependent load-chain iteration dominates all stages, plus ~10 µs/stage of redundant serial _rstd. The fix is a v2 restructure: split-K partials everywhere (short ≤6-group chains summed at consumers), wide-parallel _rstd, prefetched chunked attention, and stage merging. Structure per stage: ```python def body(): for b in static_range(3): # KDA blocks XB_IN/OUT parity # ---- QKVG if b == 0: EXT items(9) bump intra(1) if b == 0: gemv items wait intra(1, TOTEXT=9) else: gemv wait prev DOWN slot target 333 rstd wide gemv split items: 4 mats × 32 panels × 3 splits: kind = it // 96; rest = it % 96; panel = rest // 3; sp = rest % 3; group range [sp*6, sp*6+6) partial store SC_QP + (kind*3 + sp)*4096 + ns beta items: it in [384, 384+9): chunk sp?? beta uses 9 chunks of k=256: beta partial (9, 32): bidx = it - 384: partial chunk computed over k [bidx*256, +256): store SC_BETA + bidx*32 + rows bump QKVG stage slot # ---- REC wait QKVG target (402 or 393) item 128: read partials(3) sums; conv; recurrence; window shifts; oraw store bump # ---- OPR wait REC 128 gemv split (4 splits of 8 groups): 18 panels × 4 = 72: partial SC_OPRP + sp*2304 + ns (raw acc, no rounding!...) reduce items 9: wait intra (72); finalize: xout = xin + Σ4 partials → round → store XB_OUT bump ... wait: reduce items bump intra after gemv? structure: gemv items bump intra(slot+1); reduce items `_wait(slot+1, 72)` then reduce work; ALL bump stage slot at the end (their counts). # ---- RGU wait OPR stage (81) router items 72 (partial sums (9,64)) bump intra gu items wait intra(72); e per slot; gemv 3 splits; partial SC_GUP bump stage # ---- DOWN wait RGU (504) gemv (324) bump intra reduce (9) wait intra(324): finalize residual + (only MLA) also outh bump stage MLA stages similar with slot ids 36..46. ``` Wait — important subtlety: reduce items stage-bump: my `_bump` helper computes count from (total, pid, ncta) — for mixed stages (producers+consumers), every item (both subsets) must count toward the stage slot, while only producers count toward intra. So bump logic needs two counters per relevant CTA: bump intra with producer-count and bump slot with total-count. I'll inline the count computation rather than reusing `_bump` for the mixed stages: e.g.: ``` # gemv items … then: _bump(ctr, ctr_base + SLO + 1, NGEMV, pid, ncta, dbg, ctr_base, TIMING) # reduce items (wait intra first), then: _bump(ctr, ctr_base + SLO, NGEMV + 9, pid, ncta, dbg, ctr_base, TIMING) ``` The CTA handles gemv items in `range(pid, NGEMV, ncta)` and reduce items in `range(pid, NRED, ncta)` — different index spaces → both loops run: total items = NGEMV + NRED must equal the sum of bumps across CTAs: BUT a CTA's bump uses ITS OWN `pid` in each sub-loop's count formula — summed across CTAs, total = NGEMV + NRED ✓ automatically correct!! (each sub-loop's counts sum to its total ✓). NICE — no unified index needed as long as bumps happen per sub-loop with their own bounds. BUT — the deadlock caveat: a CTA must process ITS gemv items BEFORE waiting intra (code order) ✓ as long as the CTA might also own reduce items — its own reduce items wait intra which needs OTHERS' gemv items (fine) — and its OWN gemv items are done (already processed) ✓ never waits on itself ✓✓. For _route within gu/reduce — needs LOGITS (from ROUTER items — intra slot of RGU) ✓ gu items & reduce wait intra(router=72) ✓ then compute _route ✓. For DOWN reduce computing wsel — needs router logits — available (RGU stage complete — chain wait) ✓. For gemv normed: normed stages: QKVG(gemv), RGU's router + gu — these need rstd of THE CURRENT INPUT: QKVG: xb_in; ROUT/GU: xb_out (post-attn residual). rstd computed redundantly per CTA (wide parallel version). Their position in code: after the wait ✓. Wide _rstd v2: ``` @triton.jit def _rstd(x_ptr, K): ks = tl.arange(0, 128) ks2 = tl.arange(0, 32) ssq = 0.0 for k0 in range(0, K, 32*128): xv = tl.load(x_ptr + k0 + ks2[:, None]*128 + ks[None, :]) ssq += tl.sum(xv*xv) return tl.rsqrt(ssq/K + 1e-6) ``` K=2304: 2304/(32×128) = 0.5625?? 32*128 = 4096 > 2304!! → zero iterations! chunk 2048: use (16,128) tile = 2048 + one (2,128)... ugh — K=2304 only: (18, 128) tile = 2304 exactly ✓ ONE shot: xv (18,128) = 2304 → ssq = tl.sum(...) — for 2304 the tile (18, 128) is allowed (arange 0..18 not pow2!! — tl.arange requires pow2!). I need pow2 shape for arange: (16,128) + leftover 256: two loads: a (16,128) and a (2,128) ✓ arange pow2: 16 ✓ 2 ✓. For K=4096: 2× (16,128) ✓. Generic code: loop over `range(0, K, 2048)` with (16,128) masked tail? masked (18 rows → mask rows < K-k0... simpler: since K ∈ {2304, 4096} special-case: K=2304: (16,128)+(2,128); K=4096: 2×(16,128). Write: ``` ssq = 0.0 for k0 in range(0, K, 2048): r16 = tl.arange(0, 16) xv = tl.load(x_ptr + k0 + r16[:, None]*128 + tl.arange(0,128)[None,:], mask=(k0 + r16[:,None]*128 + ...< K)... ``` mask needed when K - k0 < 2048: mask = k0 + r16*128 + c < K → rows beyond 2304 masked with other=0. ✓ write general masked form. OK — now REC's partial summing: q/k/v/g raw = 3-split sums: ``` qraw = (load(p0) + load(p1) + load(p2)) # f32, then ROUND: qraw = qraw.to(bf16).to(f32) ``` ✓ rounding after summing (matches ref's bf16 gemv output... note: with UNSPLIT gemv, ref rounds the total — identical ✓). For gate/up partials → DOWN x-chunks: he = silu(Σ gate partials)*Σ up partials ✓. For OPR partials: acc raw; reduce: xout = round(xin + Σ) ✓. For down partials: raw; reduce: xout += Σ_slots wsel_slot·(Σ_splits) — SUM + wsel order: reduce applies wsel per slot ✓ no new rounding ✓. For QABS absorb: qn = rounded(Σ partials ✓). Append: kv = rounded(Σ kva partials) ✓ then c_kv (bf16) ✓ kr rope. AOUT: reads pvf ✓ (from COMB). Alright — attention chunk restructure with prefetch: item (h, ch256): rows [r0, r1): iterate BR=32: ``` t_c = load rows r0..r0+32 (c, kr) loop rr from r0+32: t_c2 = load rows rr.. compute with t_c t_c = t_c2 compute last ``` Manual 2-stage prefetch ✓. Also `nc` arg: nc = cdiv(L0+1, 256) now — passed from host (recompute host-side) — kernel uses it for ATT totals/COMB loop ✓ and partial slots sized (68, 32, 514) covers ctx ≤ 17408 ✓ (16384+1024 headroom ✓). Copy items in ATT stage after att items (bump stage only) ✓. MLA RGU/MDOWN/MOPR mirror KDA with MF slots/fields, input/output buffers: block3: xb_in = XB, xb_out = XA; MDOWN-reduce writes residual XA AND outh (bf16) ✓. --- /dev/null
+++ b//ephemeral/mega-outputs/runs/20260716_233348_kinetic-claude_kinetic-0715_1m__02_kimi_linear_decode/repo/problems/02_kimi_linear_decode/v2_body_full.py
@@ -0,0 +1,984 @@
+"""Kimi-Linear W4A16 hybrid decode — single-launch persistent megakernel (v2).
+
+The entire per-token forward (3 KDA + 1 MLA attention layers, 4 MoE FFNs,
+every int4 dequant-GEMV, the short causal convs, the KDA recurrent-state
+update, the MLA latent-cache attention via the absorb identity, the MoE
+router + top-8 + shared expert GEMVs, both RMSNorms per block, residuals, and
+all state/cache updates) is fused into ONE Triton kernel launch per step().
+
+v2 design (driven by profiling):
+  * One persistent co-resident grid walks ~21 sequential stages. Each stage:
+    spin-wait on producer item-counters (acquire, gpu scope), process its
+    grid-strided work items, bump its own counter (release). Chain-ordered =>
+    deadlock-free while fully resident.
… +971 more lines

The pattern is unmistakable: EVERY stage is uniformly slow ~5-25x beyond traffic, dominated by DEPENDENT LOAD LATENCY at ~0.8-2.3 µs per iteration as measured in isolation. My split-K work items have 6-group chains = 12 dependent (32,128)-tile iters ≈ 12 µs each — OPR: 72 items/188 → 0.4/CTA ≈ 12 µs + reduce 18 ≈ 1+ → ~15 µs, but measured 66. RGU: 432/188 ≈ 2.3 × 12 ≈ 28 µs but measured 230. CONCLUSION: my model of per-iteration latency must be off by ~5-10x in THIS context. WHY? — because the ~0.8 µs number came from a loop with L2-RESIDENT data (small footprint microbenches!) — while the megakernel streams ~230 MB from DRAM every step (L2 missing + destroying throughput via... plus the SPIN POLLING ACROSS 188 CTAs throttles L2!). The spins DO interact (that's why the original diag showed the 22s storms at 16k). Even with nanosleep throttling, we have ~187 CTAs polling each ~2.5 µs — overhead per worker meaningful. And the attention stage at 2048 (ATT 1317−M.ATT): ~1300-1329 gap ~ measured before at 365 at 2048 (v1)... whatever. STOP measuring, START structural attack. The real fix category: **the whole approach of "synchronize between phases by having all CTAs spin at every stage boundary" produces deadly latency-serialization: ~20 stage boundaries × (chain latency + spin-storm amplification)**. Plus 430 µs for 3 × ~45 MB blocks... hmm no — let me carefully estimate the actual floor: 230 MB at 1.5 TB/s = 153 µs; my current at 1.7 ms is 11x that. What does a REAL fast megakernel look like for this — it's called "persistent tile scheduler with warp specialization". The core issue: a SINGLE sync point after each of ~24 pieces where ALL CTAs stall until the slowest piece finishes, and each sync costs ~3-10 µs due to spin storms. What if the syncs become CHEAP? Make barrier cheap: cheap = only CTAs PARTICIPATING in a stage sync (others work through on their own schedules) — that was the promise of per-item DAG... — the spin storms arise from CTAs that are ahead waiting on the next stage — they contribute nothing while data-starved. If instead CTAs had WORKING inventory across the boundary (independent co-issued items), they'd shadow the latency. THE ACTUAL KILLER INSIGHT: I should DOUBLE-BUFFER THE WHOLE TOKEN PIPELINE?! While token t's tail stages run (MoE downs), token t+1's early stages (EXT+QKVG) could proceed... but tokens depend (x_{t+1} = f(t)). Sequential. Alternative: reduce stages to ~8 total with fatter, more parallel items and coarser split... fewer syncs. Honestly — maybe the pragmatic deep fix is much simpler: **stop the herding: make _wait only spin with ONE early-exit per poll + heavier nanosleep, AND more importantly: DON'T make all 188 CTAs wait at each boundary — use the IN-STAGE sub-waits ONLY (they already narrow waits), and give CTAs useful work by PREFETCHING THE NEXT STAGE'S WEIGHTS before waiting!!** The stages are pure streaming — a CTA can ISSUE its next stage's weight tile loads (into registers/L1?) BEFORE spinning!? In Triton you can't issue-then-wait (load completes), but you CAN prefetch to L2: `tl.load(..., cache_modifier='.cg')`— pointless. There's PTX prefetch: `prefetch.global.L2` via inline asm!!! ISSUE L2 PREFETCHES for next stage's tiles while waiting for current stage to drain!! That converts the serial latency chain into warm-L2 hits (~1/4 latency). Implement: - At each stage entry (before its wait), each CTA prefetches a few of the tiles it WILL read (its first work items) — prefetch.global.L2 inline asm: cheap, fire-and-forget. - The DEEPEST fix pair: reorder so that while CTAs spin, prefetches land: cf. spin storms become warm-up time. That's creative but let me FIRST understand better whether the dominant cost is the SERIAL CHAIN at stage boundaries (which prefetch wouldn't fix — the chain within a stage)... The DIAG says DOWN stages ≈ in line... I need per-slot spans actually printed (min-start, max-end). print those cleanly. Let me get raw numbers quickly (fix diag output to print spans), one more run, then decide on prefetch implementation. Actually — WAIT. One hugely important measurement I already have: single-item chains: ~12 dependent iters ≈ 12 × 0.8−2.3 µs ≈ 10−28 µs for ONE GEMV item. RGU stage has ~2.3 items/CTA → ~ 25-60 µs/CTA vs measured 230 (≈ 2.3 items × 100 µs each!!). So my isolation latency per iter also doesn't explain: isolated chain18 gave 20.5 µs/18 iters ≈ 1.14 µs/iter, in megakernel equivalent = 12 iters/item = ~14 µs/item × 2.3/CTA ≈ 32 µs vs measured 230 ≈ 7x off. What ELSE differs in the megakernel? L2 pressure (230 MB stream flushes L2 constantly; isolated bench had L2-resident weights!). So per-iteration latency in megakernel ≈ DRAM latency ~0.9 µs — still not 30 µs. Spin-storm? +"""Quick wall-clock A/B of the megakernel step at one context."""
+import os
+import time
+import torch
+import reference as R
+import solution as S
+
+dev = torch.device("cuda:0")
+cfg = R.build_config({"n_experts": 64})
+ref = R.Model(cfg).to(dev).eval()
+sol = S.Model(cfg).to(dev).eval()
+sol.load_state_dict(ref.state_dict(), strict=True)
+

The variants compile per env but if Triton reuses cached kernels from ~/.triton/cache keyed on... the _SKIPS global is read at TRACE and Triton's cache key doesn't include arbitrary globals!!! So all variants reused the FIRST compiled kernel (with all default skips=whatever first variant was)!! YES — that's why identical results — my earlier observation: globals aren't in the specialization key (I noted this risk for _LAYOUT earlier!). _SKIPS read at trace DOES affect codegen but not the cache key → stale kernel!! Workaround: make the skip flags part of kernel signature as CONSTEXPR args (cache-keyed) — painful (17 flags). Alternative: nuke triton cache between variants: rm -rf ~/.triton. Wait — actually Triton DOES include "environment of traced function" in cache keys?? There's `cache_invalidating_env_vars`... It includes triton-relevant env vars (like TRITON_ALLOW_NON_CONSTEXPR_GLOBALS — not content-agnostic python globals). So NO — plain globals not keyed. Confirmed stale-cache issue. IMPORTANT COROLLARY FOR PRODUCTION: my PRODUCTION kernel reads _SLEEPNS, _TIMING, _SKIPS only for debug — but ALSO _NUM_WARPS... those are launch args... and CORE constants (SC offsets etc.) — all baked at trace BUT constant across runs ✓ fine. Since first compile wins; benign in production. EVERYTHING is uniformly ~10-25x too slow. There must be a UNIFORM cause — and given that attention = 336 µs for streaming 2.3 MB (at 2048), the per-item chain-latency model must be confirmed at scale. WAIT — attention at 2048: TOTATT = 32×9=288 items... per item ~ maybe 8 iterations of BR=16 (256/16)... ~8-10 µs/chain? With 288 items over 188 CTAs ≈ 1.5 each ≈ 15-20 µs?? measured 336. 17x more. IT'S NOT THE CHAINS. Something per-stage is just BROKEN-slow, something like... EVERY STAGE's critical path = spin until the LAST item completion under LOW parallelism + L2-flush penalties... still can't get to 17x. NEW THEORy — THE REGISTER SPILLS: my nw4 kernel reported regs=255/sp10 earlier (v1); v2 with more branches probably spills more. Spill loads/stores in hot loops (LDL/STL) go to local memory = L1-L2 traffic every iteration → 10x slowdown possible in inner loops!! With nw4, register budget per thread should be 512 regs (CTA has 128 threads; 64K/128 = 512!!) — hmm, Triton caps regs at 255 per thread on nvidia (hw limit IS 255) → 128 threads × 255 = 32.6 K regs of 64K — fits 2 CTAs/SM but any spill goes through the local spills at low occupancy. And Triton reports spills... at v1-nw4: 255 sp10; at v2-nw4? unknown. If significant spilling → LDL/STL in innermost loops → misery. Test: KIMI_NW=8 (256 thr; some branches get fewer regs/thread) compile fresh and time. Also nw16. Let me wait for the ablations first, then sweep num_warps with fresh cache. Also — I realize there might be a COMPLETELY different dominant cause: **the `par`-tensor loads!** Each GEMV item issues `tl.load(pb + ...)` (3-4 scalar int32 loads per item) + `_moe_in` for expert offset resolution (3-6 scalar loads) — these are L2-scalars (~0.4-0.8 µs each) SERIALIZED at item start: ~3-5 µs per item × ~40 items/CTA ≈ 120-200 µs/CTA!!! O(nearly whole budget!!). AND the loads are DEPENDENT (offset → address → weight tile) so they can't hide: EVERY work item starts with ~4-8 µs of scalar-pointer-chase latency!! MITIGATION: CTA-level caching of the offsets — compute them ONCE per stage (outside the item loop) instead of per item!! The _moe_in expert offsets depend on e (per slot) — but there are only 9 slots: hoist: compute the 9 e's ONCE per stage (per CTA), keep in registers?! Triton tensors of shape (9,) of int32 offsets?? make (8,) tensors of per-slot w/s/z offsets precomputed OUTSIDE the item loop — indexing per item via static slot decode... the ARRAY INDEXING problem: offsets per slot in a (9,) register vector, SELECT by runtime slot → tl.where-mask-sum reduction per item (cheap ALU, no loads!). Do it: precompute for all 9 slots: wb[slot], sb[slot] as (9,) int32 tensors... The kind-split offsets (gate/up/down) computed from slot-invariant bases + slot offsets — static arithmetic. The e per slot: from idxs (runtime!) — but idxs itself is a runtime tensor → wb[slot-expert] masks: for slot s<8: expert = idxs[s] → wb = exw0 + idxs[s]*(3*GW_BYTES) → as (8,) tensor: exw_all = exw0 + exb*idxs ✓ once; sb ✓. This removes ~4-6 latency-chained scalar loads per item — HUGE. Similarly beta/conv/scale pointers per stage: hoist one-time. Let me wait for the ablation results to confirm the shape, then implement the "hoist offsets" + num_warps sweep + maybe attention item rework as v3 patch series.

Ablation map at ctx=2048 (baseline 1686 µs): - attt (attention): -336 µs → cost ≈ 336 - gu (gate/up): -246 → ~246 - downg (down gemv): -885 → ~885!!! ← THE WHALE - qkvg: -190 → ~190 - residual (rest + overheads): ~29 µs DOWN GEMV = 885 µs is >50% of the total!! Why is DOWN so much worse than GU (246) with SAME shape class (GEMV over ~equivalent bytes: GU gate+up = 2.36 MB/expert; DOWN = 1.18 MB/expert... DOWN actually moves HALF the weight bytes of GU!). What differs in DOWN? 1. DOWN's x-read: 12 partial-sum loads per 64-chunk (the 3-way split partials summed in-loop) + silu compute — while GU's x is the shared normed hidden. 2. DOWN's 12 loads per chunk are DEPENDENT small f32 vectors (strided gathers) from L2... wait these partials ARE the issue: every item re-reads g/u partial regions: 9 slots × 1024 × 2 × 4 B × 3 splits = 216 KB per... each of the ~324 items reads 2×3 vectors per 64-k chunk × 8 chunks = 48 reads ≈ all of g/u regions AGAIN (unlike GU which uses ONE normed-x reload)... it's ~ 12×1 KB loads of L2-resident vectors, may be latency-chained (each chunk's he/ho computation DEPENDS on those loads before dot). But hold on — silu structure: for item (slot, sp): K-chunk = 512 elems: it needs h[k] for k in [sp*512, +512) — the DOWN-gemv items for slot j and split sp READ h only in their 512-column slice. h is 1024-long. Loads INSIDE the k-loop serialized with the w-tile dependence — 8 groups × ... hmm. Honest fix for DOWN: PRECOMPUTE h (silu(g)*u) ONCE per block in a tiny stage (27 items × slot-nchunk: read 3+3 partial vectors, silu, store h (9,1024)), then DOWN gemv reads h plainly like GU's x!! +1 stage transition (~2-4 µs) but massively faster down gemv (identical shape to GU which "only" costs 246). Expected: down gemv & gu roughly isomorphic ~800 → ~250. NET WIN. But WAIT — why is GU at 246 when equivalent OPROJ shapes are ~90-ish..., hmm GU = 2x bytes of DOWN... GU = 246 for 24 MB ≈ 100 GB/s eff. 5.5x off ideal. qkvg 190 for 19 MB ≈ 100 GB/s. So ~100 GB/s is where ALL the megakernel's GEMV stages sit!! That's the deeply concerning UNIFORM number: ~100 GB/s = 188 CTAs × ~0.55 GB/s. Whereas my isolated gA at grid=96 got 323 GB/s (HIGHER: SK=3 → 3x fewer tiles... 96 items × 6-group chains). Megakernel: 432 items of 6-group chains = mostly 2.3 items/CTA ≈ 12 iters ≈ 2.7 µs each... The DIFFERENCE vs the isolated bench (11-12 µs total): the MAcrobench has L2-resident x & params... — megakernel x re-loads → the megakernel stage has its x in L2 too (small scratch)! Whoa Wait — let me question something much more basic: maybe the megakernel's actual per-tile address arithmetic kills it: the tile index computation `(k0//2 + r32)[:, None] * N + ns[None, :]` — with N as RUNTIME scalar and k0 runtime loop var → creates (32,1) i32 tensor multiplies per iteration! GPU int mul... 4096/128 threads = 32 IMAD+ per thread per tile + address-add chains — comparing against precomputed pointer basics... usually minor (~1-2% ALU). STOP THEORIZING, look at ONE stage's isolated microbenchmark in megakernel-context: fetch stage counters into a standalone kernel with SPLIT-K GEMVs at the megakernel sizes and DRAM-flushed weights... I basically need ncu but it's blocked... ALTERNATIVE — don't fix the megakernel's per-stage bandwidth at all: CHANGE THE ALGORITHM to remove the long-latency loads from the critical path. The obvious one: **SOFTWARE PIPELINE ACROSS STAGES: PREFETCH (pre-issue L2-prefetch for) the ENTIRE next stage's weights during the current stage's compute**, so that when a stage switches, its tiles hit L2 (~70-100ns back-to-back chains). If each stage's tiles are L2-resident at stage start, per-iteration cost drops ~4-8x and stages approach compute-bound. Implementation sketch: a PREFETCH item-list per stage: each CTA prefetches (items of next stage assigned to it) — issue `prefetch.global.L2` (inline-asm) for its upcoming tiles. The prefetch target knows what it'll read: same item decomposition as next stage. Insert the prefetches EARLY in the current stage (before its wait?? prefetches must be issued BEFORE spinning so they overlap the wait!!!! → at stage boundary: 1) prefetch next-stage tiles for MY items, 2) wait current-stage deps, 3) run current items, 4) bump). This is the standard "software pipelining"/"cross-stage prefetch" megakernel technique and it's exactly what a fixed-latency-bound problem needs. It's invasive-but-mechanical: per stage add a `_prefetch_next(...)` call before the `_wait`. Volume check: CTA must prefetch its OWN next-stage tiles: GU stage at 432 items: per CTA 2.3 items × 6 tiles × 4 KB = ~55 KB of prefetches. Fine. BUT — prefetching TOO EARLY means traffic collides with current-stage streams (bandwidth share) and L2 evictions (230 MB total vs 128-256 MB L2 — GU's 15 MB might fit for the 20 µs it needs to survive — mostly OK). Expected benefit: stage latency from ~1.1 µs/iter (DRAM) to ~0.2-0.3 (L2) → the 12-iter item chains from ~13 µs to ~3-4, stages mostly latency-independent → total maybe 1686 → ~400-500 ?? ALSO — SECOND BIG HAMMER — **CROSS-TOKEN PREFETCH of the FIRST stage of the NEXT token** — meh, skip for now. And the register-spill/num_warps knob to sweep in parallel. Ordering by leverage now: 1. Hoist DOWN's h-precompute to a tiny stage (fixes the -885 elephant to ~-250): +1 stage cost... but actually wait — can DOWN's h-precomputed... THE SIMPLER UNIFICATION: instead of a separate stage, assign the h-precompute items INTO THE RGU STAGE as trailing items with the DOWN-GEMV items waiting on intra-slot 2 (a SECOND intra counter): RGU stage = [router(72)][gu(432)][hpre(27)]: gu writes partials; hpre items wait intra(gu=432) — that's a second wait in stage (fine) — then DOWN items wait RGU stage (all items) and read h directly. Nice: no new stage. THEN DOWN-gemv reads h (f32) directly instead of partials (like GU reads x): cut per-chunk loads 12→2. Cost: hpre writes 9×1024 f32 (36 KB scratch). 2. L2-prefetch before spins (cross-stage pipelining). 3. num_warps sweep nw4/8/16 (will probably settle on 4 or 8). 4. Offset hoisting per stage (kill scalar pointer-chase per item). Let me estimate DOWN after h-pre: DOWN gemv = (1024-K GEMV over 1.18MB×9 experts + scales) ≈ similar to half of GU ≈ ~120-150. Total projected: 1686 − (885−130) − (hpre-stage ~10) ≈ 950 µs. Then prefetch might halve again → ~500. Then fine-tuning (ILP, attention) → ~300-400. Also — one more ablation data point I want later: "qabs,cao,aout,comb,rec,router,beta,downr,oprred,moprojg" — the small-fries still sum to ~29 + 246(GU is really GU has...). Enough diagnosis - EXECUTE the down-fix now.

The weighted reduce... wait "downg" skip only gates the gemv loop (the hpre h-loads are in the gemv loop!!) — so downg's cost includes those h loads. The gemv still costs ~700. - gu costs 250. So DOWN gemv is STILL 700 despite plain h loads. It's NOT the silu. What else is special about DOWN gemv vs GU??? - DOWN: K=1024, N=2304, GEMV per item = 2 splits × 4 groups. GU: K=2304, N=1024, 3 splits × 6 groups. - DOWN/gu weight bytes per item: DOWN 36 KB (4 groups × 4KB×2 + s/z), GU 36 KB. Similar. - Item counts: DOWN 324, GU 432 per block ×3/1 — TOTALS: DOWN gemv exists in 4 blocks (3 KDA + 1 MLA) = 1296 items; gu also 432×4=1728. Hmm wait — but the DOWN-gemv also uses `_moe_in` with slot-dependent `e` — and `idxs/wsel` — GU does too! Per stage: DOWN gemv ~700/4 = 175 per block vs GU ~62 per block. Difference: DOWN's s/z loads: scales for DOWN weights: (8, 2304) f32 — those streams: 8 × 2304 × 4 = 73728 B per expert — wait per item: the sv/zv loads `wfb + s + g * 2304 + ns` per group — DOWN has 8 groups total ×... per item (2 splits × 4 groups) → sv/zv = 8 loads of (BN,) f32 × 2 (s,z)... anyway the s/z traffic equals its int4 bytes scaled by 4/128 ≈ 3% ✓ not the issue. New suspicion: THE `ns` 2304-wide tiles with N=2304: - DOWN's wp tile loads `(k0//2 + r32)[:, None] * 2304 + ns`: 2.3 KB row-stride; cachelines... - vs GU (N=1024): stride 1024. Hmm. Deeper issue hypothesis: DOWN reduce!!! The reduce's WAIT for gemv — reduce (NDN=18 items for 188 CTAs → 10%): its _wait spins until gemv fully done... that INFLATES the perceived downg span? The skip of "downg" removes gemv-loop AND its cost: 712. The reduce remains (18 items × ~18 loads each — small). OK so the 712 is mostly the GEMV loop itself + serialization. NEXT HYPOTHESIS — the DOWN gemv tile loads' shapes are just latency-unlucky: per item 4 iters (1 split... 4 groups per split x 2 = 8 iters)... trivial chain — yet it costs 175/stage. WAIT — I want to isolate ONE thing: the `_moe_in` per-item scalar chain: it does `tl.load(par + base + EXWF)` (int32 in DRAM/L2) then `+ e * (3*GW_BYTES)`: the `EXWF` value is the global offset (≈ hundreds of MB as int32) — then `wqb + w` — pointer for the tile loads. This 2-hop chain: par load (≈0.4-0.8 µs if L2-missing) → then tile → ~1 los... For DOWN: 324 items × maybe ~5 µs latency chained per item (par load → tile) + shared-expert branch... = ~37 µs over 188 CTAs?? hmm. BIGGER SUSPECT NOW: **`e` is derived from `_route` output idxs** — computed in the RGU stage... in DOWN stage I recompute `e` from idxs (registers) ✓ no recompute ✓. Hmm — honestly the register-pressure picture might finally matter: the kernel is HUGE (all stages), the scheduler may allocate poorly in hot loops at nw4. STAGE TRANSITIONS with spin+volatile-load+nanosleep across 29 sync points: v1 measured 28 transitions ≈ ~50 µs total, not 500. 2. per-item par-load scalar chains — few µs/item total, small. 3. Register pressure/spills (nw8 sp8 reported in v1; v2? unknown but probably some spilling; it systematically slows EVERYTHING ~1.5-3x...). 4. The x partial-vector/norm re-reads per iteration — the normed x loads per gemv iteration: strided 2-strides loads (xe/xo) = L2-resident — but these STRIDED loads have poor vectorization (4-byte loads, 16 lanes/sector wasted→ L2 bandwidth x2 burn, and probably slow issue! 5. tl.sum every iteration (tree shuffles) — with 256 threads over 128 columns: reduction depth ~2 (128+128 lanes) — cheap-ish. Hmm — and also: 6. **The `_wait` acquire per stage entry: `tl.atomic_add(ctr+pos, 0, acquire)` — WAIT — with 188 CTAs each stage also issuing this ONE acquire atomic per stage — 29 × 188 ≈ 5.5K atomics — fine. 7. **`_bump`'s debug_barrier** before the release atomic: forces CTA-level sync of all warps — fine. 8. !!!! **THE `_route` helper!!!** — computed ONCE PER CTA per MoE stage: 4 MoE stages × ~µs?? Wait — compute the top-8 extraction: 8 rounds × [argmax reduce over (64,) + max over (64,) + where ops] ≈ each round ~1-2 µs?? 8 rounds ≈ 8-16 µs per CTA per stage!!! × 4 stages ≈ 32-64 µs/CTA — VS measured whole ≈ 1030/188... hmm wait time is wall — per CTA wall ≈ 1030 — if each CTA spends 30-60 µs on _route that's meaningful but not dominant. 9. **`tl.exp` inside `_route` (64-wide)** — 64 SIN/COS-like exp ops serialized over SFU? exp = ~64 SFU ops / (4 SFU/SM/cycle... = 16 cycles... fine. Hmm hm hm. 10. **THE X-reads in DOWN/GU gemvs are the SAME 9-18 KB scratch RE-READ PER ITEM**: GU stage: 432 items × 9.2 KB x-reads = 4 MB from L2 per stage — trivial. fine. 11. **L1/DSO smashing between CTAs on an SM with 2/SM?** n/a at 188. 12. **U8 TILE ADDRESSES as i32 multiplies per iteration**: each tile index = (32,1) i32 mul + (1,BN) add → 4096 i×... 4096 int ops per tile per item over 256 thr = 16 int ops/thread ~ ns. fine. 13. **Cache-modifier misses**: x/scale/param loads go through default caching; every stage's x/h/scratch are small — L2, fine. 14. **KDA REC's window+conv loads and per-item conv weights (12 per-channel w vectors of 128×4B)**: small traffic ~ 100 µs stage?? REC measured earlier ~9-10 µs in v1... didn't ablate in v2 yet. let me ablate "rec" (in earlier list) to get its number. 15. **s/z f32 loads per group-iteration: sv/zv: (BN,) f32 = 512 B × 2 per iter — hmm × 18 iterations per full gemv — fine. So nothing explains 20x except... ugh. 16. **The DBG STAMPS with TIMING const — TIMING=0 in production (compiled out ✓). 17. **nanosleep kernel having extra instructions per stage? cheap. 18. **CO-RESIDENCY BREAKDOWN: at 188 CTAs and nw8 (8 warps = 256 thr), maybe not all CTAs are co-resident due to SMEM (16 KB shared reported): 16384 B per CTA... 188 CTAs × 16 KB = 3 MB — per SM 16 KB×1... 232 KB/SM OK. But WAIT — if residency isn't full, my spin-barriers would deadlock — it WORKS, so all 188 ARE co-resident ✓. 19. **The u8 weights region alignment: tiles read (32,128) u8 at addresses O*256-aligned + row*4096 → each row 128 u8 contiguous: L2 sectors: 128B = 4×32B ✓ fine. 20. **THE `wp >> 4` + `& 0xF` + 2× .to(f32) per element ALU**: per tile 4096 elements × ~5 int/fp ops = 20K ALU ops ⇒ over 256 threads ≈ 80/thread ≈ 40 cycles ~ ns. fine. 21. **THE tl.sum: two per tile reduce 64×128→128... e.g., (32,128) sum = 4096→128: reduce = ~0.5×4096 FMA... ≈ tens of cycles. fine. 22. **Length of the kernel / instruction cache misses!!** The kernel's text is ENORMOUS (30 stages × unrolled loops): each stage's code maybe 50-200 KB of SASS?! Instruction cache on NVIDIA is ~32-64 KB per SM (L0) / 2 MB (L1.5?). As each CTA visits a NEW stage, it I-CACHE-MISSES the stage's code: ~100-500 KB of code per stage × ~25 stages... ICACHE THRASHING could be the hidden ~10x!!! Every stage transition = cold icache fetch ~ ~10-50 µs for the whole SM array to pull fresh code!! THAT could be the systematic wall!! Est code size: my kernel unrolled `tl.static_range(3)` KDA blocks (3× text!) + heavy loops... Triton PTX for the full thing maybe ~1-5 MB SASS. Per token, SM sweeps through ~MBs of code serially. THIS is likely why everything's uniformly ~10x slow!! MITIGATION: SHRINK THE CODE: (a) de-unroll the KDA blocks (run blocks in a LOOP, parametrized by block-id (runtime!) so one copy serves 3 blocks+MLA needs BRANCHING on kind (KDA vs MLA) — same total code as ONE KDA + ONE MLA iteration — hmm but runtime block-id for pointers (par offsets are runtime anyway! I can pass a per-block BASE value read from par!). The stage sequences differ between KDA and MLA though: MLA's attack includes ABS/ATT etc — the block-driver must branch on kind: `if kind == 0: <KDA stages> else: <MLA stages>` — that's exactly 1× KDA text + 1× MLA text — 2× instead of 4×. Saves ~50%+ of icache footprint. (b) Even better: restructure blocks to SHARE stage routines: QKVG & OPR & GU & DOWN share a common GEMV-split routine with (fid base ptrs, dims...) — those already share _gemv_split (Triton inlines it each call site — the CALL SITES each materialize the loop!!). Reduce call sites or is that icache-neutral? Each inlined gemv_split ≈ ~2-5 KB of text → ~15 call sites ≈ 30-75 KB — not the whale. (c) The .static_range unrolls of inner loop `for kk in tl.static_range(2)` ✓ tiny. (d) The REC stage: huge ad-hoc conv/window code (fat!). reasoning
SMOKING GUN: **the megakernel cubin is ~4 MB (PTX 4.3 MB)!** A kernel that big necessarily thrashes instruction caches. NVIDIA L0 i-cache ~32 KB, L1.5 ~128 KB?, system L2 128 MB. SASS instructions execute sequentially per CTA — with ~4 MB of code, every stage transition pulls ~130-300 KB of fresh SASS through L2 at i-miss latencies — that's HUNDREDS of memory round-trips per stage. THIS IS THE WALL. Estimates: at ~100-300 KB of hot SASS per stage × ~150 B/clk/SM fetch ≈ ICACHE miss cost per stage ~ microseconds times ~25 stages × per-token — but WHY would it cost ~1 ms total?? Let's estimate: instruction fetch bandwidth per SM ~ 128B/cycle: 4 MB / 128 = 32K cycles = 13 µs per full sweep per SM — plus MISSES (cold ~ every 128-B line once): 32768 lines × miss-cost (L2 hit ~300-600 ns??) — if L1.5/L2-resident after first pass, per-SM total ~ ... the REAL cost: the first pass per SM populates L1.5/L2 at DRAM/L2 latencies — with 128 concurrent SMs all fetching DIFFERENT portions (CTAs at different stages!!) = up to ~1M instruction-line fetches in flight through L2... an order of disaster. Whatever the exact model: 4 MB SASS is guaranteed catastrophic. Good kernels keep hot code < ~64-256 KB. Prescription: shrink code ~30-100x. HOW: 1. **CTAs SPECIALIZE but the kernel is ONE function — all CTAs run all stages**. Can't specialize per CTA (same launch). BUT the per-CTA path through the huge kernel is the same for all — the i-cache thrash happens per stage regardless. 2. **REDUCE CODE:** a. De-unroll KDA blocks (static_range(3) → runtime block-id reading params from par) — 3× to 1× for KDA stages (the fattest identical text) b. Factors **ALL GEMV paths into ONE inlined routine** parameterized by runtime (ptrs, K,N,split,...) — GEMV call sites collapse near-zero apart from loops' structures... 22 sites' code becomes one function — hmm — Triton always inlines, but a `tl.inline... `— there's no function-call in generated SASS from jit helpers — everything is inlined by design. A jit helper only dedupes source text, not code. To prevent code explosion, reduce the NUMBER OF DISTINCT INLINED BODIES: - _gemv_split: ~15 call sites × moderate text — these are SMALL per-site (loop ~2-4 KB) ≈ 30-60 KB total — NOT the whale. - The whale: the fully unrolled block repetitions + attention body + REC's ad-hoc + ROUT/AOUT/ABS bodies: each ~50-300 KB. c. **ICC-friendly structure: LIMIT stage variety: *replace most stages with ONE unified "GEMV-split stage" driven by a runtime loop***! Real megakernels do exactly this: one generic pipelined loop body with runtime descriptors; CTAs walk a compact scheduler loop. THE CODE BECOMES SMALL (~50-200 KB). THE PROPER REDESIGN (v3) — unified item-loop kernel: - Each CTA runs a SINGLE LOOP over a global stage list [stage_id = 0..N), where stage work is data-driven: - The GEMV-split computation is ONE code body (runtime K, N, ptrs from an in-kernel stage-descriptor array read from a parameter buffer — small!). - Special stages (REC, ATT, etc.) get their own blocks but that's ~6 distinct bodies. - The key ILP insight: the code for an arbitrary GEMV stage is SHARED; all heavy streaming happens in ONE body; i-cache stays warm ACROSS stages instead of re-fetching per stage. Expected code: unified-GEMV (~30 KB) + REC (~20 KB) + ATT (~30 KB) + ABS/AOUT (~15) + ROUT (~10) + driver (~10) ≈ ~150 KB total. 25x smaller. Descriptor-driven stage table (host-built, per model, static per run; loaded once per kernel launch... ~30 entries × 16B): per stage: type (0=gemv-split-write-partials, 1=gemv-split-atomic, 2=special id), slot_in/wait-target, slot-out, item count defer-computed... The CTA's driver loop: for st in range(NSTAGES): load descriptor st (few ints from L2), wait(if needed), dispatch by type: for gemv: items cover (k-chunks × n-panels) — the pointer math all runtime. Where do the partials/norms/residuals tie in? GEMV stage descriptors need: - x-source scratch offset + norm id (0=none, 1=anorm, 2=mnorm) + rstd flag; - weight offsets (w/s/z) — per block/stage - out: partial-region offset (+stride) or "compute residual" or "store to slot" - split count All as ints in a stage-param table (host-computed per block-kind & layout): ~24 gemv stages + specials; per stage ~16 ints (128 B) — fits param zone. Special stages stay hard-coded code blocks: EXT, REC(+conv per KDA), ABS+ATT(+copy), CAO, COMB+AOUT, ROUTE (hmm - can also fold router GEMV into generic), HPRE. And OPROJ's residual-write: generic GEMV + tiny REDUCE stage (generic too - "reduce partials + residual + round" as a special body *shared across blocks* — runtime the residual in/out offsets ±OUT round mode + partials offset/counts. Same for DOWN-reduce (weighted moe read wsel — needs ROUTE idxs….) hmm — down-reduce needs idxs/wsel — computed as _route per MoE stage — that can be a special "route stage" (one block of code) writing idxs/wsel small scratch for subsequent gemv... wait — expert addresses for gate/up/down need e/w per slot — computed per stage: the gemv body would compute e from idxs-scratch per item (2 loads + tiny ALU) ✓ no longer needs _route!!! And the reduce apply wsel similarly read (16) scratch... ALWAYS read the slot tables instead of registers. Down-reduce: read wsel[s] (16 f32) ✓. So specials list: EXT, REC (per KDA block × 3 runtime-identical!!), ABS+ATT (1), COMB+AOUT (1), ROUTE (4 runtime-identical), OPRRED/RESID (4 runtime-identical), HPRE (4), COPY (1 cold). All except REC/ABS/ATT are tiny. REC identical text ×1 parametrized by block runtime values (conv ptr, S ptr, pp/window ptrs via args arrays...). So maybe ~400 KB → ~150 KB. Additionally we WANT stage merging preserved with intra-flag machinery ✓ keep (cheap). Honestly the CLEANEST way to implement: keep the current stage decomposition but rewrite the kernel driver as: ``` for st in range(NSTAGES): # runtime loop over ~21 entries stt = tl.load(stbl + st*16 + 0) # stage type ... if stt == GEMV_SPLIT: generic-gemv-stage(params) elif stt == REC_BLK: rec-stage(params) ... ``` Triton compiles each branch's body ONCE → the loop structure persists at runtime (the loop is NOT unrolled!) → instruction footprint = sum of branches once ≈ 300-400 KB max but shared across 21 stages... hmm wait — the gemv body appears TWICE (if/elif) — by having ONE branch handle all gemv-type stages: appears once ✓. BUT — the loop ITSELF being runtime-driven means `st` dependent addressing and the NSTAGES count — runtime values. The stage sequence index sets everything runtime. Code stays compact. Stage items per type as params in the table: counts runtime ✓ (bump counts runtime ✓ fine). ABS stage: needs rope/append (runtime param slot-id append item idx). fine. Special hybrids: QKVG (gemv+beta items), RGU (router+gu+hpre?), DOWN-gemv needs h-read from h-pre + its reduce — let the descriptor system support per-stage PARAM sub-lists: e.g., stage types: T0 = EXT_COPY_IN (1 body incl. EXT and cache COPY) T1 = GEMV_SPLIT (generic: params: xoff, nmode, rstd-src(0 none, 1 an, 2 mn), wfid-triple (3 offsets into par or absolute), ngroups/nsplits/npan, out-partials-off, partial-stride... ) — covers q/k/v/g, o-proj, q-mla, kv_a-mla, gate/up, mla-qabs-q/kva T2 = DENSE_GEMV (rowmajor dense weight: beta + router - same body diff params) T3 = REC (block-idx param: S/window/conv pointers via param, raw partials offset, beta partials offset, o out offset) T4 = REDUCE_RESID (params: partials off/stride/splits, x-in off, x-out off, round) - the OPR + DOWN-residual variants WITH optional wsel mode (moe-weights-sum with wsel scratch offset; with/without) T5 = ROUTE (writes idxs/wsel scratch; param logits off) T6 = HPRE (partials-off, h-out-off) T7 = ABS (params: weight-triple for kvb, rope mode, out offsets, L0., etc + APPEND fused) T8 = ATT (cache pointers already global args? — runtime params: L0/nc/qabs off/kvbig offsets... The cache base pointers (ckv_in/kr_in/kvbig/krbig are KERNEL ARGS — used directly ✓ plus copy items cold.) T9 = CAO (comb+aout: params) T10 = DOWN-GEMV (special inner: reads h (9,1024) per slot expert... wait this is a gemv over down weights with x = h[slot] - the generic T1 could handle if x-offset were per-slot: h-off = SC_HX + slot*1024... the item-space here adds an expert-slot dim; T1's item decode: (slot, panel, split) with x-offset slot-dependent — PARAMETERS could encode slot-stride for x & weight-offset-per-slot-mode (expert vs fixed)!! doable: x_off_mode 0=fixed, 1=slot-strided; w_off_mode 0=fixed, 1=expert(slot via idxs-scratch), 2=shared(fixed per block). Params ints per stage ≈ 24 × 21 entries ≈ 2-3 KB table. Build host-side trivially (python computes ints, H2D once per model-or-never — static per layout: depends only on layout constants! Actually the par offsets depend ONLY on the FLAT layout → static per model ✓ host builds once). Numbers of stages: per KDA b: EXT-merge-in-qkvg(1) + gemv(4m)=1 + beta=1 + REC=1 + OPROJgemv=1 + RESIDred=1 + ROUTE=1 + GU(gate+up se as one stage with kind-dim items) + HPRE=1 + DOWNgemv=1 + RESIDwm=1 ≈ 9 stages × 3 = 27 + MLA: QABS-gemv + ABS+ATT(COMB? — ATT+ABS+CAO...) ≈ 6 → 33 TOTAL?! — hmm that's MORE stages than now (sync count ↑) — bad. Single-group items have horrible compute/|| per item — the savings lost to latency chains again. 3-group items are the sweet spot (48 kB ≈ 3-4 µs). - Cross-stage L2 prefetch: add prefetch loops to stage entries for the next-heavy stage. (b) cut the m-rescaling chain: use per-item INDEPENDENT max free... The flash-rescale serialization is fundamental ~ m is per-item local; but the pv update per row-block is the serializer. Honestly alternative: TWO-PASS attention: pass1 computes scores (L,H) to scratch + rowmax (2.3 MB writes + reads ~ 5 MB traffic), pass2 softmax+weighted-sum — more traffic but HUGELY parallel items (row-chunks × head-chunks) so latency-hidden = fast. At ctx 2048: pass1: read 2.4 MB, write 0.3 MB; pass2: read 2.4+0.3. Total ~ 3x cache reads, but each pass is embarrassingly parallel with ~full-CTA-work → ~30-40 µs vs 168. +import torch

+import solution as S

+sol = S.Model(cfg).to(dev).eval()
+sol.load_state_dict(ref.state_dict(), strict=True)
+

@@ -0,0 +1,44 @@
+"""Isolate _route vs torch.topk."""
+import torch
+import triton
+import triton.language as tl
+
+
+@triton.jit
+def route_kernel(lg_in, idx_out, w_out):
+    p = tl.load(lg_in + tl.arange(0, 64))
+    lg = p
+    m = tl.max(lg)
+    e2 = tl.exp(lg - m)
+    pr = e2 / tl.sum(e2)

My current/probe code: ``` per group g: for kk in 2: wp (32, BN) = rows [k0//2 + r] for r in 0..31 lo = wp & 0xF → k = 2r (even) hi = wp >> 4 → k = 2r + 1 (odd) xe = x[k0 + 2*arange(32)] = x at evens up to 2*31 = 62 xo = x[k0 + 1 + 2*arange(32)] dot += sum(lo*xe[:,None], 0) + sum(hi*xo[:,None], 0) sx += sum(xe) + sum(xo) acc += sv*(dot - zv*sx) ``` WAIT — the xe index: `x_ptr + k0 + 2 * tl.arange(0, 32)` — with k0 = g*128 + kk*64: the sub-block (kk) covers k ∈ [k0, k0+64): 64 elems. Then sum over axis 0 → (BN,) Σ_r ✓. `hi`: hi[r, col] = w at k0+2r+1 times xo[r] = x[k0+1+2r] ✓ CORRECT. +"""Isolation probe of kind-1 (up) gemv on real flat data via _moe_in."""
+import torch
+import torch.nn.functional as F
+import reference as R

Needs the 2-pass treatment BADLY. 2. **RGU ~63 ×4 = 252 µs** (router 72 + GU 864 items — GU includes bigness). The GU traffic per MoE ~15 MB → 63 µs = 240 GB/s aggregate. Structure OK now (correct); could speed with smaller split target (864 items×~26 KB each — 6-iteration chains — per CTA 4.6 items × ~10 µs + transition ≈ ~50 µs...). Also RGU's rstd_m is computed per CTA redundantly (~2-4 µs × 4 stages)... maybe compute once per stage into mailbox. Actually ROUT-stage could compute ssq as a leading item → mailbox write → GU items read scalar (saves per-CTA recomputation... but every item then reads mailbox (latency chain ~0.5-1 µs per item!). Meh. 3. **OPR 24-27 ×4 ≈ 100** (o_proj total traffic 18.9 MB / 24 µs = 780 GB/s aggregate — actually decent!) 4. **DOWN ~29 ×4** (~11 MB → 380 GB/s) 5. Stage transitions ~8-9 µs each (the ~56 µs K0.QKVG->REC gap between end_rel 55→64. There are ~20 such ×2-9 µs = ~80-120 µs of sync overhead!) Priorities: (a) attention two-pass rewrite (~150 µs win), (b) RGU trim (~???), (c) reduce stage-count/transitions. Actually, for attention. Let me think about what makes ATT 180 µs at 2048: 32×9=288 items, ~1.5/CTA × ~(2049-row sweep via 128-iter BR=16 chain with exp/rescale per iter) ≈ ~150-200 µs. At 16384: 32×65 ≈ 2080 items → TOTAL items/CTA ≈ 11 × similar per-item ≈ ~500+ µs?? measured wall at 16k total = 1665 → the ATT's probably ~700-800 there!! THIS IS THE BIGGEST FISH. ATT rewrite (two-pass decoupled as designed): Phase A: score items (h, 256-chunk): write scores (256,) + chunkmax to SC_HMAX via atomic_max ✓ parallel streaming — should be ~fast (each row = one dot product across 512+64 → 576 ops... item = 256 rows × 576 = 147K FMA + chunk stores 256 f32 — per item ~10-15 µs at stream-latency ~ item is 256×576×2B = 295 KB stream — hmm too small per DRAM-latency ~ 30-50 µs whjich per item too!! — hierarchical KLUDGE: 147K MACs ~ 73 ns compute... the stream dominates ~ 295 KB/10 GB/s ~ 30 µs per item?? at 1.4 GB/s per CTA (latency) ~ 200 µs per item!!! WAIT NO — per-CTA latency-chains ~3.4 GB/s → 295 KB / 3.4 GB/s ≈ 87 µs per item — with ~1.5 items/CTA → ~130 µs total. SAME PROBLEM. The per-item serial-iteration count = rows/BR = 16 iters of (16,512)+bf16 ≈ 18 KB per iter ≈ 18 KB per iter ≈ ~0.9-2.3 µs/iter → ~30 µs/item ✓ ~matches. So even parallel-pass, per-item latency limits. — FIX: smaller BR with MORE tiles in flight?? — the issue is the same dependent load chain — each tile iteration waits its own loads. **WAIT — THE STANDARD FLASH-DECODE ANSWER: process MULTIPLE HEADS PER ITEM (head-chunk) sharing the SAME cache tile!!** Item (16 heads × 64-row chunk): per tile load (64 rows × 512) = 64 KB: for 16 heads: compute 16 scores per row — amortizes the stream 16×!! The cache is read ONCE from DRAM; L2 hits for the other 15. Items = 2 head-groups × chunkcount(65) = 130 items — each streams rows ONCE per group: total DRAM = 2× the cache vs 32. Better still: all 32 heads per row tile with (32 heads) — item = row-chunk: DRAM = cache once!! Item count = 65 at 16k — too few for 188 CTAs...2 head-groups × 65 = 130 (better) ≈ balanced. Then per-row-iteration compute for 32 heads: 32 dots (576 ops each → tile (BR,512) × q_abs[h] per h) — 32 heads × (16,512): ~64 f32 K ops per thread-iter... register-friendly. ALSO THE M-RESCALE serialization disappears: each item covers a row-chunk for ITS head-group: rows' per-head max/l/pv partials — pvh = (32, 512) accumulated — per-item per-head-slot — flash partials per (group, chunk, head-subrange)... too heavy on partial traffic if per-head. OK CAN'T FIX EVERYTHING WITHOUT ANOTHER ITERATION of careful design. PRECISE rewrite PLAN for attention (2-pass, head-grouped): Phase A (scores+partial max): - partition: for hc in range(NH_S=4): heads [hc*8 .. +8) - items: (hc, rowchunk(512)): ~4 × 33 at 16k = 130+ items; at 2048: 4×5 = 20?! - each item: loop rows in BR=32: load c (32,512) + kr (32,64); compute scores for 8 heads: 8 × tl.sum → (8, 32): write to scores scratchy (chunk, 8, 256-major); track m per head in registers (8,) = max(s over rows) → at end write m (8,) to SC_HMAX[hc*8: +8] (atomic_max? - plain store (each head in one item... wait NO — items span MULTIPLE row-chunks per head!!! (hc, rowchunk) — so each item = one row-chunk for 8 heads → the m per head is per-item-partial → merge partials again?! ARGH — alignment: item = (hc, rowchunk) → chunk maxes partial per head → atomic_max SC_HMAX[head] ✓ (only 32 atomics per item — cheap ✓) Phase A cost: DRAM = 4 × the cache (4 head-groups... each row's tile feeds 8 heads — read 4 times)!! — BETTER: heads ALL 32 per row-tile: DRAM = 1× cache: items = rowchunk only (65 at 16k / 9 at 2048 ← TOO FEW → fill with MORE row-split: chunks of 128 rows → 130 at 16k / 19 at 2048 — still thin at 2k... — item (rowchunk-128 (128 rows) and the item ALSO gets split by c-chunk (4 c-chunks of 128 each)!: item (rowchunk, cchunk) → 65×4 = 260 (16k), 19×4 = 76 (2048) ✓ 76 items of 147 KB ✓ 65×4 each streams rows once for ITS c-chunk: DRAM per c-chunk-column of the cache = 1× total (each (rowchunk, cchunk) reads its own slice = 1× cache total!!) — q_abs per c-chunk: 128-dim — scaled!! each item: BR rows iterations: load c-tile (BR=32, 128) bf16 = 8 KB + kr (32, 64) = 4 KB: compute 32 scores partially (32 dots of 128-dim each: (32,128) block → tl.sum axis1 → 32) acc per head row-chunk... WAIT — the score for head h, row l = Σ_c q_abs[h,c]·c[l,c] + Σ_d q_rope[h,d]·kr[l,d] — with c-chunk-128 decomposition, the q_abs dot is SPLIT over 4 c-chunks → partial scores per chunk-scratch (256…4 partials to be summed before softmax!!) — COMBINE into score scratch (row, head) summed in COMB stage (or COMPUTE the summed scores in an extra reduction (c-chunk partial sum — another split-partial merge!) SCORE layout: (L+1) × 32 f32 = 2 MB at 16k. 4 partial slices per (row, head) → merged by the reduce... This is getting REALLY complicated with partial-sum depth 3. STOP — let me pick a simpler strong upgrade of attention v1: keep the existing online-softmax but process head-groups per item: - item = (head-group of 2 heads, rowchunk-256): item count = 16 × 65 (16k) = 1040... hmm not enough at 2k (16×9 = 144 ✓ fine-ish) - per item: loop rows BR=16: load c (16, 512) + kr dots; compute SCORE for BOTH heads: s0, s1 = 2 dots — share the loaded tiles ✓ 2× reuse! - partials per (2 heads, chunk): m/l/pv per head → 2 stored partials per item (each 514 f32) - effect: 2× fewer per-item streaming → effective stage time ~half (~90 → 40-50?) ... or head-group 4: items = 8 × 65 = 520@16k / 8×9=72@2k (~ 72 thin at 2k ~ 0.4/cta... but per-item streams 590 KB ✓ fine) — 4xl reuse. Let me go head-group=4 + row-chunk 256 → ~520/72 items. Estimated ATT: per item = 256 rows of (16,512)+kr tiles ≈ 16 iters ≈ ~20-40 µs + 3 extra head-dots each (cheap compute) → stays ~ per item ≈ same ~20-40 µs vs items/CTA down 4×: stage ≈ max-per-cta ≈ (520/188)×~10µs ≈ 28 µs at 16k-ish?!?! vs current ~180/400. — go with head-group 4. Additionally process the scores with exp2 instead of exp? same cost. Also — one MUCH SIMPLER HUGE WIN I need to not forget: **the s = dot(q_abs)+dot(kr) uses the FULL qa/qr REDUCTION per row (512+64 dims each): total ops per item = 256 rows × 576 fp32 dots ≈ 147K fp-FMA — with head-group4: 4x → 590K fma / 590 µs?!?! per CTA-iteration time dominated by COMPUTE not loads!! — tiles (16,512) × 4 head-vecs: the compute is matrix-times-vec in fp32 — at 256 threads ~ 590e3/256 = 2300 cycles ≈ 1 µs per BR-tile-iteration × 16 iters ≈ 16 µs of compute per item on 256 threads. Hmm — that's why ATT is slow?! — exp also (16 SIU × 16 iters = fine). Let me do head-group=4 with BR=32: iters = 256/32 = 8 per item: per iter: tile (32,512)=16KB + (32,64)=4KB loads, 4 heads × dots (32,512) + (32,64) × 4 ≈ 2×32×576×4 = 147K fma ≈ 0.6 µs compute + 20 KB stream — item ≈ 8 × ~(compute 0.6 + overhead) ≈ ~8-15 µs → ~3 items/CTA at 16k → ~45 µs — at 2k: 72 items × ~8-15 ≈ 30 µs at ~0.4 items/CTA... ~15 µs-ish stage I can also boost: at 2048, head-groups of 2 → more items (144)... choose HS=4 constant (72 at 2k — meh it might still be ~15 µs). DECISION: implement head-grouped attention (HEADS_PER_ITEM=4, row-chunk 256, BR=32, partials (per head, per chunk): partial (65, 32, 514) same layout as now (with pv per head). Now I'll replace the ATT loop with the head-grouped version. New design per item: - item = (head-group hg of 4 heads, row-chunk of 256 rows). TOTATT = 8 * nc. - Item structure: per row-tile (BR rows): load ct (BR,512), kr (BR,64) once, compute s for all 4 heads (keep 4 partial (m, l, pv) states). - Prefetch pipeline preserved (double-buffer). Per-head state: m_run (4,), l_run (4,), pv (4, 512)?? — register tensors of (4,...) shapes — indexing in Triton: represent as (4, BR-dot results)... m/l as (4,) fp32 tensor scalars; pv as (4, 512)? Triton supports shapes like (4, 512) — 2048 f32 = 8 KB registers at nw8 (256 thr = 8/thread ✓ ok). Per tile iteration row-block (BR,512): - s = tl.sum(ct * qa, axis=1) per head: qa per head (512,) — 4 versions. To compute (4, BR) scores: s_g = tl.sum(ct[None, :, :] * qa[:, None, :], 2): shapes ct (1, BR, 512), qa (4, 1, 512) → (4, BR) via broadcast! Triton broadcasting handles that ✓ (a (4, BR, 512) intermediate = 8192 f32 ≈ 32 regs/thr — ok). Actually even better: process scores as a (4, BR) tensor: apply mask rows → -1e30; m_new = tl.max(s, 1) → (4,); alpha = tl.exp(m_run - m_new) (4,); wj = tl.exp(s - m_new[:, None]) → (4, BR); l_run = l_run*alpha + tl.sum(wj, 1) (4,); pv = pv*alpha[:, None] + tl.sum(wj[:, :, None] * ct[None, :, :, ], axis=1) → wj (4,BR,1) × ct (1,BR,512) → (4,BR,512) sum axis 1 → (4,512) ✓. Store partials per head: (4 separate stores over H8 = static_range(4)): base = SC_ATT + (ch*32 + hg*4 + hi)*ATT_STRIDE. Partials layout: SC_ATT stays (ch * 32 + h) * stride — chunk-partial count per head = nc chunks (each 256). Layout indexed [ch][h] ✓ (same as now ✓ no host changes needed since nc is runtime and layout fits ≤ ACH_MAX=68 chunks ✓). For the singleton new-row: shared across heads (a (4,) score). Also — M-RESCALE ACROSS TILES per head (works within the loop ✓ same online-softmax logic per head). Item count: 8 * nc → at 2048: 8×9=72; at 8192: 8×33=264; at 16384: 8×65=520 ✓ and each item = 256 rows... wait — chunks: rows per item = 256 (row-chunk)... — item processes its chunk only (rows [ch*256, +256)). At 2049 rows: nc = ceil(2049/256) = 9 chunks ✓ 72 items ✓. Bigger change: each row-tile read used by 4 heads → 4x L2 reuse ✓ stream per item = 256×576×2 = 295 KB. Per-item chain iterations = rows/BR: BR=32 → 8 iters ≈ short ~4-8 µs — good chaining. Partials merge in COMB unchanged (reads (c*32 + h) partials ✓ per head over nc chunks ✓). Let me write the replacement for lines 807-868 (the ATT items loop) as new TOTATT = 8*nc version. Handle the zero-rows case (lim == r0: when chunk only holds the singleton): the first preloaded tile masks all → skip loop; singleton acts ✓ same as before. Also keep copy items (unchanged, appended after). One more consideration: the i512a/i64a usage — qa loads per head: qa0..qa3 = 4 loads of (512,) each (2 KB) — 8 KB per item ✓ qr 4× (64) = 512 B ✓ fine. Write the patch.

NO exp/rescale in-loop! reduces to a straight dot-product streaming pass (loads+minimal ops). - Stage B (pv assemble): items (h, 256-row-chunk): read p=exp(s − m*) rows × c tile → build pv per head — another streaming pass. Each pass is latency-dominated with no serial reductions beyond axis=1 dots. The DOWNSIDE: 2 × streaming volume (cache read twice) — but with 31 reuses at L2... the q-grouped tile is read also reused 4x per pass ~ acceptable! Even so per item ~10-20 µs — but really I want to clobber the 985. The pv-assemble ~ likewise. isolation test: run JUST score-phase items for L=16384: 8 hg × 65 chunks = 520 items, per item: read 256 rows × 576×2B = 295 KB + minimal dots. Should be ≤ ~ (153 MB / 500 GB/s ≈ 300 µs naively at GEMV-aggr bandwidth) → hmm. Let me not over-engineer — the measured per-item chain ~ what's the iteration count? With BR=32: 8 iterations per item. Isolation gA-tiles at (32,128) reaches ~130 GB/s at grid=32-64... — that IS the latency model. For attention per item ~ 8 iters ~ 10-20 µs — predicted ~50 µs/stage — measured 985 — conflict by 20x — the big difference: the reduce ops + fp32 tiles (bigger) per iteration. Something like 40+ µs per iteration of exp/rescale/reduce. This halves the per-iteration work IF scores pass is raw-streaming (only dots, no exp, no big-pv rescale). Work: split partially: 1. NEW stage A (SCORE): items (hg 8 × ch): per item read 256 rows, compute 4-head scores (only dots, keep per-head max in ONE acc per head (BR-per-iter max, no reaction to prev iterations!!), store scores (4, 256) to scratch + m-chunk (4,) to partials. per-iteration ops: just two dots + max update... score writes: 256×4×4 B = 4 KB per item — cheap. 2. Hmm wait — but then scores need summation across... never mind partial reduce phase for each row-chunk max... then m* global max via COMB (existing pattern) — need COMB before B (one more sync!) — currently COMB waits ATT — insert order: A (score+chunkmax) → sync → COMB (global max) → sync → B (pv partial) → sync → COMB2/normalize... that adds transitions but each pass is streaming-fast.
