The structural latency model premium: every GEMV stage takes ~(items × per-item-latency / 188) + transition ~ and per-item-latency ist ~10 µs CORRESPONDING to ~10 round-trip iterations. The fused version reduce items by 3x for QKV (768→192) and 2x for GU (864→432) with 2-3x bytes/item — same aggregate — won't fly since the per-item latency is ITERATION-limited, not throughput-limited. The only real attack is to cut iterations per item (deeper splits won't help as we've set 3 — each group iter = (32,BN) tile + 2 sub-eaters of x. The REAL fight now: the transition/tail per stage: ~63-65 µs of RGU where ideal is 42 µs-ish. And ATT 180 at 2048. Next lever candidates: 1. **Transition overhead per stage ~5-10 µs (** measured ~8 µs between REC and QKVG, etc. ~26 hops × ~6 ≈ 150!!). Look at K0.QKVG end_rel 55 → REC 63.5 = 8.5 apart = REC stage+barrier. Each block boundary costs ~4-8. So ~26 × ~5-8 = 130-200 of pure overhead! REDUCE STAGE COUNT. Merges possible right now (removing waits): - QKVG → REC: REC depends on all QKVG — no merge w/o flags... - The reduce steps (OPR-reduce into OPROJ's gemv — mixed but the reduce item can be an in-stage trailing item with an intra wait ✓ ALREADY DONE (no barrier between gemv and reduce beyond intra). - RGU intra-waits are intra (no full barrier). So per-KDA block full barriers: QKVG|REC|OPR|RGU|DOWN = 5 barriers per block. Can't reduce without fuzzy per-head flags. 2. Fuzzy flags for REC per-head: REC(h) needs q/k/v/g raw for head h + beta: with per-head-flag counters (32 per stage): REC(h,ch) items wait on flag[h]... the QKVG items are n-panels NOT head-major: a panel (n0..n0+128) maps to heads [n0/128.. (n0+128)/128) — aligned (panel = exactly one head!!) with BN=128 → REC(h) waits on the head-h q/k/v/g panels + beta → per-flag counters: 4×32=128 flag-slots + beta... REC(h, t): wait(q-flag[h]) — but q k v each have their own panel+flag... 128 flags + bump targets 1 each. Saves pipeline-depth: REC starts for head 0 as soon as its 3 panels done + beta — overlap ~30-50% of stage boundary time. Also fuzzy flags for DOWN-gemv vs GU: down gemv needs h from hpre for all slots → no fuzzy useful. 3. **NCTA=376 retry?** with reg pressure reasons — sized 128+ likely → probably not. 4. **Cut `_route` cost?** ~measure?— it's once per RGU-stage per CTA ~10-20 µs×4 stages?! — have item 0 do it ONCE into RIDX, others read... naaah. 5. **Per-stage `_rstd` cost reduction**: 1-2 µs × ~8 = ~15 µs — moderate. Implement mailbox: a stage's first item computes full ssq (1 global atomic add) → EXCEPT its consumers need it EARLY (before their gemv body, right after the stage wait) — put ssq item as LEADING item of the PREVIOUS stage's tail (its x complete by then? — x for gate/up norm is the OPR-reduce result — computable... the ROUTE stage could compute the moe-norm ssq itself as leading item → ROUT items wait... rather than reduce-per-CTA redundancy: each CTA redundantly sums 2304 — ~9 chunks × latency — could be 2-4 µs per stage-CTA ≈ ~175 stages-entries/step ≈ ~350-700 µs total?!?! WAIT — every CTA of every normed stage (QKVG, RGU, QABS, MRGU, ROUT, GU+fused) computes it per stage-entry: rstd/rstd_m — that's ~6 stages × 188 CTAs × 1-2 µs of redundant serial time per step ≈ 1-2 ms?!?! — NO WAIT — it's once per CTA per stage — the duration is bounded within the stage's ~10-60 µs spans — hidden inside, mostly. The stages' per-CTA budget IS ~30-180 total — the rstd redundancy is small. Skip. So the plan beyond tape-out: (a) fuzzy per-head flags on QKVG→REC to overlap; (b) reduce stage count by making REC MORE PARALLEL... REC is 128 items/188 = fine. (c) The RGU/DOWN deep-dive: GU fused items: 432 items × ~39 KB ≈ 39/3.4 ≈ 11 µs each — again iteration-limited. Honestly, the wall structure is becoming clear: with everything mostly-modelled, attainable remaining wins: (i) between-stage overlap (fuzzy) ~50-100 (ii) barrier overhead trim via fewer waits (volatile-ld+fast-detect ~10-20) (iii) ATT single-pass' ~180@2048 to ~?? — for 2048, attention is 0.18 of 0.85!! ncta sticks at 188 even with KIMI_NCTA=376 (regs). The kernel uses >128 regs/thread → only 1/SM. Can't change that without major register diet. Let me try L2 prefetch — the single biggest untapped hammer: issue `prefetch.global.L2` for my own next-stage tiles while spinning on the current stage. Implementation: - Add a small helper `_prefetch(ptr, nbytes)` via inline asm: `prefetch.global.L2.pf [ptr];` — prefetch granularity: 128B lines: prefetch per line: a tile is (32,BN)=4KB u8 → 32 lines of 128B; issuing 32 prefetches per tile ~ many instructions. We can issue a couple of pages (1KB = 8 lines × ~4 instr). - Where: at each stage entry BEFORE `_wait` (so prefetches overlap the spin + the previous stage's tail): each CTA prefetches its own (next-stage) item's first few tiles. If too slow to cover, prefetch at previous stage entry. Complexity est: knowledge of next stage's bytes requires per-stage decode (same shape switch). Rather than reimplementing per-stage decode, approximate: prefetch into L2 RECURSIVELY the FLAT weight region slice used by the NEXT stage (computed per stage: byte range of upcoming gemv weights ~ 4.7-24 MB region!) — too much ⇒ prefetch only a few first tiles per CTA (~4-16 KB each — the crucial startup launches; deeper tiles stream themselves as usual but by then DRAM queue is warm). Yet maybe a BIGGER opportunity is not prefetch but **stage-time-compressed prefetch: all CTAs prefetch for THE SECOND following stage (there's always a next-heavy GEMV 2 stages ahead... e.g., while running REC (light), prefetch the OPROJ gemv tiles; while running QKVG, prefetch RGU's first items' tiles.** The overlap window is 8-30 µs — enough to stream ~5-20 MB into L2. Actually — WAIT — per-item-latency ≈ ~1 µs/iter — my prefetch idea reduces that to ~0.2-0.3 µs/iter (L2-hit latencies) IF warm. That multiplies per-CTA throughput ~3-5x. per-stage wall at 1/3... projected total ~ 400-500 µs → ~15x geomean. Implement in 2 spots to start (watch effect on the 2 biggest stage classes): Stage-entry prefetch func: `_prefetch_at_lines(ptr, nlines)` emitting `prefetch.global.L2` per 128B line. Decide mechanism carefully: putting many inline asm instructions inside a persistent kernel = code size again. Each prefetch loop = compact. Which CTAs prefetch? To avoid duplicate prefetch traffic from all 188 CTAs on overlapping data, let each CTA prefetch ITS OWN next-stage work-slice (item assignment derived per next stage mapping). Decode per-stage web... simpler approximation: prefetch weights of the CURRENT STAGE's items right after its own wait (the first few tiles of my items) — hits the upcoming GEMV loop in ~microseconds (start its DRAM stream EARLY (before the wait!) — benefitiing the stage's OWN items too). That covers the ramp. Then additional deeper prefetching of the NEXT stage's first items at each stage entry with a light per-stage mapping = complex... FIRST ORDER approximation (low effort, half the benefit): at each GEMV stage entry (before wait), prefetch the tiles of MY OWN first 1-2 items (16-32 tiles → 64-128 lines). Code: for t in range(0, 64): asm prefetch(base + t*128). Actually prefetch instructions: loop of 1-2 iterations with BR-boundary offsets. Hmm, wait, even simpler: since we established that DRAM is what makes latencies long, **prefetch WHOLE weights on a trusted schedule: dedicate a small fraction (like 1/4) of CTAs to CONTINUOUSLY prefetch the FLAT weight buffer start-to-finish.** Too coarse, evicts L2... OK - do simple per-stage-entry own-items prefetch and measure. Sites: KDA QKVG (i own first-2 items), RGU, DOWN, OPR, QABS, MRGU, MDOWN, MLA-QABSGEMV... — add a helper `_prefetch_tile_ptrs` taking (ptr,count) issuing prefetches for ~count lines. Actually — the 'own items' already emerge right after the wait; prefetch is only EARLIER by the wait duration (~2-6 µs = only ~5-20% of stage wall) — the ESTIMATED benefit is much wider when MOST of a stage comes right after the wait... basically == everything!! So prefetch exactly maps to everything valueable: expected ~2-3x stage speed where wait+startup ≈ latency. Implement + measure per-context, then decide whether to deepen (next-stage prefetch), tune counts. Write helper and insert at ~8 stage entries. Careful: prefetch instructions have NO side effects on correctness (fire and forget). Place AFTER its wait — the very first instructions of the stage's real work — masks the item-first-loads ~ so each CTA prefetches ITS OWN first-item tiles (its assignment!) — guaranteed to hit its own immediate work = the app's OWN personal working set! Honestly — better to do RAII: prefetch EXACTLY flat-region offset of THE FIRST TILES OF THE STAGE (region offsets from par) contiguous over the whole stage's flat span, distributed by CTA (pid × 96 lines × 128 B contiguous). +"""Controlled prefetch-value experiment at RGU-like sizes."""
+import time
+import torch
+import triton
+import triton.language as tl
+
+
+@triton.jit
+def _pfq(base, cnt):
+    for t in range(0, cnt):
+        tl.inline_asm_elementwise("prefetch.global.L2 [$0];", "=l,l", [base + t * 128],
+                                  dtype=tl.int64, is_pure=False, pack=1)
+

Achievable next moves: barrier waits are short (~5-8) so the big fish are RGU(220) + QKVG(165) + OPR(120) + ATT(133) — all GEMV-latency-bound at ~130-160 GB/s aggregate. The next-best-thing: the **"dual ILP per CTA"++ from the fused gate+up** didn't cut through! — because two tiles per loop still sequentialize on slowly-returning loads?? — (DG-tokens, Triton's sched per loop iter is iterative not pipelined. Points of low-hanging fruit seen in diag: 1. `_pf_span` helper is dead code (prefetch unused). Remove. 2. Debug-store instrumentation: `if b == 0:` DXN dump + `SC_SHAD` shadow stores in hot path!!! — 4 shadow stores in DOWN-reduce + hpre (72-item × 2 stores × 2304?? those are per-item tiny — hmm — SC_GSUM dump stores are 2×1024 f32 per hpre item — negligible vs 15 MB. Fine leave. 3. **The rstd/rstd_m per-stage redundancy** — every CTA reads full x and reduces — for QKVG: 188 CTAs × 2304 × 5 stages ≈ 2.2 MB L2 extra. ~10 µs total. compute ssq ONCE via a tiny producer count? — full detail earlier — saves maybe ~5-10. 4. THE RGU/GU work items each read x CHUNKS 2-strided per iteration (72 × 32f32 loads/item from L2) + nrm ± embedded norms per iter — that is ~20% of L2 reads per item. Hmm - convert normed x ONCE per stage into a scratch buffer bf16? — xn scratch: one stage item computes x_normed (2304) once (9 items × 256) → gemvs read xn flat (no per-iter norm compute + no norm loads + no rounding per iter!!) — saves per-item-iteration overhead PERIODICALLY (every normed gemv = QKVG q/k/v/g fused, GU gate/up fused, ROUT + QABS's 2 mats!!). GEMV iteration currently: loads xe,xo (2 strided) + ne,no (2 strided) + 2 mults + 2 cvt-to-bf16 + 2 cvt-back per row ≈ ~50% of iteration instructions! PRE-COMPUTED STAGE "norm scratch" (1 LINE of item list) replaces that by ONE flat xe/xo load per iter. — should move per-item-iteration ALU by ~40% LESS and 4 fewer L2-loads per iter. NUMERICALLY IDENTICAL (same computation order, just pre-rounded: every gemv sees EXACT same bf16-rounded normed x ✓✓✓). This is DELICIOUS — do it: replace normed-GEMV X-reads with reads from a precomputed XN scratch. Head flag target = 6 items ✓. - g_proj mash: h-panels too ✓ extend? — My g gemv isn't fused: 6 splits per head — put those flags INTO the same per-head flag: 12 items per head total (6 qkv-fused + 6 g) ✓ target = 12 ✓. - beta: REC needs global beta per head → beta items (9 partial chunks of all heads!!) — needed by ALL REC: keep the full-stage intra for beta: set REC items to wait beta-intra (72... actually beta = 9 items ✓ their own intra-flag... (#1 slot qkv-intra?) — let me just keep including beta via a 2nd wait slot (nb flag: (2*((b)*5+0)))+1 = intra with target=9 ✓ — but that slot is used by xn(b0 b>0) ✓ collision?? reuse ✓ fine (their different times: xn done, beta done — cumulativy count: xn(9)+beta(9) = 18 → beta-wait target should be 18... write target = 18 with cur... — mmm — with intra-slot +1: xn(9) + beta(9) → cumul 18 ✓ beta-stage-initial-wait = 18 ✓ general correctness— inductively launched prior to... — instead I'll give beta its OWN slot: slot (2*((b)*5+0)))+2 : bump 9, wait 9 ✓ clean. - REC items (h,t): wait flag[h] == 12 && beta-slot == 9 — wait BOTH — the flag counters accumulate per step → must be per-launch offsets!! — the flag counters are per-step: add ctr_base offset per block like now (i.e., flags at slot (2*((b)*5+0)))+3+h?? — NO — I'm using slot space per launch per stage… Use a separate FLAG ARRAY part of the same ctr buffer: offsets 100+. Beta items: bump ctr + BETA_FLAG + b with 1 per item (9 → wait 9 ✓). At REC stage start: remove full-stage `_wait` and put per-item waits: ``` for it in range(pid, TOTR): h = it // 4 t = it % 4 ... ``` Each REC ITEM: two scalar spins: ``` c1 = tl.sum(tl.atomic_add(ctr + ctr_base + HQF_BASE + b*32 + h + tl.arange(0,1), 0, sem="acquire", scope="gpu")) while c1 < 12: (poll...) c2 = tl.sum(tl.atomic_add(ctr + ctr_base + BETA_FLAG + b + tl.arange(0,1), 0, ...)) while c2 < 9: ... tl.debug_barrier() ``` These spins are per-item (the flag arrivals can happen in ANY order — independent). The CTA then proceeds to produce S updates. Then REC's stage bump with TOTR ✓ remains (for downstream waits of REC stage). Bump code edit (inside the fused qkv loop): put after its three stores (aq/ak/av)... — hmm since items bump per-head only when they're done computing all three mats ✓ that's endpoint ✓. +"""Kimi-Linear W4A16 hybrid decode — single-launch persistent megakernel.

 update, the MLA latent-cache attention via the absorb identity, the MoE
 router + top-8 + shared expert GEMVs, both RMSNorms per block, residuals, and
 all state/cache updates) is fused into ONE Triton kernel launch per step().

+co-resident grid executes ~20 sequential stages per token.

9.80x geomean, full PASS. Solid. Let's see if there's room for more without destabilizing. The cron watch is well-placed to squeeze a bit more, but everything is quite stable now. Consider a few more careful wins: 1. Split the KDA-REC per-head flag requirement: REC's per-head flag target is 12 (6 qkv-fused + 6 g) ✓ — but actually qkv-fused covers 6 per head and g 6 ✓ = 12 ✓ per head... — I could reduce to 3 groups per split — no. 2. THE LAST BIG THING POSSIBLE: attention at 2048 (133 µs) uses adaptive chunk=64: 33 chunks × 8 groups = 264 items → ~1.4/CTA. At 16384: chunk=256 → 8×65=520 items ~ 296 µs. The attention's remaining per-item stream may shed by dviding attention-partials sub-drive: pv per item is (HPI,) f32 — va the serialization... measured best already. 3. What about the QABS/gemv of MLA's q (6144-N, 3 splits of 6 groups): could fuse more? — with fused (q) maybe doubling ⚡ and the 6144 output vs 2304: also ABS has absorb(512) inside (shared with QABS's switch). 4. Bigger BR in passing for ATT (32) — measured best. 5. Maybe consider num_warps=8 for gemvs vs 4 for attention via separate kernels?? — no single launch. Time budget sanity: official benchmark showed similar numbers at multiple runs (0.744-0.975). Score margin over baseline ~9.8x. Given this, my remaining moves: correctness-watch check across the benchmark contexts is clean. Let me also run check.py multiple times for stability (the harness will run its own additional unseen variations: maybe other seeds/ctx/shapes). Also run benchmark.py once more to confirm benchmarking stability. One more stress-test idea: many-step chains (benchmark does 32/16-step chains ✓ tested via _run ✓ passes... Also — important hardening: TRITON cache fingerprint is computed with `open(__file__).encode()` — fine, deterministic. Reject leftover worry: the `_dbg` buffer for TIMING=0 in production — extends 128 slots — timing instrumentation compiled out via TIMING=0 constexpr ✓ zero-cost. `_SLEEPNS` knob for nanosleep (unused but fine). `tf.import hashlib` — fine. One more coarse gain: set _NCTA_REQ default 188 ✓ keep. Also — the eager debug path (_DEBUG_EAGER) — allowed to remain (only engaged via env). OK — I want to spend the remaining effort in another careful way: current hidden tails — look once more at the diag to see stage costs at 2048 in the FINAL version (fuzzy + XN + chunk-adaptive): spots where a half-hour more effort pays. Let me grab that, then call it.

Interesting: QKVG shows no stamps (the fuzzy bumps don't write min-stamps downward the stage SLOT (fuzzy waits lowered? hmm my stage _bump writes on the same counter — K0.QKVG shows (no stamps) because its wait was removed by the fuzzy change for REC but QKVG's `_wait` (its upstream stage = prev-Down) is the `_wait(ctr, ctr_base + (2*((b-1)*5+(4)))...` line for b>0 — the wait is there?! "no stamps"?? — the wait uses the same pos... — no matter. Current breakdown at 2048 (~750-846): - QKVG visible end_rel 57µs - OPR boundary ~32 (89→121?? K0.OPR 89.70 vs K0.RGU spans 49.25 (end 132)... - RGU spans ~49 (measured ~55→49) — good. - ATT ~130 (523→654??) ✓ adaptive chunk's win - CAO ~16, MOPR ~33, MRGU ~40, MDOWN ~26 Remaining heavier hitters: RGU ~49×4 ≈ 200, QKVG ~55×?, OPR ~30×4, ATT ~130 (2048)/~280 (16k). An even-more-detailed look at RGU (span 49 µs): GU traffic per block = ~15 MB → ~300 µ SB!? — 15 MB at 49 µs ≈ 306 GB/s aggregate!! — excellent (up from ~150!). QKVG (~55 less) — bottleneck is qkv-fused item critical path (12 iters total × ~1 µs?! With XN at hand, RGU's remaining ~49 = gateway+up gemv at ~ 306 GB/s — the latency already near the earlier grid-limited ceiling (isolated gA at grid = 96 → 323 GB/s!!). So per-per-item-latency chains are now the constraint again (432 items × ~36 KB ≈ 15 MB — with ~11 µs per item ~ hmm — per CTA ~ 2.3 items × 11+ transition = 30-40 ≈ consistent!!) — the only deeper attack: shorten gemv chains FURTHER (1-group splits!? — latency = 6 vs 3 per item ~ halves? — with 864 items of 12.5 KB (~1 µs each) ~ honestly not much sub-1-µs benefit: the plan cost remains (2 iterations per group-tile → per group ~1 µs ✓ serial-unavoidable...). WAIT — ONE UNIFIED STRUCTURAL FIX: **DOUBLE N-panels per item**: BN=256 per item covering 2×128 columns = halves iterations... — my current tile (32,BN=128) with 2 kk per group. With BN=256: tile (32,256) u8 = 8 KB per kk → same element count per iteration as (32,128) ×2 ≫ bigger vectorization gains and fewer iterations! per-item time roughly halves?! — traffic-per-item doubles but iters same. per-item critical path: latency = per-tile RT (~1 µs) — iterations SPLIT BY n... try BN=256 as a cheap experiment (single bump-constant change!). Item-counts halve (QKVG fused: 96; GU: 216; OPR: 36; QABSGEMV: fewer) → fewer more-parallel-friendly-chained items... hmm — at 2048 RGU=432 with ~2.3/CTA → at BN=256 216 → ~1.1/CTA — per-item latency doubles (~stream 2x) — measured earlier with BN=128 at per-double workload... — net ~unsure. Just try BN=256. It also affects ALL stage item-total derivations (all count-from-BN formulas are themselves dynamic-ish via NQ, NGCH, NDN, NQQ, NKVA, NOP?! CHECKING impact on the beta-flag split factors and partial region strides... — OPR reduce iterations loop is `NQO = 2304 // BN` (dynamicish, fine); REC unaffected; others unaffected. Partial slot layouts: ((kind * 9 + slot) * 6 + sp) * 1024: fine ✓ all that uses Nc-specific constants... BN only changes n0 = x*BN arithmetic ✓ changes masks internally ✓✓ self-consistent everywhere?! — the split diments: (kind * 6 + sp)*4096 etc fine ✓ Let me also bufsize the attn partial stride: unchanged ✓ Try KIMI_BN=256 (that env knob exists!) — handier than editing: step() passes BN from env ✓ — quick smoketest:

Done. The megakernel is complete and validated.

Final result

~9.8× geomean speedup over the optimized-PyTorch baseline, all correctness gates PASS:

| context | solution | baseline | speedup | |---|---|---|---| | 2048 | 0.746 ms/tok (1341 tok/s) | 7.62 ms/tok | 10.2× | | 8192 | 0.852 ms/tok (1174 tok/s) | 8.29 ms/tok | 9.7× | | 16384 | 0.978 ms/tok (1022 tok/s) | 9.19 ms/tok | 9.4× |

check.py PASSES on every seed (out/S/cache cosine ≥ 0.98, typically 0.999+). python benchmark.py prints peak_fraction: 9.7738, RESULT: OK.

What solution.py contains

The whole per-token forward — all four blocks (3×KDA + MLA), their 4 MoE FFNs, every int4 dequant-GEMV, the short convs, the KDA recurrent state update, MLA attention, router + experts, norms, residuals, and all state/cache updates — as a SINGLE Triton kernel launch (persistent grid of 188 CTAs, ~20 sequential stages, no graphs/compile/per-op loops):

  • Fused int4→bf16 GEMVs everywhere: all weights repacked into one flat region with per-group scales/zeros; unpack + asymmetric group-128 dequant fused inline, streamed exactly once per token.
  • Item-counter stage sync (release/acquire, GPU-scope) with a chain-ordered DAG — provably deadlock-free — plus per-head fuzzy flags so KDA recurrence items start the moment their head's panels land.
  • Split-K = 3-group-deep work items — relied on the key profiling discovery that batch-1 decode on this GPU is dependent-latency-bound (~0.8µs/load), not bandwidth-bound — with fp32 partials summed by consumers before any nonlinearity, preserving reference rounding order.
  • q/k/v and gate/up GEMVs fused for 2–3× load-issue density per latency window; h=silu(g)·u precomputed once per block so the down GEMV streams like a plain GEMV.
  • Precomputed bf16 normed-x scratch instead of per-iteration norm math.
  • Absorbed MLA attention ((W_k^T q_nope)·c_kv + q_rope·k_rope, (Σ p·c_kv) W_v^T) — the growing cache stays compressed latents (~2-18 MB) rather than materialized K/V, via a head-grouped online-softmax flash pass with adaptive chunking by context length.

The final deliverable is solution.py in the workspace; the official harness gates (check.py correctness, benchmark.py score + final correctness re-check) all pass cleanly and deterministically (a self-fingerprinted Triton cache dir guarantees reproducible kernels per source).

Done. The megakernel is complete and validated. ## Final result **~9.8× geomean speedup** over the optimized-PyTorch baseline, all correctness gates PASS: | context | solution | baseline | speedup | |---|---|---|---| | 2048 | 0.746 ms/tok (1341 tok/s) | 7.62 ms/tok | 10.2× | | 8192 | 0.852 ms/tok (1174 tok/s) | 8.29 ms/tok | 9.7× | | 16384 | 0.978 ms/tok (1022 tok/s) | 9.19 ms/tok | 9.4× | `check.py` PASSES on every seed (out/S/cache cosine ≥ 0.98, typically 0.999+). `python benchmark.py` prints `peak_fraction: 9.7738, RESULT: OK`. ## What `solution.py` contains The whole per-token forward — **all four blocks (3×KDA + MLA), their 4 MoE FFNs, every int4 dequant-GEMV, the short convs, the KDA recurrent state update, MLA attention, router + experts, norms, residuals, and all state/cache updates — as a SINGLE Triton kernel launch** (persistent grid of 188 CTAs, ~20 sequential stages, no graphs/compile/per-op loops): - **Fused int4→bf16 GEMVs everywhere**: all weights repacked into one flat region with per-group scales/zeros; unpack + asymmetric group-128 dequant fused inline, streamed exactly once per token. - **Item-counter stage sync** (release/acquire, GPU-scope) with a chain-ordered DAG — provably deadlock-free — plus **per-head fuzzy flags** so KDA recurrence items start the moment their head's panels land. - **Split-K = 3-group-deep work items** — relied on the key profiling discovery that batch-1 decode on this GPU is dependent-latency-bound (~0.8µs/load), not bandwidth-bound — with fp32 partials summed by consumers before any nonlinearity, preserving reference rounding order. - **q/k/v and gate/up GEMVs fused** for 2–3× load-issue density per latency window; **h=silu(g)·u precomputed once per block** so the down GEMV streams like a plain GEMV. - **Precomputed bf16 normed-x scratch** instead of per-iteration norm math. - **Absorbed MLA attention** (`(W_k^T q_nope)·c_kv + q_rope·k_rope`, `(Σ p·c_kv) W_v^T`) — the growing cache stays compressed latents (~2-18 MB) rather than materialized K/V, via a head-grouped online-softmax flash pass with adaptive chunking by context length. The final deliverable is `solution.py` in the workspace; the official harness gates (`check.py` correctness, `benchmark.py` score + final correctness re-check) all pass cleanly and deterministically (a self-fingerprinted Triton cache dir guarantees reproducible kernels per source).
